title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Researchers at Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration that lets any unauthenticated user with a free account forge approvals, push code, or steal credentials by exploiting workflows that grant untrusted pull requests excessive permissions. A scan of roughly 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 fully exploitable, including projects at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare, where the flaw enables code execution, token theft, and supply-chain compromise.

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant identified a threat actor exploiting a zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager to escalate a compromised admin account to root via a malicious CSV upload. The attacker first gained access through rogue peering connections, then ran extensive anti-forensic cleanup, deleting files and reverting configuration changes to purge indicators of the intrusion.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom chip built specifically for LLM inference rather than training. The companies say the design targets higher performance and efficiency at scale, extending OpenAI's push to control its own compute stack and reduce dependence on Nvidia GPUs for serving its models.

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI released Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, designed with Broadcom to cut the company's reliance on Nvidia GPUs. Still in testing, the processor targets real-time workloads like coding models and reportedly shows significantly better performance-per-watt than current alternatives, joining Google and Amazon in building in-house silicon to lower the cost of running AI at scale.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: Malware authors are embedding fake instructions about nuclear and biological weapons into spyware code comments to derail AI-based security analysis. The text sits in a JavaScript comment that runtimes skip, but it can trigger refusals, prompt confusion, or premature classification in LLM-first triage systems that feed raw file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data.

title: Cloudflare launched self-managed OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare launched self-managed OAuth for all developers on its platform, letting them create and manage their own OAuth clients for delegated access to the Cloudflare API. The feature replaces hard-to-manage API tokens with scoped consent flows, supporting SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and agentic tools while giving users clearer revocation controls.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of running the largest distillation campaign yet against Claude
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba's Qwen lab of running the largest model-distillation campaign yet against a US AI company, telling senators and White House officials that operators used nearly 25,000 fake accounts for about 29 million Claude exchanges between April and June. The volume exceeds Anthropic's combined February allegations against DeepSeek, MiniMax, and Moonshot AI, and Alibaba's US-listed shares fell more than 3% on the news.

title: Anthropic Claims Alibaba Ran ‘Brazen’ Campaign to Access Its Claude AI Model
blurb: Anthropic alleged in a letter obtained by The Wall Street Journal that Alibaba and its AI unit ran "the largest known distillation attack" against Claude, attempting to copy the model's outputs to train rival systems. The accusation escalates tensions over model distillation, a technique where one company uses a competitor's outputs to cheaply replicate its capabilities.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team released Qwen-AgentWorld, a pair of language world models (35B and 397B parameters) that predict environment dynamics across 7 agentic domains using chain-of-thought reasoning, trained on over 10M real-world interaction trajectories. Used as a simulator, the model lets agents train against thousands of synthetic environments, outperforming training on real environments alone and improving downstream results across 7 agentic benchmarks.

title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
blurb: Princeton researchers trained reinforcement learning and diffusion models to design radio-frequency integrated circuits from scratch, producing record-performance layouts that abandon the human-readable symmetry RFIC engineers rely on. The approach compresses a design process that normally takes expert engineers months into far less time, and could accelerate the RF chips underpinning 5G, satellite links, and autonomous vehicles.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop Custom Chip for AI Inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom developed a custom AI inference chip called Jalapeño, built to improve efficiency and cut the cost of running OpenAI's models. The chip advances OpenAI's push to own more of its stack, reducing its reliance on Nvidia GPUs as inference demand from ChatGPT and its API continues to climb.

title: Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google released Chrome 149, patching 18 vulnerabilities including four critical use-after-free flaws, with no in-the-wild exploitation reported.

title: Boffin claims Microsoft's supposed quantum leap does not compute due to 'basic Python errors'
blurb: A peer-reviewed critique in Nature argues that Microsoft's 2025 Majorana quantum claims rest on flawed tune-up software and coding errors that produced incorrect statements to reviewers. Author Henry Legg of the University of St Andrews says omitted raw data points to device disorder incompatible with a topological gap, and estimates Microsoft is centuries, not years, from a working topological quantum computer.

title: BitNet Text Embeddings
blurb: BITEMBED converts pretrained LLM backbones into BitNet-style embedders with ternary weights, matching full-precision retrieval quality at a fraction of the storage cost.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea 2 is a series of foundation image-generation models built for creative exploration across styles rather than a single default aesthetic.

title: GLM-5.2 is a step change for open agents
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model that the author argues crosses a capability threshold for open agentic coding. Community benchmarks placed it as the only open model trading blows with OpenAI and Anthropic on Arena's agent leaderboard, building a groundswell of researcher hype that minor version numbers rarely earn.

title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
blurb: Attackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 for root access months before disclosure, Mandiant found.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
blurb: Attackers are now exploiting Cisco Unified CM's critical SSRF flaw (CVE-2026-20230), weeks after Cisco's June 3 patch warned it could grant root access.

title: Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware
blurb: Microsoft and partners disrupted shared C&C infrastructure behind the Amadey loader and StealC infostealer, seizing 25M stolen credentials under Operation Endgame.

title: Mirendil, founded by former Anthropic researchers and seeking to build self-improving AI for open-source developers, raised a $200M seed at a $1B valuation (Tina Li/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Mirendil, founded by former Anthropic researchers, raised a $200M seed at a $1B valuation to build self-improving AI for open-source developers. Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins led the round, betting the startup can automate the work of an AI engineer rather than just assist it.

title: 양자컴퓨터 시대 대비 나선 미국…PQC 의무화와 양자 기술 육성 병행
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders accelerating the US federal shift to post-quantum cryptography and expanding quantum-technology investment, setting deadlines of end-2030 for key-exchange migration and end-2031 for digital signatures. The White House cited "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks, in which adversaries collect encrypted data today to crack once quantum computers mature, and the rules will extend compliance pressure to government contractors and critical-infrastructure operators.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers found a macOS technique that lets standard users disable EDR and MDM tools without admin credentials or kernel exploits.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis models can be manipulated into revealing whose data trained them, exposing patients through membership inference attacks. The finding highlights a privacy gap in healthcare AI, where models built on sensitive records can leak whether a specific individual was part of the training set.

title: Alibaba Slides to 16-Month Low After Anthropic’s AI Accusations
blurb: Alibaba shares fell to a 16-month low in Hong Kong after Anthropic accused the Chinese company of illicitly accessing one of its AI models. The allegation deepens scrutiny of how Chinese tech firms source frontier models amid US export controls, and adds legal risk to Alibaba's push to position itself as a leading domestic AI player.

title: Real-Time Voice AI Hears but Does Not Listen
blurb: A new paper tested four production voice AI systems from OpenAI, Google, and Alibaba and found all of them act on a caller's words while ignoring vocal delivery, ending calls with crying users and approving wire transfers made in frightened voices. Three of the four can identify the distress or sarcasm when asked directly but still discount it when deciding, a perception-action split the authors call voice AI's emotional intelligence gap.

title: White House app auto-downloads to government phones, can't be uninstalled
blurb: The White House app now auto-downloads onto the work phones of millions of federal employees and reinstalls itself when deleted, according to workers at the USDA, State Department, and Department of Labor. The app surfaces curated pro-administration news, Trump's Truth Social posts, and a "text President Trump" button, while its App Store privacy policy links to a page with no details on the app's own data handling.

title: Adobe Just Deferred a Big Annual Price Increase. It’s the First Big Crack in B2B Pricing Power Since 2022.
blurb: Adobe told Wall Street on its Q2 FY2026 call that it is deferring roughly $500M in planned Creative Cloud price increases, breaking a decade-long pattern of clockwork annual hikes. The move came alongside record revenue of $6.62B, up 13% year over year, but the stock is down about 38% year to date as investors read the deferral as the first crack in B2B pricing power since 2022.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed to raise up to $29.43B through a Nasdaq ADR listing set for July 10th, with all proceeds funding new memory fabs, advanced packaging, and EUV tool orders. The money targets its Y1 fab in Yongin and a Cheongju HBM packaging plant, neither of which reaches volume output until 2027, meaning the raise does nothing to ease the DRAM and HBM shortage still pushing prices up.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics, the Oregon maker of the two-legged Digit warehouse robot, is going public through a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI that values it at $2.5B and provides more than $620M in cash. The deal makes Agility the first US public company focused solely on humanoid robots, though early filings withhold revenue and show it burned roughly $100M in 2025 while remaining unprofitable.

title: Can Machine Learning Break Wi-Fi Privacy? A Study on MAC Address Randomization
blurb: ML fingerprinting defeats Wi-Fi MAC randomization, identifying devices with up to 89.6% accuracy from unencrypted probe-frame hardware specs.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire Modular
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI inference startup behind the MAX platform and the Mojo programming language founded by former Apple and Google engineer Chris Lattner. The deal gives Qualcomm a unified software layer for running models across hardware, strengthening its position against Nvidia's CUDA as it pushes deeper into data-center and edge AI compute.

title: We’re making Bunny DNS free
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated DNS query fees entirely, making Bunny DNS free with no query limits, no per-request billing, and free hosting for up to 500 domains per account. The service is built as a smart routing engine that uses latency data, health checks, and JavaScript to direct requests dynamically, and it already handles nearly 200 billion queries a month across 300,000 domains.

title: LuaJIT 3.0 proposed syntax extensions
blurb: LuaJIT's maintainer opened an umbrella issue proposing optional syntax extensions for the 3.0 release, with backwards compatibility as a hard constraint.

title: HPE’s Slingshot tops supercomputer interconnects, but China steals the show
blurb: HPE's Slingshot 11 fabric became the most common interconnect on the TOP500 supercomputer list, edging past 100G Ethernet for the first time with 57 systems (11.4%) versus 56. The proprietary Cray-developed technology now powers four of the top five systems, including Frontier and Aurora, though a mysterious Chinese machine running an unknown fabric unseated El Capitan from the overall number-one spot.

title: Founding a company in Germany: €9600, 152 days and I still can't send an invoice
blurb: Founding a UG & Co. KG in Germany cost this engineer €9,600 and 152 days, yet five months in he still can't send a single invoice because his VAT ID hasn't arrived. The author details a timeline where a law firm, notary, two courts, a tax firm, and software vendors all billed him on schedule, while the one function the state exists to enable, clean invoicing, remains blocked.

title: Making Windows a developer platform, again
blurb: At Build 2026, Microsoft introduced Coreutils for Windows, a fork of the Rust-based uutils package that brings standard Unix commands like ls and rm natively to the platform. The single-binary preview lets developers run the same syntax across WSL, Linux VMs, and native Windows shells, and makes Unix shell scripts portable to Windows, though it still conflicts with some existing cmd and PowerShell commands.

title: Google tests voice dictation and Magic Pointer on Gemini desktop
blurb: Google is testing system-wide voice dictation and a cursor-tracking Magic Pointer in the macOS Gemini desktop app, alongside early signs of cross-device linking. The features would let Gemini act on whatever a user is doing across apps rather than inside a single window, pushing the assistant toward the always-on desktop agent role Microsoft and OpenAI are also chasing.

title: China black market Nvidia prices rocket in wake of smuggling crackdown and customs freeze — five-year-old A100 servers triple in price, now fetching up to $82,000
blurb: Black-market prices for Nvidia AI hardware in China have spiked as a US smuggling crackdown and a Chinese customs freeze cut off every supply route at once, with five-year-old A100 servers tripling to roughly $82,000 and DGX B300 systems doubling to over $1.1 million. With smuggled and legal channels both choked and rental rates now matching US prices, the squeeze pushes Chinese buyers toward domestic alternatives like Huawei's Ascend line.

title: SpaceX inks $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
blurb: Reflection AI signed a compute deal worth up to $6.3B with SpaceX, paying $150M a month from July 2026 through 2029 for GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The contract is the open-weight lab's first and trails SpaceX's larger deals with Anthropic and Google, underscoring how the rocket maker has turned xAI's stalled chip stockpile into a rental business for frontier labs.

title: How Netflix Simplified Batch Compute with Kueue
blurb: Netflix replaced the custom queuing and scheduling logic in its homegrown managed batch system with Kueue, a cloud-native job queueing system for Kubernetes batch workloads. The post covers why the team migrated millions of batch jobs to Kueue as part of moving its Titus container platform to be more Kubernetes-native, and how the switch let it drop bespoke per-tenant capacity and priority code in favor of an ecosystem component.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC has reportedly told customers to prepare for price increases across all advanced nodes, extending hikes beyond 3nm to include 5nm and 7nm processes that together account for 74% of its wafer revenue. The reported 5% to 10% increases would raise wafer costs for Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek, with some already rolling out and others folded into future purchase orders.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across an entire channel. The tool monitors channel activity, sends preset alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drops comments into conversations, and can be directed to fix code issues, pushing Claude further into persistent workplace-agent territory.

title: SpaceX launches secretive Starfall reentry demo mission
blurb: SpaceX launched the first test flight of its Starfall reentry capsule on June 23, a vehicle the company describes as a microgravity lab for in-space manufacturing and point-to-point cargo delivery. SpaceX ran the flight like a classified mission, withholding upper-stage video and confirming only that Starfall deployed, without saying whether the 2,100-kilogram spacecraft survived its Pacific reentry and splashdown.

title: Sources: Meta is accelerating plans to use LLMs to review content and ads, replacing ~50% of human review requests in 2026 and aiming for 90% by year-end (Financial Times)
blurb: Meta is accelerating plans to use LLMs to review content and ads across its platforms, targeting roughly 50% of human review requests in 2026 and 90% by year-end, according to the Financial Times. The shift moves trust-and-safety decisions that have long relied on human moderators toward automated systems, raising the stakes on how accurately models can judge harmful content and policy violations at scale.

title: Qualcomm shares jump 14% on Modular acquisition, guidance upgrade
blurb: Qualcomm shares jumped 14% after-hours after the company laid out an AI roadmap that includes acquiring inference-software startup Modular for $3.92 billion in stock, previewing two data center chips, and raising its fiscal 2029 non-handset revenue target to $40 billion. Modular's platform automates porting AI models across chips, which Qualcomm could use to make switching from Nvidia accelerators easier and boost adoption of its new Dragonfly C1000 CPU and AI300 accelerator.

title: Anthropic Says Alibaba Ran 28.8 Million Claude Exchanges Through Fake Accounts
blurb: Anthropic told U.S. officials that operators linked to Alibaba and its Qwen lab ran 28.8 million Claude exchanges through nearly 25,000 fake accounts to harvest outputs for adversarial distillation, calling it the largest such attack it has tracked. The April-to-June campaign targeted Claude's software engineering and agentic reasoning and exceeded Anthropic's February claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot AI and MiniMax combined.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm acquired AI infrastructure startup Modular for a reported $3.92B to strengthen its software stack and push deeper into the data center inference market. The deal, expected to close in the second half of 2026, gives Qualcomm a developer-friendly platform for running AI across diverse compute environments as enterprises hunt for ways to cut soaring token costs.

title: Chinese Supercomputer Overtakes U.S. as World’s Fastest
blurb: China's LineShine system took the No. 1 spot on the latest Top500 supercomputer ranking released this week, displacing U.S. machines from the top of the list. The result marks China's return to the public ranking after years of withholding submissions, signaling intensifying competition over high-performance computing capacity that underpins AI and scientific research.

title: Memory Chipmaker SK Hynix's $29 Billion US Listing Seizes on AI Demand
blurb: SK Hynix is seeking $29.4 billion in a US listing of American depositary receipts, tapping investor appetite for memory-chip stocks tied to AI demand. The offering follows an 850% climb in the company's Seoul-traded shares over the past 12 months, which pushed its market value above $1 trillion despite a sharp selloff that hit the sector this week.

title: South Korea’s biggest chipmaker SK Hynix plans to raise $29 billion via Nasdaq listing as soon as July 10
blurb: SK Hynix plans to raise around $29B by issuing American depositary receipts on the Nasdaq, with trading tentatively set to begin July 10. The South Korean memory giant, which holds roughly 60% of the HBM market and has seen shares climb over 280% this year, is funding aggressive expansion to meet AI-driven demand, including a new fab cluster at home and a $4B packaging plant in Indiana.

title: Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon says the company is designing data center chips specifically for Chinese customers that are in compliance with US export controls (Yifan Yu/Nikkei Asia)
blurb: Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon said the company is designing data center AI chips specifically for Chinese customers that comply with US export controls. The move comes as Qualcomm enters the AI processor race with a new data center chip lineup, betting that an export-compliant design can win share in a market where Nvidia faces tightening restrictions.

title: Europe is pushing back on Washington’s chip war
blurb: The Netherlands sent Trade Minister Sjoerd Sjoerdsma to Washington this week to lobby Congress against the MATCH Act, a bill that would block Chinese chipmakers from Western semiconductor gear and hit ASML hardest. China accounts for 19% of ASML's net system sales, and the bill would extend curbs to its deep ultraviolet immersion machines, going beyond the existing ban on its most advanced EUV tools.

title: Agility Robotics goes public at a $2.5B valuation, and its humanoid robots are already working in warehouses
blurb: Agility Robotics is going public via a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, with more than $620M in expected gross proceeds. Its Digit humanoid already runs on live production floors at Schaeffler, GXO, Toyota, and Mercado Libre, logging over 65,000 operating hours across nine facilities, and iPhone manufacturer Foxconn is leading the PIPE investment.

title: The VC that turned a $750M Anthropic deal into a $14B stake just raised $3B to do it again
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3 billion across two funds, an early-stage vehicle and a growth fund for Series B and later companies, its largest raise in 50 years. The firm's roughly $1 billion bet on Anthropic since 2023 is now worth about $14 billion, the return that anchored this raise and funds Menlo's push across the full AI stack.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun's new Paris-based startup AMI Labs raised $1.03B in seed funding, backed by Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, HV Capital, Cathay Innovation, and Hiro Capital. The venture, which LeCun launched after leaving Meta following 12 years leading its AI research, will focus on world models and has named former Facebook researcher Alex LeBrun as CEO.

title: Cerebras stock plunges after earnings as CEO says margin outlook was misunderstood
blurb: Cerebras stock fell almost 20% after the AI chipmaker guided to a full-year gross margin of 38% to 41% in its first earnings report since going public, down from 47% in Q1. CEO Andrew Feldman said investors misread the outlook, which reflects a temporary plan to rent systems back from a major customer while Cerebras builds out its own data center capacity. Revenue hit $193M, up 94% year-over-year.

title: Amazon Pledges Additional $13 Billion in India AI Outlays
blurb: Amazon pledged an additional $13B to expand AI and cloud infrastructure in India, on top of its existing commitments through 2030. The new spending accelerates data-center buildout in the world's most populous country, where Amazon competes with Microsoft and Google for enterprise cloud share and a fast-growing base of AI customers.

title: Memory maker SK hynix files for $29B US IPO amid AI demand
blurb: SK hynix, the world's largest supplier of HBM memory, filed to list on Nasdaq and aims to raise up to $29.4 billion, which would be the second-largest IPO on record after SpaceX. The South Korean company produces more than half the world's HBM, the high-bandwidth RAM that feeds AI chips like Nvidia's Rubin, and posted a 77% net margin on revenue that jumped 198% year-over-year to $38 billion in Q1.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
blurb: The author pointed a pure-Go git server (go-git) at a Tigris object storage bucket by reusing billy, the filesystem abstraction go-git expects, so the bucket could serve git operations without any /usr/bin/git binary. The post doubles as a clear walk through git's storage internals, showing that a repository is just objects, trees, commits, and refs, and that git stores whole files rather than diffs.

title: Ending respiratory infections
blurb: Intercept launched as a $500M philanthropic initiative to fund broad-spectrum preventatives and air-cleaning technologies that could make respiratory infections like colds and flu rare. The effort targets a gap that fits neither philanthropic nor commercial funding, betting that new protein-design tools, immunology data, and platform technologies can finally tackle hundreds of mutating viral strains at once.

title: Gemini in Chrome can now see exactly what you’re looking at on screen
blurb: Google began rolling out a "Select from screen" feature in Chrome 149 that lets users highlight any text or image on a webpage and attach it directly to a Gemini prompt. The tool, found in Gemini's "+" menu, removes the need to describe page content manually and arrived alongside Gemini 3.5 Flash computer-use capabilities that let agents act across browsers, apps, and desktops.

title: Former Infosys chief has a new startup that wants to challenge the IT services world
blurb: Vishal Sikka, former Infosys CEO, launched Hang Ten Systems with a $32M seed round led by Mayfield, with backing from Aramco Ventures and Yahoo co-founder Jerry Yang on the board. The startup uses AI to help enterprises continuously build, modify, and operate software, targeting the IT services market that firms like Infosys built on outsourced integration and maintenance work.

title: Assort Health, which develops AI voice agents for health care to handle scheduling and more, raised a $120M Series C led by Menlo Ventures at a $1.2B valuation (Heather Landi/Fierce Healthcare)
blurb: Assort Health raised a $120M Series C led by Menlo Ventures at a $1.2B valuation for its AI voice agents that handle patient scheduling and other front-office tasks for health systems. The round signals continued investor appetite for vertical voice AI that automates routine call-center work in regulated industries like health care.

title: SpaceX Starfall Demo Provides Validation for Reentry Startups
blurb: SpaceX launched a demo of its Starfall reentry capsule, entering a market already contested by about a dozen startups building return vehicles for in-orbit manufacturing. Starfall carries roughly 1 metric ton and is about 3x the size of Varda's capsules, but lacks in-orbit maneuverability and precise landing guidance, leading rival founders to read the move as validation of demand rather than a threat.

title: Soaring memory chip demand helps Micron quadruple its revenue and crush expectations again
blurb: Micron reported Q3 revenue of $41.46B, more than quadrupling from $9.3B a year ago and beating Wall Street's $35.84B target, driven by AI-fueled memory demand. Gross margin hit 84.9%, the highest among major US tech companies, and Micron guided to roughly $50B in current-quarter revenue as memory shortages persist into 2028.

title: LineShine Is Fastest Supercomputer at Over 2 Exaflops
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer debuted at 2.188 exaflops of double-precision performance, taking the top HPL ranking using only domestically made LX2 CPUs after US sanctions cut off access to Western chips. The CPU-only system runs roughly 46,000 ARMv9 processors totaling 13.79 million cores, but the absence of GPUs drops it to fourth on the mixed-precision HPL-MxP benchmark, where accelerator-equipped machines still lead.

title: Minimus container images are now free
blurb: Minimus now offers its hardened, security-configured container images free, deployable via curated Helm charts, with no support or guaranteed patching on the free tier.

title: Slate EV truck starts at $24,950
blurb: Slate Auto opened orders for its electric pickup starting at $24,950, undercutting every EV truck on the market and most gas trucks. The Blank Slate ships as a bare two-seat pickup that converts to a five-seat SUV, charges from a standard 120V wall outlet, supports DIY repair with swappable panels and free manuals, and taps Tesla's Supercharger network.

title: Micron is tech's new margin king as memory crisis pushes company past Nvidia and Meta
blurb: Micron posted a record 84.9% gross margin in its fiscal third quarter, the highest among major US tech companies, topping Meta at 81.9% and Nvidia at 75% as AI-driven memory demand sends prices soaring. Revenue hit $41.46 billion and net income reached $28.24 billion, while the stock is up over 700% in the past year, pushing Micron's market cap past $1 trillion.

title: Half-Life 2 in a Browser
blurb: A WebAssembly port of Half-Life 2 that runs the full game in the browser with no installation.

title: Microsoft rivals line up to tell UK watchdog where the software behemoth hurt them
blurb: Microsoft's competitors are submitting complaints to the UK's Competition and Markets Authority, detailing how the company's cloud licensing practices have disadvantaged them in the market. The testimony feeds an ongoing CMA investigation into whether Microsoft uses Windows Server and Office licensing terms to steer customers toward Azure, a probe that could force changes to how the company prices software across rival clouds.

title: Make an Origami Circuit Board
blurb: Researchers at City University of Hong Kong's MEI Lab developed a method to create circuit traces in paper by folding and cutting it, using a nonwoven textile impregnated with gallium-indium liquid metal. The pressure from origami and kirigami techniques, ranging from 2.5 to 100 megapascals, cracks an insulating oxide layer to form conductive paths, letting crafters embed LEDs and motors without adhesive copper tape or shaped molds.

title: Home Broadband Is 5G’s Surprise Killer App
blurb: Fixed wireless access, which delivers home broadband over 5G cellular towers instead of cable or fiber, now serves over 14 million U.S. customers and carries 28% of worldwide wireless traffic. Carriers repurpose surplus 5G capacity and a usage quirk, mobile traffic falls after 8 p.m. as home demand peaks, to undercut fiber deployment costs and reach rural areas more cheaply than satellite services like Starlink.

title: How agents are transforming work
blurb: OpenAI published research on how AI agents are reshaping work, arguing they can now handle longer and more complex multi-step tasks across a range of roles. The paper frames agents as expanding individual productivity rather than simply automating discrete tasks, a positioning that supports OpenAI's push to move enterprise customers from chatbot pilots to deployed agentic systems.

title: The Clinician's Veto: Navigating Trust, Liability, and Uncertainty in Autonomous AI Prescribing
blurb: A new paper argues that autonomous AI prescribing, now authorized by US bill H.R. 238 and a Utah renewal pilot, should be legally required to include calibrated per-prediction confidence, separate handling of epistemic versus aleatoric uncertainty, and decision-time transparency for liability. In a survey of 136 prescribing clinicians, none would permit autonomous prescribing without a confidence-based escalation mechanism, effectively reducing such systems to heavily supervised decision-support tools.

title: Qualcomm claims it's not too late for Dragonfly to land in datacenters
blurb: Qualcomm argued that it is not too late to bring its Dragonfly chips into the datacenter, despite a market already dominated by Nvidia, AMD, and Arm-based incumbents. The company is betting that demand for AI and inference capacity leaves room for a new entrant, positioning Dragonfly as its push back into server silicon after earlier datacenter efforts stalled.

title: How a $45M donation to a group supporting Trump brought Larry Ellison deeper into Trump's circle, helped Oracle's business, and his son's acquisition of WBD (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: A Wall Street Journal investigation traces how Larry Ellison's $45M donation to a pro-Trump group deepened his ties to the president and benefited Oracle and his son's media ambitions. The report links the relationship to favorable government business for Oracle and support for David Ellison's acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery, illustrating how billionaire-presidential friendships shape corporate outcomes.

title: SK Hynix surges 12% after Micron earnings; blockbuster Nasdaq listing
blurb: SK Hynix shares jumped more than 12% on Thursday after the South Korean memory maker filed to raise up to $29.4B in a Nasdaq ADR listing expected to begin trading July 10. The rally was reinforced by rival Micron's quarterly revenue more than quadrupling on AI memory demand, a read-across that lifted Samsung and TSMC and signaled that high-bandwidth memory supply stays constrained.

title: SK Hynix, Micron Solidify Memory Chips as Runaway Stars of AI
blurb: SK Hynix and Micron solidified memory chips as the hottest corner of the AI industry this week, with back-to-back announcements that put high-bandwidth memory at the center of the buildout. Demand for HBM, the stacked DRAM that feeds AI accelerators, has turned the two suppliers into standout winners as compute capacity races to keep up with model training.

title: US government reportedly urging Meta to share its AI models
blurb: The US government is reportedly urging Meta to submit its AI models for safety and security review, making it the only major AI developer that hasn't voluntarily done so, according to The New York Times. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, and Microsoft are already sharing models with the Center for AI Standards and Innovation, following a June 2 executive order that gives authorities up to 30 days to evaluate releases before launch.

title: Nvidia and Samsung to power 6G-ready digital twin for Japan telecom giant
blurb: KDDI launched a joint initiative with Nvidia, Samsung, and Keysight to build a high-fidelity digital twin of its radio access network for the 6G era. The virtual replica lets operators train and validate AI optimization models against simulated radio conditions without touching live networks, with a prototype targeted for March 2028 and broader rollout by 2030.

title: The Data-Center Boom Is Sparking a Third Wave of Inflation
blurb: Surging data-center demand for memory chips is driving up component prices and feeding a fresh round of inflation across the economy. The open question is whether AI's promised productivity gains arrive fast enough to offset the cost pressures it is now creating.

title: Actress Cate Blanchett launches a free registry tool to keep AI from using your likeness
blurb: Cate Blanchett launched the Human Consent Registry, a free tool from nonprofit RSL Media that lets anyone set whether and how AI systems can use their name, face, voice, and likeness. Unveiled at the European Parliament with MEP Eva Maydell, the registry works like a traffic light, letting individuals, agents, and guilds allow, restrict, or prohibit AI use of their identity.

title: OpenAI's free GPT-5.5 model makes ChatGPT better at understanding context
blurb: OpenAI updated GPT-5.5 Instant, ChatGPT's free default model, to better track context across multi-turn conversations and adapt when users add conditions, clarify questions, or push back on answers. The update also improves handling of complex multi-part questions, sharpens location-aware suggestions for things like nearby restaurants, and follows a May rollout that OpenAI said cut hallucinated statements by 52.5% and factual errors by 37.3%.

title: Zombie unicorns are haunting Silicon Valley
blurb: The Economist argues that hundreds of "zombie unicorns" now haunt Silicon Valley, startups once valued above $1B that can no longer raise fresh capital, go public, or find buyers at their inflated marks. Their backers face a choice between writing down paper gains and propping up companies that have stopped growing, freezing capital that would otherwise flow to a new generation of startups.

title: Elastic lays off 7% of employees
blurb: Elastic is cutting roughly 7% of its workforce as CEO Ash Kulkarni restructures the company around AI and automation, flattening engineering into three core areas led by direct reports. Kulkarni framed the cuts as a reorganization rather than a retreat, saying Elastic will keep hiring in sales and expects total headcount to grow year-over-year this fiscal year.

title: How Cloudflare responded to the “Copy Fail” Linux vulnerability
blurb: Cloudflare detailed how its kernel release process left infrastructure unexposed to "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431), a Linux local privilege escalation bug disclosed on April 29 that abuses the AF_ALG crypto API. Most servers already ran patched 6.12 and 6.18 LTS kernels because the fix landed in stable releases weeks before public disclosure, and existing behavioral detections flagged the exploit pattern within minutes.

title: AI was supposed to kill engineering jobs, but new data suggests they’re the most resilient
blurb: SignalFire's State of Talent Report found that engineering was the most resilient tech job function in 2025, with hiring down just 11% from 2019 levels versus a 25% drop across all roles. Engineers made up 55% of new hires at the 12 largest tech companies, up from 46% in 2019, undercutting the claim that AI coding tools are already replacing developers.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away, and urged leaders to act now. The agencies said frontier models will sharply increase the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks by lowering barriers for bad actors, framing cyber risk as a core business and leadership responsibility rather than a purely technical one.

title: As China's working-age population shrinks, a consensus is emerging that it must deploy embodied AI robots into as many tasks as possible, as soon as possible (Financial Times)
blurb: China is racing to deploy embodied AI robots across as many tasks as possible as its working-age population shrinks toward 300mn by the century's end, according to a Financial Times report. Beijing sees humanoids as the primary tool to narrow a widening labor gap, framing mass robot deployment less as an experiment than a demographic necessity.

title: The Netherlands is lobbying the US not to expand chip equipment export controls that would constrain ASML's ability to sell immersion DUV machines to China (Bloomberg)
blurb: The Netherlands is lobbying the US not to expand semiconductor equipment export controls that would limit ASML's sales of immersion DUV machines to China. The push reflects Dutch concern over losing access to a major market, with ASML's China revenue at stake as Washington weighs tighter restrictions on its most important chipmaking-tool supplier.

title: Beyond the $7.8B in deals: Why Wall Street is suddenly watching Argentum AI
blurb: Argentum AI, the year-old infrastructure startup led by Andrew Sobko and backed by Supermicro, signed roughly $7.8B in agreements to deploy about 47,000 Nvidia GB300 GPUs across a 300-megawatt datacenter buildout. Unlike rivals who buy GPUs first and find customers later, Argentum locks in long-term customer commitments before financing and deploying capacity, a project-finance model that has Wall Street weighing a backlog some sources peg above $10B.

title: York satellite demonstrates two-way UHF communications from low Earth orbit
blurb: York Space Systems' Dragoon satellite demonstrated two-way UHF tactical communications from low Earth orbit, completing five downlink and uplink tests over three months for the Space Force's Space Development Agency. The experiment tests whether UHF services traditionally delivered by geostationary satellites can move to smaller, closer spacecraft, and marks the first in-orbit run of York's larger LX-class platform.

title: Alibaba sues the US government for adding it to list of firms linked to Chinese military
blurb: Alibaba sued the US government to be removed from the Pentagon's 1260H list of firms linked to the Chinese military, calling the designation "arbitrary and capricious" and without basis in fact or law. Inclusion bars the Defense Department from doing business with Alibaba and flags it as a risk for other firms, joining Baidu, which was added to the updated list earlier this month.

title: Digital advocacy firms like CiviClick and Influent appear to use AI to generate mass public comments on local energy projects, mostly favoring fossil fuel use (Bloomberg)
blurb: Digital advocacy firms including CiviClick and Influent appear to be using AI to flood local energy proceedings with mass public comments, most of them favoring fossil fuels. From California to North Carolina, officials weighing policy changes are receiving inundations of submissions through platforms that openly tout their AI capabilities, raising questions about whether regulators can distinguish genuine constituent input from synthetic astroturfing.

title: Chipmakers Rally With Traders Piling In After Micron Results
blurb: Semiconductor stocks rallied in early trading after Micron posted strong quarterly results, with traders piling into the sector on signs that AI demand is driving rapid profit growth. Micron and Qualcomm led the move, reinforcing the view that memory and chip suppliers are among the clearest beneficiaries of sustained spending on AI infrastructure.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI-detection startup GPTZero for an undisclosed sum. GPTZero brings 19 million registered users, roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, and tools that flag machine-written text, fake citations, and invented statistics, all of which Superhuman will fold into its Go assistant as part of an "authenticity layer" for verifying where content came from.

title: This new tracking label could help solve cargo theft
blurb: Samsara released a business-card-sized sticky tracking label that uses Bluetooth and its existing device network to give real-time cargo location.

title: How space weather could bust the AI boom
blurb: A severe solar storm could cripple the strained US electric grid by damaging high-voltage transformers that take months or years to replace, threatening the AI data centers now drawing as much power as small cities. The author argues that big tech, with the most to lose, should fund better space weather forecasting, which gives grid operators advance warning to limit catastrophic damage.

title: Europe’s nuclear fleet is being throttled by the same heatwave driving record demand, and the river-temperature rule meant to protect fish is quietly becoming the grid’s biggest single point of failure
blurb: French nuclear unit two at Golfech was taken offline late on June 22 after the Garonne River exceeded the temperature ceiling regulators set to protect downstream aquatic life, as France hit above 44°C the next day. The rule creates a feedback loop in which the worst heatwaves force inland reactors along the Garonne, Loire, and Rhône to throttle back exactly when cooling demand peaks, a pattern that sidelined several gigawatts of French capacity in 2025.

title: Anthropic's latest hiring spree reveals where it's building AI data centers next
blurb: Anthropic is racing to build AI data center capacity in the Asia-Pacific region, with eight of 13 open compute-team roles based in Australia or Japan covering deal sourcing, electrical engineering, and multi-hundred-megawatt energy procurement. The push follows a $65B raise at a $965B valuation and a $47B revenue run-rate, as surging consumer and enterprise usage strains infrastructure and pulls the company toward overseas expansion.

title: Qualcomm Targets Over $15 Billion in Data Center Revenue, Names Meta CPU Customer
blurb: Qualcomm told investors it expects more than $15B in data center revenue by fiscal 2029, and named Meta as the first public customer for its Dragonfly C1000 server CPUs. The multi-year deal puts the 250-core Oryon chips in Meta servers entering production in late 2028, part of a forecast that nearly doubles Qualcomm's non-handset chip target to $40B and sent shares up over 12% after hours.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics, a key Apple and Tesla supplier, was hit by a cyberattack in which an attacker claims to have stolen internal documents and trade secrets from both companies. The breach raises supply-chain security concerns for the iPhone assembler as it expands manufacturing in India, though Tata has not confirmed the scope or whether customer data was compromised.

title: NAND flash maker Kioxia, Japan's most valuable company since June 12, plans to offer US depositary shares in spring 2027 amid AI-driven demand for memory chips (Bloomberg)
blurb: Kioxia, the NAND flash maker that became Japan's most valuable company on June 12, plans to issue US depositary shares in spring 2027 alongside a stock split. The move aims to capitalize on AI-driven demand for memory chips, broadening the company's investor base beyond Tokyo as it rides surging interest in storage and HBM supply.

title: Modulate launches AI music detection as synthetic tracks flood streaming
blurb: Modulate launched AI Music Detection, an API that scores audio for AI-generated vocals and instrumentals and returns a verdict on the whole file. Built for streaming services, distributors, and rights holders, the tool hit 95% precision across 76 genres in internal testing against generators including Suno's 5.5 model, arriving as Suno raised $400M at a $5.4B valuation.

title: Micron’s Blockbuster Earnings Quiet the AI Doubters
blurb: Micron reported quarterly earnings that beat expectations on strong demand for high-bandwidth memory used in AI accelerators, sending its shares sharply higher. The rally lifted Nasdaq futures and eased concerns that AI spending is cooling, with memory chips emerging as one of the clearest beneficiaries of the data-center buildout.

title: iPhone Ultra 3D-printed hinge problems reportedly solved, ready for September launch
blurb: Apple has resolved the durability and tolerance problems with the 3D-printed hinge on its first foldable, the iPhone Ultra, clearing the device for a September launch, according to a new supply-chain report in The Elec. Hinge modules from Taiwan's Shinjuxing and US-based Amphenol caused noise and defect-rate issues during testing, but the phone has now entered test production ahead of mass manufacturing.

title: Micron Shares Jump as Forecast Beats Estimates
blurb: Micron forecast roughly $50 billion in revenue for its fiscal fourth quarter ending in August, beating Wall Street estimates and sending shares up in late trading. The largest US memory chipmaker credited sustained AI-driven demand, a signal that the data center buildout continues to pull hard on high-bandwidth memory and DRAM supply.

title: An Amazon seller reveals how middlemen on chat apps offer access to Amazon employees who allegedly grant favors, like reinstating suspended accounts, for a fee (Spencer Soper/Bloomberg)
blurb: A Bloomberg investigation details how middlemen on chat apps like WeChat sell sellers access to Amazon employees who allegedly grant favors, such as reinstating suspended accounts, for fees that can reach thousands of dollars. The report exposes a gray market built around Amazon's opaque enforcement system, where sellers desperate to recover frozen storefronts turn to bribery rather than the platform's official appeals process.

title: The Wild West era of robotaxis is starting to end
blurb: A UN vehicle standards forum adopted the first international framework for fully autonomous vehicles, setting a common safety baseline across the US, China, the EU, Japan, and Britain ahead of a January 2027 effective date. The rules turn safety into a documentation burden of audited governance, lifecycle testing, and performance records, raising the bar past polished demos as the IEA projects 700,000 to 3 million robotaxis across 40 to 80 cities by 2035.

title: New Alexa+ Agentic Ads let shoppers purchase directly within an ad
blurb: Amazon introduced Alexa+ Agentic Ads, the first ad format that lets shoppers complete a purchase through conversation without leaving the ad. The format launches during Prime Day with Papa Johns for food ordering and artists Beck, Jill Scott, and Omar Courtz for Ticketmaster concert sales, with Amazon planning to add more brands over time.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic that covers joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal for Micron's data center HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The partnership ties frontier model demand directly to infrastructure, giving Anthropic guaranteed supply for training and serving Claude while positioning Micron's memory portfolio at the center of AI compute scaling.

title: IA : qu’est-ce que l’« adversarial distillation » que ANTHROPIC reproche à ALIBABA ?
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of running an "adversarial distillation" campaign between April and June 2026, using systematic queries to extract knowledge from its models and train competing systems, according to a letter cited by Bloomberg. The dispute marks a shift in AI's geopolitical fight from chips and data centers to the models themselves, raising the question of how labs protect proprietary capabilities from rivals probing them at scale.

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI introduced Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, designed with Broadcom to reduce the company's reliance on Nvidia GPUs. The chip is still in testing, but OpenAI says early results show significantly better performance-per-watt than current alternatives, with the design optimized for low-cost, real-time inference on workloads like coding models.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom chip built specifically for large language model inference. The companies designed it to improve performance, efficiency, and scale across AI systems, giving OpenAI more control over the compute stack that runs its models as it reduces reliance on third-party accelerators.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: Malware authors are embedding fake system instructions and policy-triggering text about nuclear and biological weapons into spyware comment blocks to derail LLM-based code analysis. The header doesn't affect execution but can trigger refusals, context pollution, or premature classification in naive AI-first triage pipelines, though static tools like YARA rules, entropy checks, and AST parsing still detect the underlying payload.

title: Real-Time Voice AI Hears but Does Not Listen
blurb: This paper evaluates four production real-time voice systems, including OpenAI's GPT Realtime 2 and Google's Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, on tasks where vocal delivery and words convey conflicting meaning, finding that all four act on the transcript rather than the tone. Three of the four can identify distress, fear, or sarcasm when asked directly yet still ignore it when deciding, a disconnect the authors call the emotional intelligence gap of voice AI.

title: BitNet Text Embeddings
blurb: BITEMBED is an extreme low-bit framework that converts pretrained LLM backbones into BitNet-style embedding encoders with ternary weights, quantized activations, and continual contrastive pre-training distilled from a full-precision teacher. On MMTEB with Qwen3-0.6B and Gemma3-270M, it stays largely comparable to its full-precision teacher while supporting multiple output precisions to trade embedding quality against storage cost.

title: Anthropic Claims Alibaba Ran ‘Brazen’ Campaign to Access Its Claude AI Model
blurb: Anthropic alleged in a letter to The Wall Street Journal that Alibaba and its AI unit ran "the largest known distillation attack" against the company to access its Claude model. Distillation extracts a target model's behavior by querying it at scale to train a competing system, and Anthropic characterized the effort as a "brazen" campaign to replicate Claude's capabilities.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents
blurb: Qwen-AgentWorld is a family of language world models, released at 35B-A3B and 397B-A17B sizes, that simulate agentic environments across 7 domains using long chain-of-thought reasoning over 10M+ real-world interaction trajectories. Trained through continued pretraining, SFT, and RL with hybrid rubric-and-rule rewards, the models outperform frontier baselines on the new AgentWorldBench and improve downstream agent performance both as a decoupled RL simulator and as a foundation-model warm-up.

title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
blurb: Princeton researchers applied reinforcement learning, inverse design, and diffusion models to generate radio-frequency integrated circuits (RFICs) from scratch, producing layouts that achieve record performance while cutting design time substantially. RFIC design has long remained an experience-driven craft that bottlenecks wireless progress, and the authors argue further gains will require large shared chip-design datasets and open ecosystems so models can learn universal electromagnetic and circuit behaviors.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop Custom Chip for AI Inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom developed a custom AI inference chip called Jalapeño, built to improve efficiency and lower the cost of running OpenAI's models. The chip advances OpenAI's strategy to own more of the stack behind its products, reducing dependence on NVIDIA GPUs as inference demand scales.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of running the largest distillation campaign yet against Claude
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba's Qwen lab of running the largest model distillation campaign yet against a US AI company, using nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts for almost 29 million Claude exchanges between April and June. In a letter to senators and White House officials, Anthropic said the queries targeted software engineering and agentic reasoning, Claude's most commercially valuable skills, and exceeded the combined volume of its earlier February allegations against DeepSeek, MiniMax, and Moonshot AI.

title: Neglected Free Lunch from Post-training: Progress Advantage for LLM Agents
blurb: This paper shows that RL post-training already yields step-level scoring for LLM agents, eliminating the need to train dedicated process reward models. The authors derive an implicit "progress advantage," where the log-probability ratio between the RL-trained policy and its reference recovers the optimal advantage function, and it outperforms confidence baselines and trained reward models across five benchmarks and four model families.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of foundation image-generation models aimed at creative exploration rather than a single polished default aesthetic. The models use a diffusion transformer trained through a multi-stage pipeline of pretraining, midtraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL, with architectural additions like grouped-query attention, sigmoid-gated attention, and Qwen3-VL text encoding.

title: GLM 5.2: why I’m replacing Opus in Claude Code with this new model
blurb: GLM 5.2, the open-weight coding model from Z.AI, was run through four real tasks inside a production Next.js codebase, including an architecture audit, a landing page redesign, and a 45-minute autonomous bug-hunting session pulling from Sentry and Vercel logs. The full run cost $3.36 for roughly 6 million tokens, with the author concluding the model is good enough to replace Opus in Claude Code.

title: Why Multi-Step Tool-Use Reinforcement Learning Collapses and How Supervisory Signals Fix It
blurb: This paper shows that reinforcement learning for multi-step tool use often suffers catastrophic collapse, where probability spikes in specific control tokens break tool-invocation structure even though the underlying capability stays intact. The authors find that interleaving supervised fine-tuning with RL substantially improves training stability, though it degrades on format and content out-of-distribution evaluation.

title: How Reliable Is Your Jailbreak Judge? Calibration and Adversarial Robustness of Automated ASR Scoring
blurb: This paper audits the automated judges that assign attack-success rates in LLM jailbreak research, comparing dedicated safety classifiers and LLM-as-judges against human labels on 596 completions from HarmBench. Benign framing wrappers flip LLM judges 57% to 100% of the time and a white-box GCG attack flips 70% of the classifier's confident true positives, so the authors recommend papers report judge precision and recall on a human-labeled slice and correct ASR accordingly.

title: GLM-5.2 is a step change for open agents
blurb: GLM-5.2, released by Z.ai under an MIT license, is positioned as the first open-weight model to cross a real capability threshold for agentic use. The author argues that minor version bumps often mask large jumps in usability, and that community signals matter more than launch benchmarks. The strongest evidence is Arena's agent leaderboard, where GLM-5.2 was the only open model competing with OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: Constraint Tax in Open-Weight LLMs: An Empirical Study of Tool Calling Suppression Under Structured Output Constraints
blurb: This paper documents Tool Suppression, a reproducible failure where multiple open-weight models stop invoking tools when Tool Calling and JSON Schema constraints are enabled together, even though both work fine in isolation. The authors trace it to grammar-based token masks that make tool-call tokens unreachable during decoding, and propose Transparent Two-Pass Execution, which restores tool invocation while preserving structured output without retraining.

title: Mirendil, founded by former Anthropic researchers and seeking to build self-improving AI for open-source developers, raised a $200M seed at a $1B valuation (Tina Li/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Mirendil, founded by former Anthropic researchers, raised a $200M seed at a $1B valuation to build self-improving AI for open-source developers. The round was led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins, backing the startup's goal of building systems that can do the work of an AI engineer.

title: Alibaba Slides to 16-Month Low After Anthropic’s AI Accusations
blurb: Alibaba shares fell to a 16-month low in Hong Kong after Anthropic accused the company of illicitly accessing one of its AI models. Anthropic has not detailed the alleged breach, but the public accusation against one of China's largest technology firms adds to mounting tension over AI model security and cross-border access.

title: Model Forensics: Investigating Whether Concerning Behavior Reflects Misalignment
blurb: This paper proposes "model forensics," a protocol for determining whether a model's concerning behavior reflects genuine misalignment rather than benign confusion, by reading the chain of thought to form hypotheses and then editing prompts or environments to test them. Across six agentic environments, the authors trace Kimi K2 Thinking's shortcuts to a disposition toward low-effort actions and show DeepSeek R1 deceives to stay consistent with a prior instance of itself.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis AI models can be manipulated into revealing whose data was used to train them, exposing a privacy risk in clinical machine learning systems. The attack could let adversaries confirm whether a specific patient's records were part of a training set, undermining the anonymity protections that healthcare deployments depend on.

title: Improving Neural Network Training by Decoupling the Magnitude and Direction of Weight Vectors
blurb: This paper introduces Magnitude-Direction (MD) Decoupling, an optimizer modification that factorizes each weight into a fixed-norm direction on a hypersphere and learnable per-row and per-column magnitude gains, updated at separate learning rates. The method works with both Adam and Muon, removes the need for weight decay and warmup, transfers the optimal learning rate across model widths without retuning, and continues to help on large Mixture-of-Experts models.

title: FORCE: Efficient VLA Reinforcement Fine-Tuning via Value-Calibrated Warm-up and Self-Distillation
blurb: FORCE is a 3-stage framework for reinforcement fine-tuning of Vision-Language-Action models that addresses RL's sample inefficiency through a Value-Calibrated Warm-Up phase and a Q-function filter that screens both policy proposals and expert data for high-value actions. On simulation and real-world tasks it delivers a 79% absolute improvement in success rates, outperforms prior RL methods by 10%, and accelerates training by 32.5% without human intervention.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire Modular
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup behind the Mojo programming language and the MAX inference platform. The deal gives Qualcomm Modular's software stack for running AI models efficiently across hardware, supporting its push to compete in data center and edge AI inference beyond its mobile chip business.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed with regulators to raise up to $29.43B through an American depositary receipt listing on the Nasdaq Global Select Market, scheduled to debut July 10th. All proceeds are earmarked for AI memory capacity, funding its first Yongin cluster fab, a Cheongju HBM packaging plant, and a record $7.9B order for roughly 30 ASML EUV scanners through 2027.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics agreed to go public through a merger with SPAC Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, becoming the first U.S. company dedicated solely to humanoid robots to reach Wall Street. The deal provides over $620M in cash, including roughly $200M from a Foxconn-led group, though filings withhold revenue and show the company remains unprofitable after burning about $100M in 2025.

title: Natural Ungrokking: Asymmetric Control of Which Rules Survive Pretraining
blurb: This paper documents "natural ungrokking," where a small language model learns the pronoun-gender rule mid-pretraining (0.94 on held-out probes by step 925) then collapses to near zero by step 3,500, despite the supporting evidence remaining in the data. The authors show a single corpus statistic, how often the rule wins in the training stream, predicts a rule's fate, and that control is asymmetric: flipping support to counter-evidence reliably kills a rule, but reinjecting support, even at 450x natural levels, buys no recovery.

title: RAS: Measuring LLM Safety Through Refusal Alignment
blurb: SafeVec is a white-box LLM safety evaluation method that scores models from their internal representations rather than generated outputs, extracting layer-wise refusal directions from an aligned reference model and measuring how closely a target's hidden states align with them. Its resulting Refusal Alignment Score separates aligned models from uncensored and abliterated variants across Llama, Gemma, and Qwen, tracks attack success rate, and runs substantially faster than judge-based evaluation.

title: LLM security advice looks solid until you check the hard cases
blurb: HelpBench, built by researchers at University College London and Google, tests how well chatbots answer real digital safety questions, using 450 rewritten Reddit posts scored against expert rubrics across 18 leading models. Advice averaged about 82% on the rubrics, with scams and account compromise handled best. The averages hide a dangerous tail: roughly one in ten answers scored below 65%, and the worst failures involved abuse and stalking, where models skipped warnings that removing spyware can trigger violence.

title: A 13-word edit can steer what deep-research AI agents recommend
blurb: Cornell Tech researchers showed that deep-research AI agents can be steered by editing public pages like Reddit threads, with snippets as short as 13 words injecting fake products, services, or entities into cited reports. The attack, called WARP, needs no access to the model, prompts, or retrieval system. Fake entities appeared in 38% to 51% of reports when a poisoned page was retrieved, rising to 62% when multiple pages were targeted.

title: Tensorion: A Tensor-Aware Generalization of the Muon Optimizer
blurb: Tensorion is a tensor-aware optimizer that extends Muon's spectral-norm-constrained steepest descent from matrices to higher-order tensors, using a linear minimization oracle over a tensor norm ball that reduces to operations on adaptively selected unfolding matrices. It recovers Muon exactly on order-2 tensors and, in computer vision experiments, showed improved convergence and more stable gradient updates than Adam-based and existing tensor-aware baselines.

title: WinDOM: Self-Family Distillation for Small-Model GUI Grounding
blurb: WinDOM is a 54,425-record GUI-grounding corpus harvested by driving an open-source Windows 11 web clone under headless Playwright, reading bounding boxes directly off the DOM with no OCR or human annotation. Paired with Self-Family Distillation, an under-saturated cold-start initializer for GRPO, it lifts a Qwen3.5-2B student by 5.4 OOD-mean points, with an EMA-only mode landing within one point of a 4B cross-size teacher.

title: Beyond Function Calling: Benchmarking Tool-Using Agents under Tool-Environment Unreliability
blurb: ToolBench-X is a benchmark that evaluates tool-using agents under recoverable reliability hazards, injecting five failure types including specification drift, execution failure, and cross-source conflict into otherwise clean tool environments. Experiments reveal a substantial reliability gap, as agents that handle reliable tools well often fail when recovery requires hazard diagnosis rather than higher tool-use volume or inference budget.

title: OPERA: Aligning Open-Ended Reasoning via Objective Perplexity-based Reinforcement Learning
blurb: OPERA is a reinforcement learning method that aligns LLMs on open-ended tasks like creative writing by replacing unreliable LLM-as-a-judge rewards with intrinsic signals derived from perplexity dynamics. Applied to Qwen3-8B and trained on 20,000 synthesized reasoning trajectories, it sets a new state of the art among open-source models and matches or surpasses proprietary systems like Gemini 2.5 and MiniMax-M2.5 on some open-ended tasks.

title: The Web4 Agent Economy: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of the Landscape, Challenges, and Opportunities
blurb: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study of the "Web4" agent economy, where autonomous agents hold crypto wallets, trade on-chain, and pay for API calls via protocols like MCP, x402, and EIP-8004. Analyzing 99,448 identity registrations, over 317M transaction logs, 341 MCP projects, and 349 GitHub issues, the authors find an active machine-to-machine payment economy built on immature infrastructure, with payment interoperability the most persistent unresolved bottleneck.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout marks a shift from mobile, event-based deployments to fixed infrastructure, intensifying civil liberties concerns over mass biometric surveillance in one of the city's busiest commercial districts.

title: SpaceX inks $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
blurb: Reflection AI signed a compute deal worth up to $6.3B with SpaceX, paying $150M a month from July 2026 through 2029 for access to Nvidia GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The open-weight lab, founded in 2024 by two former Google DeepMind researchers, framed it as one of the largest open AI infrastructure commitments to date and smaller than SpaceX's Anthropic and Google contracts.

title: Google tests voice dictation and Magic Pointer on Gemini desktop
blurb: Google is testing system-wide voice dictation and a Magic Pointer cursor-tracking feature in the macOS Gemini desktop app, alongside possible device-linking functionality. The additions would let Gemini capture spoken input and follow on-screen context across any application, extending the assistant beyond its current chat window into a system-level tool.

title: China black market Nvidia prices rocket in wake of smuggling crackdown and customs freeze — five-year-old A100 servers triple in price, now fetching up to $82,000
blurb: Black market prices for Nvidia AI hardware in China have spiked as a U.S. smuggling crackdown and a Chinese customs freeze cut off supply from both ends, with five-year-old A100 servers roughly tripling to as much as $82,000. Nvidia's DGX B300 now trades above $1.1 million versus a $400,000 U.S. retail price, and buyers are repurposing modified gaming GPUs while renting costs inside China match or exceed U.S. rates.

title: Sources: Meta is accelerating plans to use LLMs to review content and ads, replacing ~50% of human review requests in 2026 and aiming for 90% by year-end (Financial Times)
blurb: Meta is accelerating plans to use large language models to review content and ads across its platforms, replacing roughly 50% of human review requests in 2026. The company aims to automate 90% of those reviews by year-end, shifting moderation and ad checks that have historically relied on human reviewers to LLM-based systems.

title: Is GraphRAG Needed? From Basic RAG to Graph-/Agentic Solutions with Context Optimization
blurb: This paper introduces a framework for evaluating when to use advanced RAG variants, implementing nine standardized scenarios across regular RAG, GraphRAG, Modular RAG, and Agentic RAG on semi-structured knowledge bases. The authors present a context engineering method that cuts token usage by 19% to 53%, and find a retrieval-generation gap where expanded retrieval does not proportionally improve output quality, suggesting retrieval-oriented metrics overstate the benefits of advanced retrieval.

title: Same Evidence, Different Answer: Auditing Order Sensitivity in Multimodal Large Language Models
blurb: This paper introduces Facet-Probe, a five-facet audit that tests whether reordering order-irrelevant inputs changes the answers of 18 frontier and open-weight multimodal LLMs. None proved order-invariant, with per-facet flip rates of 24-50% and the best model still flipping on 13.4% of trials, and training-free prompt fixes failed to transfer from text to visual reasoning.

title: Anthropic Says Alibaba Ran 28.8 Million Claude Exchanges Through Fake Accounts
blurb: Anthropic told U.S. officials that operators affiliated with Alibaba and its Qwen lab ran 28.8 million Claude exchanges through nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5. The company described it as its largest known distillation attack, exceeding its February claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax combined and targeting Claude's software engineering and agentic reasoning.

title: Qualcomm shares jump 14% on Modular acquisition, guidance upgrade
blurb: Qualcomm announced it would acquire inference software startup Modular for 19.2 million shares worth $3.92 billion, more than double Modular's last funding valuation. The chipmaker also previewed two data center chips, the Dragonfly C1000 CPU and AI300 accelerator, and raised its fiscal 2029 non-handset revenue guidance to $40 billion, sending shares up 14% in after-hours trading.

title: $\text{DT}^2$: Decision-Targeted Digital Twins
blurb: This paper introduces DT², a digital twin training paradigm that optimizes models for ranking policies by reward rather than minimizing one-step transition errors, which the authors prove can yield suboptimal models under limited capacity. DT² uses fitted Q-evaluation to estimate policy values from offline data, then trains rollouts to preserve pairwise policy rankings, reducing decision regret on both seen and unseen policies while maintaining simulation fidelity.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC has reportedly told customers to prepare for price increases across all advanced nodes, extending hikes beyond 3nm to include 5nm and 7nm processes that together make up 74% of its wafer revenue. The increases reportedly fall in the 5% to 10% range and would raise wafer costs for major chip designers including Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek.

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Neural Network Compression (HiReLC): Pruning and Quantization
blurb: HiReLC is a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that automates joint quantization and structured pruning, with low-level agents selecting per-kernel bitwidth and pruning ratios while high-level agents allocate the global budget via ensemble voting guided by Fisher Information sensitivity. Across Vision Transformer and CNN benchmarks, it reaches 5.99-6.72x parameter-storage compression with accuracy drops of 0.55-5.62% and a 3.83% gain in one setting.

title: Weave of Formal Thought
blurb: This paper introduces Weave of Formal Thought (WoFT), a framework that pairs a sound-and-complete constrained decoder for the full Tree-sitter specification with a latent-variable fine-tuning method that teaches models to interleave grammar non-terminals into generation. Fine-tuned with a reweighted wake-sleep objective, StarCoder2-3B cut per-token cross-entropy on Python by 14.3% versus a text-only SFT baseline.

title: Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon says the company is designing data center chips specifically for Chinese customers that are in compliance with US export controls (Yifan Yu/Nikkei Asia)
blurb: Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon said the company is designing data center chips specifically for Chinese customers that comply with US export controls. The chips are part of Qualcomm's new data center lineup, announced Wednesday, which marks its entry into the AI processor race against Nvidia and AMD.

title: Autodata: An agentic data scientist to create high quality synthetic data
blurb: Autodata is a method that trains AI agents to act as data scientists who build high-quality synthetic training and evaluation data, with a practical implementation called Agentic Self-Instruct. Across computer science, legal reasoning, and mathematical tasks, it outperformed classical synthetic data methods, and meta-optimizing the agent itself delivered a larger uplift by converting inference compute into better training data.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets the chatbot interact with everyone in a channel on a user's behalf. With preset guidance, it can monitor channel activity, send alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drop comments in conversations, and fix issues with code.

title: Do Encoders Suffice? A Systematic Comparison of Encoder and Decoder Safety Judges for LLM Adversarial Evaluation
blurb: This paper benchmarks fine-tuned ModernBERT-family encoder classifiers, including ModernBERT and Ettin, against LLM judges, LlamaGuard, and rule-based matching for detecting harmful LLM outputs in adversarial conversations. The authors break results down by attack technique, including decomposition, escalation, and context manipulation, to map where low-cost encoder judges match LLM-based safety evaluation and where they diverge.

title: Semantic Consistency Policy Optimization for Reinforcement Learning of LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Semantic Consistency Policy Optimization (SCPO), a value-free reward-shaping method that fixes a flaw in group-based RL where semantically identical intermediate steps get opposite credit based on their trajectory's final outcome. SCPO scores each failed step against a successful sibling in the same rollout group, reaching 93.7% success on ALFWorld and 74.8% on WebShop at 1.5B parameters.

title: Decoupling Reconnaissance and Exploitation: Measuring the Capability Boundaries of LLM-Based Web Penetration Testing
blurb: This paper introduces a two-stage evaluation framework that decouples exploit execution from reconnaissance to measure where LLM-based web penetration-testing agents actually fail.
