Across 50 vulnerabilities, agents reached a 90% functional success rate when given accurate vulnerability context but plateaued near 50% recall during autonomous reconnaissance, mostly from failures parsing unstructured telemetry.

title: TabClean: Reusable LLM-Synthesized Programs for Tabular Data Cleaning
blurb: TabClean is a model-training-free system that compiles LLM reasoning into reusable, guarded Python cleaning programs, so recurring batches run as deterministic code instead of repeated LLM inference. Given a dirty table and a small annotated dev set, it profiles evidence, synthesizes transformations, and validates them with cell-level feedback, improving F1 over rule-based, learning-based, and LLM-based baselines on five of six benchmarks while cutting recurring runtime and API cost.

title: Learning Action Priors for Cross-embodiment Robot Manipulation
blurb: This paper introduces a two-stage training framework for Vision-Language-Action models that pretrains the action module with cross-embodiment motion priors before cross-modal alignment, rather than learning physical motion from scratch alongside it. Across 13 cross-embodiment tasks in simulation and the real world, the method achieves faster convergence, higher success rates, and stronger performance on data-scarce real-world tasks, with larger Stage 1 action data yielding more generalizable priors.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm announced it would acquire infrastructure startup Modular, reportedly for around $3.92B, to strengthen its AI software stack and data center push. The deal targets the inference market and is expected to close in the second half of 2026, positioning Qualcomm to help enterprises run AI more efficiently as token costs climb.

title: Chinese Supercomputer Overtakes U.S. as World’s Fastest
blurb: China's LineShine system took the No. 1 spot on the latest Top500 supercomputer ranking released this week, overtaking U.S. machines as the world's fastest. The result marks a shift in the high-performance computing race that underpins AI training and large-scale scientific work, reversing the lead the U.S. had held atop the list.

title: LibEvoBench: Probing Temporal Knowledge Stratification in Code Generation Models
blurb: LibEvoBench is a multi-task benchmark spanning multiple versions of widely used Python libraries, paired with a Software Evolution Understanding Score (SEUS) that measures how consistently models handle evolving APIs. The authors find that state-of-the-art models are largely version-oblivious, with accuracy degrading on changed APIs and specifying the target version offering no help, while supplying the relevant documentation significantly improves results.

title: Cerebras stock plunges after earnings as CEO says margin outlook was misunderstood
blurb: Cerebras stock fell almost 20% on Wednesday despite better-than-expected Q1 earnings, after the AI chipmaker guided to a full-year gross margin of 38% to 41%, down from 47% in the quarter. CEO Andrew Feldman said investors misread the guidance, which reflects a temporary decision to rent systems back from a large customer while Cerebras builds out its own data center capacity.

title: TL++: Accuracy and Privacy Preserving Traversal Learning for Distributed Intelligent Systems
blurb: TL++ is a traversal-learning framework for distributed training that builds virtual batches across nodes to recover centralized mini-batch gradient behavior, exchanging cut-layer activations and gradients instead of full models. On CIFAR-10 it reached 91.41% accuracy, exceeding the strongest non-TL++ baseline by over 12 points while cutting per-step communication 13.1-fold, with a secure mode that secret-shares activations between two non-colluding servers.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation with Sampled Demonstrations Reduces Output Diversity
blurb: This paper shows that on-policy self-distillation, which trains a model as both teacher and student conditioned on a correct demonstration, achieves strong pass@1 accuracy while quietly collapsing output diversity and flattening pass@k curves. The authors trace this to compounding biases that concentrate probability mass on already-dominant modes, causing the distilled models to match RL on average but fail on out-of-distribution tasks requiring diverse strategies.

title: Amazon Pledges Additional $13 Billion in India AI Outlays
blurb: Amazon committed an additional $13B to its India operations, accelerating the buildout of AI and cloud infrastructure through 2030. The pledge expands Amazon's existing investments in the country, where it is scaling data center capacity to meet rising enterprise demand for AI and AWS services.

title: The Unfireable Safety Kernel: Execution-Time AI Alignment for AI Agents and Other Escapable AI Systems
blurb: This paper argues that placing AI agent controls inside the agent's own runtime, such as system prompts and output filters, is futile because any control in the agent's address space is reachable by the inputs that influence it. The authors present the Unfireable Safety Kernel, a Rust implementation that enforces authorization through process separation and externalized signed evidence, refusing all 704 escape attempts on a self-modifying world model's safety-critical core across 1,000 trials.

title: Variable Bound Tightening for Nash Equilibrium Computation in Multiplayer Imperfect-Information Games
blurb: This paper derives finite bounds on the slack and multiplier variables in the nonlinear complementarity formulation used to compute exact Nash equilibria in multiplayer imperfect-information games. The tighter bounds strengthen the convex relaxations inside Gurobi's spatial branch-and-bound, yielding substantial speedups on exact equilibrium computation in three-player Kuhn poker, a game prior methods could not fully solve within 24 hours.

title: IntentTester: Intent-Driven Multi-agent Framework for Cross-Library Test Migration
blurb: IntentTester is a multi-agent framework that migrates unit tests across libraries and languages by abstracting them into a language-agnostic Test Description Language, aligning them to a repository graph, and synthesizing executable tests through LLM-guided reasoning and iterative validation. Across nine Java and Python projects it produced 2,776 syntactically correct tests at 85% correctness versus 51% and 43% for baselines, and surfaced previously unknown defects including stack overflows and null dereferences.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun's new Paris-based startup AMI Labs raised $1.03B in seed funding from investors including Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, and HV Capital, with Alex LeBrun joining as CEO. The lab, which LeCun announced after leaving Meta in November, will focus on world models, AI systems built to understand and reflect how the physical world works.

title: Memory maker SK hynix files for $29B US IPO amid AI demand
blurb: SK hynix, the world's largest supplier of HBM memory, filed to list on Nasdaq in an offering that could raise up to $29.4 billion, the second-largest US IPO on record after SpaceX. The company produces more than half of the world's HBM, the high-bandwidth memory that surrounds AI chips like Nvidia's Rubin, and posted a 77% net margin on $38 billion in Q1 revenue.

title: Gemini in Chrome can now see exactly what you’re looking at on screen
blurb: Google started rolling out a "Select from screen" feature for Gemini in Chrome that lets users highlight specific text or images on a webpage and send them directly into a Gemini prompt. The capability ships with Chrome 149 from the assistant's "+" menu, replacing manual descriptions of on-screen content with a screenshot-style selection tool.

title: Helpful or Harmful? Evaluating LLM-Assisted Vulnerability Patching via a Human Study
blurb: This paper proposes a controlled human study evaluating whether LLM-assisted vulnerability patching outperforms manual debugging, using a balanced crossover design and hidden "Ghost Tests" that check patch integrity beyond visible functional requirements. The authors measure remediation speed, security validation pass rates, and developer perception to test whether LLM assistance accelerates fixes or produces superficial repairs that pass functionality checks while failing security.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Computer-Use Agents: A Benchmark across Vision-Language Models and GUI Grounding Datasets
blurb: Argus is a cross-regime benchmark for post-hoc uncertainty quantification in single-step GUI grounding, spanning a 27-method open-weight matrix across 4 vision-language agents and 4 datasets plus an 8-method closed-source matrix over 3 frontier vendors. The authors find selective transfer: UQ method rankings stay stable across datasets for a fixed model but degrade across model classes, so closed-source agents should be reranked on the target rather than extrapolated from open-weight results.

title: Scoring AI hackers when there is no answer key
blurb: Irregular released FrontierCyber v1.0, an offensive-cyber benchmark that drops AI models onto real systems like phones, hosted services, databases, and live networks with intact defenses, no planted bugs, and no hints. To handle targets with no answer key, it rates each challenge's difficulty before the run and uses canary strings, planted files, and database markers as tripwires to credit partial progress when a model breaks in but misses the goal.

title: When Certainty Is an Artifact: Keyword Lexicon Blindness and the (Mis)Measurement of Rhetorical Stance
blurb: This paper argues that keyword-based certainty scoring in computational social science can produce findings that are entirely artifacts of the measurement instrument. Analyzing 85 interviews, the authors show a strong negative-affect/emphatic-certainty correlation (r = 0.72-0.93) collapses under LLM zero-shot classification across 32,625 sentences, with Dalio's r dropping from 0.851 to 0.206, exposing how lexicons invert meaning on phrases like "never absolutely totally confident.

title: Tracing Target Answers in Poisoned Retrieval Corpora via Token Influence Attribution
blurb: TRACE is a lightweight framework that detects corpus poisoning attacks in RAG systems by tracing answer-related tokens through token influence attribution, avoiding the overhead of auxiliary classifiers or extra LLM verification. Across three QA benchmarks and six LLMs, it identifies recurrent high-influence keywords, confirms their effect on predictions, and recovers the attacker-specified target answers.

title: BiPACE: Bisimulation-Guided Policy Optimization with Action Counterfactual Estimation for LLM Agents
blurb: BiPACE is a drop-in advantage estimator for training long-horizon LLM agents with stepwise group-based RL, fixing the state-action credit mismatch in methods like GiGPO without adding a critic, auxiliary loss, or extra rollouts. It clusters steps by cosine distance in the actor's hidden-state geometry and recenters returns with action-conditioned peer baselines, raising ALFWorld success on Qwen2.5-7B from 90.8 to 97.1 at 11.3% overhead.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the salaries of the developers who use them, as token consumption from autonomous, long-running tasks climbs. The piece argues that current per-seat and subscription pricing masks the true expense, and that as agents take on more work, those costs will surface and keep rising.

title: Representation Matters: An Empirical Study of Program Representations for LLM Vulnerability Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces RepBench, a benchmark comparing how different program representations affect LLM vulnerability reasoning on real-world C/C++ testcases. Feeding the model an AST plus Program Dependence Graph hit 83.2% accuracy versus 53.5% for raw source code, and graph-only prompts beat source-plus-graph prompts while using far less prompt overhead, revealing a context dilution effect from adding raw code.

title: Evaluating LLMs on Real-World Software Performance Optimization
blurb: SWE-Pro is a repository-level benchmark built from 102 expert-written optimizations in open-source projects, pairing each task with parameterized tests that measure runtime, peak memory, and Time-Weighted Memory Usage under noise-aware conditions. Current LLMs deliver negligible runtime gains and almost no memory improvements, while expert implementations achieve a 15.5x aggregate speedup and 171.3x peak memory reduction across the tasks.

title: SoK: AI Secure Code Generation: Progress, Pitfalls, and Paths Forward
blurb: This SoK introduces a three-level framework that separates models' natural-language understanding of secure coding principles from their code-level actuation of those principles, then measures the gap between the two across models and coding agents on function-level and full web-application benchmarks. Principle understanding strongly predicts security and functional correctness, but large knowledge-actuation gaps persist, as models recognize the right security principles yet still fail to translate them into secure, working code.

title: So You Want to Sell Inference
blurb: This post argues that reselling inference at cost is a zero-margin payment rail, and the only way to hold 30+ points of gross margin is to escape cost-plus pricing. Cost-plus markups compress toward zero as inference commoditizes and customers route to the raw API, while value-based pricing charges for outcomes like resolved tickets or completed tasks and stays decoupled from the inference line. The author notes distillation to proprietary small models is the most defensible way to widen margin.

title: Assort Health, which develops AI voice agents for health care to handle scheduling and more, raised a $120M Series C led by Menlo Ventures at a $1.2B valuation (Heather Landi/Fierce Healthcare)
blurb: Assort Health raised a $120M Series C led by Menlo Ventures at a $1.2B valuation to expand its AI voice agents for health care. The agents handle patient scheduling and other front-office calls, targeting the administrative workload that strains provider staff.

title: Soaring memory chip demand helps Micron quadruple its revenue and crush expectations again
blurb: Micron reported third-quarter revenue of $41.46B, more than quadrupling from $9.3B a year earlier and topping Wall Street's $35.84B target, as AI demand drains memory production capacity. Gross margin climbed to 84.9%, the highest among major US tech companies, and Micron guided to roughly $50B in revenue for the current quarter against the Street's $43.58B forecast.

title: Agentic System as Compressor: Quantifying System Intelligence in Bits
blurb: This paper proposes measuring an agentic system's intelligence by how many bits it saves when reconstructing a target object, formalizing the "compression is intelligence" view under a fixed task distribution, interface, and compute budget. The authors operationalize the metric with arithmetic coding and seed coding across five settings, including chess moves, protein sequences, and retrieval-augmented QA, and find that agentic components reduce codelength in all of them.

title: Is Variational Monte Carlo Robust? Sharp Moment Thresholds and Heavy-tailed Stochastic Optimization
blurb: This paper analyzes when Variational Monte Carlo, the optimization core of neural-network ansätze like FermiNet, breaks down, proving that the nodal geometry of the wave function makes its local energy and gradient estimators heavy-tailed and often lacking higher moments. The authors introduce PS-Clip-VMC, which clips both the energy and gradient variables, prove it converges in the weak moment regime, and show improved robustness when training FermiNet on atoms with up to 18 electrons.

title: Color Matters: Trigger Color Affects Success in Federated Backdoor Attacks
blurb: This paper shows that the color of a semantic backdoor trigger significantly changes attack success in federated learning, even when trigger object, placement, and poisoning budget are held fixed. On a four-class CelebA hair-color task, white triggers worked best against the blond target class and black triggers against the black class, and the effect persisted under robust aggregation.

title: Security and Privacy in Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Architectures, Threats, Defenses, and Future Directions for Building Trustworthy Systems
blurb: This survey examines security and privacy risks introduced by Retrieval-Augmented Generation, where retrieval indices, query logs, and federated updates create exposure beyond conventional LLM threats. The authors present a unified taxonomy of attack surfaces across centralized, on-device, federated, and hybrid RAG, covering membership inference, index inference, poisoning, gradient leakage, and collusion. They review architectural, algorithmic, and cryptographic defenses, along with the privacy-utility trade-offs each entails.

title: CrypFormBench: Benchmarking Formal Analysis Capability of Large Language Models for Cryptographic Schemes
blurb: CrypFormBench is a benchmark for evaluating how well LLMs perform formal security analysis of cryptographic schemes, spanning 700 instances across 677 schemes, 7 formal verifier languages, and 160 security properties. Across 9 state-of-the-art models, most handle interpretation and completion well but struggle with generation, transformation, and correction, with Claude-3.5 topping the field at just 48.7 out of 100.

title: The Clinician's Veto: Navigating Trust, Liability, and Uncertainty in Autonomous AI Prescribing
blurb: This paper argues that US efforts to authorize autonomous AI prescribing, including bill H.R. 238 and Utah's renewal pilot, should require calibrated per-prediction confidence, explicit separation of epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty, and decision-time transparency for liability allocation. A survey of 136 prescribing clinicians found they would reject autonomous prescribing without confidence-based escalation, effectively collapsing "autonomy" into heavily supervised decision support.

title: How agents are transforming work
blurb: OpenAI published a research paper examining how AI agents are reshaping work by handling longer, more complex tasks across a range of roles. The paper documents productivity gains as agents take on multi-step workflows, expanding the scope of what can be automated beyond single, narrow tasks.

title: The Inference-Compute Frontier and a Latency-Efficient Architecture for Limit Order Book Prediction
blurb: This paper tests whether a scaling-law-style inference-compute frontier appears in limit order book prediction, finding that a power-law fit to low- and mid-compute models extrapolates across orders of magnitude to predict the high-compute frontier with R²=0.941. Because the same relationship breaks down in latency space, the authors introduce FastBiNLOB, a hardware-friendly axis-separable LOB mixer that beats published macro-F1 targets at lower latency than existing SOTA architectures.

title: US government reportedly urging Meta to share its AI models
blurb: The US government is reportedly urging Meta to submit its AI models for safety and security evaluation, making it the only major AI developer that hasn't voluntarily turned its models over for review. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, and Microsoft have already agreed to give the Center for AI Standards and Innovation early access, following a June 2 executive order that asks companies for up to 30 days of pre-release review.

title: NSA Loses Anthropic Mythos Access After June Export-Control Order
blurb: The NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 cybersecurity model after the Trump administration's June export-control directive forced Anthropic to suspend its most advanced models for all foreign nationals, including noncitizen employees, The New York Times reported. Officials clarified that earlier claims of Mythos breaking into classified systems in hours referred to a controlled red-team exercise, not an outside breach.

title: [AINews] It's Meta-Harness Summer
blurb: Databricks CTO Matei Zaharia introduced Omnigent, an open-source, pluggable architecture for pulling any coding or knowledge-work agent into a standardized, secure, and scalable system. It joins a wave of "meta-harnesses" including Zed's ACP, Cloudflare's Flue, and Vercel's Eve, all converging on a common pattern as roughly a thousand AI-native shops independently rediscover the same orchestration layer.

title: SK Hynix, Micron Solidify Memory Chips as Runaway Stars of AI
blurb: SK Hynix and Micron made back-to-back announcements this week that cemented memory chips as the hottest segment of the AI hardware market. Surging demand for high-bandwidth memory used in AI accelerators has turned both suppliers into standout performers, outpacing other parts of the chip industry.

title: Coval, which develops simulation and evaluation tech for testing, launching, and monitoring AI voice and chat agents, raised a $28M Series A led by Norwest (Duncan Riley/SiliconANGLE)
blurb: Coval raised a $28M Series A led by Norwest to expand its platform for simulating, evaluating, and monitoring AI voice and chat agents. The startup helps teams stress-test conversational agents before launch and track their behavior in production, addressing the reliability gap that has slowed enterprise voice AI deployments.

title: Can Trustless Agents Be Trusted? An Empirical Study of the ERC-8004 Decentralized AI Agent Ecosystem
blurb: This paper presents the first empirical study of ERC-8004, a permissionless trust layer for AI agent economies built on on-chain Identity, Reputation, and Validation registries, analyzed across Ethereum, BSC, and Base through May 2026. The authors find most registrations are placeholders, reputation can be manipulated at minimal cost, and coordinated Sybil reviewers account for 59% to 91% of feedback, leaving most rated agents with no valid reputation signal once Sybil records are removed.

title: OpenAI's free GPT-5.5 model makes ChatGPT better at understanding context
blurb: OpenAI updated GPT-5.5 Instant, ChatGPT's default model, to better identify the underlying goal of a query and carry context across multiple back-and-forths as users add clarifications or push back. The update also improves handling of complex multi-part questions and location-aware recommendations, building on the May release that cut hallucinated statements by 52.5% and factual errors by 37.3% in testing.

title: Privacy Vulnerabilities of Attention Layers in Tabular Foundation Models and Protection of High-Risk Queries
blurb: This paper shows that tabular foundation models leak membership information through their attention mechanism, since sensitive records passed as in-context examples can be recovered via Membership Inference Attacks. The authors introduce AMIA, a shadow-model-free attack that beats confidence-based methods by 7.7%, and a k-anonymity-inspired defense that cuts leakage by 50% on high-risk queries while degrading predictive performance only 3.9%.

title: When Does Synthetic Data Augmentation Improve Score-Based Imbalanced Classification?
blurb: This paper develops a theoretical framework for when synthetic minority augmentation helps score-based imbalanced classification across metrics like AUROC, AUPRC, and best-threshold F1. The authors show that under well-specified score models augmentation offers no population-level gain beyond finite-sample variance reduction, while under misspecification it can correct ranking errors by shifting the effective class balance.

title: RevengeBench: Reverse Engineering Code-Space Policies from Behavioral Experiments
blurb: RevengeBench is a benchmark of 75 Elo-calibrated, LLM-generated game policies that tests whether a learner can reconstruct an agent's hidden decision program as executable code from behavioral traces alone. Across twelve frontier LLMs, recovery quality ranged from 34% to 72% of initial action-distance closed, and the reconstructed policies gave measurable competitive advantage, especially for weaker models that otherwise struggle to design counter-strategies.

title: A 3D-Printable Dataset for Fair Testing and Comparisons of Tactile Sensors
blurb: This paper introduces a 3D-printable dataset of mathematically defined textures, the first physically reproducible benchmark for fairly comparing tactile sensors across labs.

title: AI translation of literary texts is "fine", but readers still prefer human translations
blurb: A study of literary translation had 15 avid readers compare human translations against an agentic LLM pipeline across 15 recent French, Polish, and Japanese novels. Readers rated machine output "fine" but preferred human versions for clarity and immersion, more clearly at the chunk level (522/772). They could not reliably tell the two apart, and automatic metrics, including LLM-as-a-judge, favored MT and failed to recover reader preferences.

title: AutoSpec: Safety Rule Evolution for LLM Agents via Inductive Logic Programming
blurb: AutoSpec is a framework that automatically evolves expert-designed safety rules for LLM agents using counterexample-guided inductive synthesis paired with inductive logic programming, which prunes the search space by identifying predicates that separate false positives from false negatives. Across 291 execution traces in code execution and embodied agent domains, it raised rule F1 to 0.98 and 0.93, cut false positives by up to 94%, and converged within 4-5 iterations.

title: MiniOpt: Reasoning to Model and Solve General Optimization Problems with Limited Resources
blurb: MiniOpt is a reinforcement learning framework that trains compact LLMs to solve optimization problems by decomposing them into structured modeling and executable solver generation. It uses OptReward, a hierarchical reward that scores both formulation and solution to avoid expert demonstrations, and MiniOpt-3B reaches the highest average solving accuracy among sub-10B models while staying competitive above 10B.

title: An Ex-Meta L8's Agentic Engineering Setup
blurb: A former L8 principal engineer at Meta, Microsoft, and Atlassian who led Atlassian's Rovo Dev walks through his complete agentic engineering setup after leaving big tech to build solo. He describes shifting from writing most code himself to directing a team of agents like an engineering manager, focusing on deciding what to build and judging quality while custom tooling handles the work in between.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: Five Eyes signals agencies for Australia, the US, the UK, New Zealand, and Canada issued a rare joint statement warning that frontier AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away. The agencies said the technology lowers barriers for bad actors and accelerates the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks, urging leaders to treat cyber risk as a core business responsibility rather than a technical issue.

title: As China's working-age population shrinks, a consensus is emerging that it must deploy embodied AI robots into as many tasks as possible, as soon as possible (Financial Times)
blurb: China's shrinking working-age population, set to fall to 300mn by the end of the century, is driving a consensus that the country must deploy embodied AI robots across as many tasks as possible. Beijing is betting on humanoids to narrow the labor gap, prioritizing speed of deployment over waiting for the technology to fully mature.

title: Beyond the $7.8B in deals: Why Wall Street is suddenly watching Argentum AI
blurb: Argentum AI, an infrastructure startup led by Andrew Sobko and backed by Supermicro, signed roughly $7.8B in agreements to deploy about 47,000 NVIDIA GB300 GPUs across a 300-megawatt datacenter buildout. Industry sources suggest the company's contracted backlog may exceed $10B, pointing to a project-finance model that secures long-term customer commitments before deploying infrastructure rather than buying GPUs first.

title: The Netherlands is lobbying the US not to expand chip equipment export controls that would constrain ASML's ability to sell immersion DUV machines to China (Bloomberg)
blurb: The Netherlands is lobbying the US not to expand semiconductor equipment export controls that would limit ASML's ability to sell immersion DUV machines to China. The push reflects Dutch concern over protecting one of its largest exporters as Washington weighs tighter restrictions on chipmaking tools reaching Chinese buyers.

title: Unlocking Model Potentials Through Adaptive Multi-Agent Scaffolding for Efficient Issue Resolution
blurb: icat-agent is a decentralized multi-agent scaffolding that replaces shared context with event-based message passing and uses a rubric to pivot between parallel patching for well-defined issues and preliminary exploration for ambiguous ones. It improves 6.3-18.5% on SWE-bench Pro over baselines including Claude Code while cutting cost by $1.18 per instance, with GPT-5.4-xhigh resolving 67.4% of problems.

title: Digital advocacy firms like CiviClick and Influent appear to use AI to generate mass public comments on local energy projects, mostly favoring fossil fuel use (Bloomberg)
blurb: Digital advocacy firms including CiviClick and Influent appear to be using AI to generate mass public comments on local energy projects, most of them favoring fossil fuel use, according to a Bloomberg investigation. Officials from California to North Carolina weighing policy changes are being flooded with submissions from platforms that openly tout their AI capabilities.

title: Anthropic's latest hiring spree reveals where it's building AI data centers next
blurb: Anthropic is hiring for 13 compute-department roles, eight of them in Australia and Japan, signaling an Asia-Pacific data center buildout to meet soaring demand for its products. The push follows spring US data center deals and a $65B May raise at a $965B valuation, with Australian listings citing "multi-hundred megawatt procurement efforts" across the region.

title: Multi-Agent Goal Recognition with Team- and Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning and Factorized Branch-and-Bound
blurb: MAGR-BB recognizes which multi-agent teams form and their goals from trajectories alone, matching exhaustive search while cutting hypothesis materialization by orders of magnitude.

title: Variational Autoencoder Layer
blurb: This paper introduces a method to embed Variational Autoencoders as a layer inside neural networks rather than using them as standalone models. The authors propose a new training strategy for architectures incorporating these layers and analyze their performance across applications, building on a probabilistic autoencoder family valued for producing smooth, continuous latent spaces.

title: Detect, Unlearn, Restore: Defending Text Summarization Models Against Data Poisoning
blurb: This paper presents a post-hoc defense framework that detects and remediates fine-tuning-stage data poisoning in abstractive summarization models, using influence-function analysis in white-box settings and behavioral auditing of perturbation sensitivity in black-box settings. Across nine architectures and six datasets under adaptive attacks, the defenses reach 85-92% detection precision, while gradient-ascent unlearning restores up to 96% of original behavior with under 0.6% ROUGE degradation.

title: SARA: Unlocking Multilingual Knowledge in Mixture-of-Experts via Semantically Anchored Routing Alignment
blurb: This paper introduces SARA (Semantically Anchored Routing Alignment), a framework that improves low-resource language performance in Mixture-of-Experts models by aligning their routing distributions with high-resource semantic anchors via a symmetric Jensen-Shannon divergence constraint. Unlike logit-based distillation, SARA matches the internal expert-selection of MoE layers directly, beating standard instruction tuning by 0.8% on Qwen3-30B-A3B and 1.2% on Phi-3.5-MoE-instruct on Global-MMLU.

title: CodeChat-Eval: Evaluating Large Language Models in Multi-Turn Code Refinement Dialogues
blurb: CodeChat-Eval is an evaluation framework that builds multi-turn code refinement sessions using a dynamic instruction selection algorithm, testing whether LLMs preserve functional correctness when developers ask for follow-up changes. Across open-weight and proprietary models, functional correctness dropped between 19.2% (GPT-5 Nano) and 69.2% (Llama 3.1 8B) over multiple refinement turns, with logic-level and additive change requests causing the largest declines.

title: AI Coaching for Accelerating Human Skill Development with Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper formalizes AI coaching as a non-cooperative dynamic game in which a learner optimizes task performance while an embodied coach targets the learner's independent competence, using reinforcement learning to combine adaptive shared control with probabilistic models of the coach's influence on skill development. In a user study of 33 people learning first-person-view drone racing, the approach produced significant gains in learning outcomes over state-of-the-art AI coaching baselines.

title: The Problem is Prompt Debt
blurb: The author argues that hand-tuning natural language prompts creates "prompt debt," a slow-accumulating liability that caps what AI applications can become. Plain-English prompts make prototyping effortless but degrade into brittle thickets of edge cases and all-caps threats, slowing iteration, becoming illegible to teammates, and locking systems to a single aging model. Datadog data backs this up: GPT-4o remains the most-used model in observed traffic because hot fixes break when teams switch models.

title: MedGuards: Multi-Agent System for Reliable Medical Error Detection and Correction
blurb: MedGuards is a multi-agent framework that treats medical error detection and correction as an in-context learning task, with separate agents to detect, localize, and correct errors and a confidence-guided arbitration mechanism to resolve disagreements. It requires no additional training and introduces the Keyword-Prioritized Correction Score, which gauges whether critical keywords are reproduced correctly across four multilingual clinical-note datasets.

title: Coval raises $28M as enterprises push voice agents into production
blurb: Coval raised $28M to expand its testing platform for AI voice agents, which runs simulations, tracks live performance, and labels data so teams can evaluate agents before and after launch. Founded in 2024 by former Waymo evaluation engineer Brooke Hopkins, Coval now counts more than 60 customers including Zoom and Deepgram, and says its probabilistic evaluations cut manual QA work by up to 30 times.

title: Analyzing Claude Code usage with CloudWatch and OpenTelemetry
blurb: This post walks through shipping Claude Code telemetry to Amazon CloudWatch using the now-generally-available OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) endpoint, authenticated with a bearer token instead of collectors, sidecars, or IAM credential wiring. The setup yields per-developer cost attribution, team-level usage analytics, and operational alerting on token consumption, all queryable in PromQL, with companion guidance available for OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot.

title: Modulate launches AI music detection as synthetic tracks flood streaming
blurb: Modulate released AI Music Detection, an API that flags AI-generated music directly from the audio by scoring the likelihood that a clip contains AI vocals or AI instrumentals. The system runs three models in ensemble, separating vocal and instrumental analysis to catch hybrid tracks, and reached 95% precision across 76 genres in internal testing against generators including Suno's 5.5 model.

title: When LLM Rationales Become User-Facing: Effects on Trust Perception, Decision-Making, and Gaze Behaviors
blurb: Two studies test whether the step-by-step rationales LLMs now show users actually help people calibrate trust or just persuade through fluent reasoning. Presentation format (instant, delayed, on demand) had no reliable effect; rationale correctness and certainty framing drove trust and decision confidence, and eye-tracking showed incorrect rationales pulled more attention to evidence and raised cognitive effort. The authors argue against "more reasoning is always better," favoring selective, evidence-linked rationales.

title: Agentic infrastructure startup Seltz raises $12.5M to help AI agents search the web for answers
blurb: Seltz raised $12.5M in seed funding led by Speedinvest and B Capital to build search infrastructure optimized for AI agents rather than human users. The startup built its entire stack, including crawlers, index, retrieval models, and ranking systems, to handle long parallel queries that surface structured evidence from tables, images, and page-level content instead of returning lists of links.

title: How Robust is OCR-Reasoning? Evaluating OCR-Reasoning Robustness of Vision-Language Models under Visual Perturbations
blurb: This paper introduces OCR-Robust, a benchmark of 812 samples that evaluates how well vision-language models reason over text in images under controlled visual degradation, using five perturbation types at three severity levels each and metrics like Relative Corruption Retention and a composite Corruption Robustness Index. Testing 18 proprietary, open-source, and OCR+LLM systems, the authors find that higher clean accuracy does not imply stronger robustness, and that charts and tables degrade far more than document-like inputs.

title: Getty Images Announces Display Partnership with OpenAI
blurb: Getty Images announced a partnership with OpenAI to display its licensed editorial and creative imagery within ChatGPT, with attribution and links back to Getty. The deal positions Getty as a sourced visual provider inside the assistant, extending its licensing strategy with AI platforms while giving ChatGPT users access to professionally captioned photography.

title: IA : qu’est-ce que l’« adversarial distillation » que ANTHROPIC reproche à ALIBABA ?
blurb: Anthropic sent a letter, reported by Bloomberg, accusing Alibaba of running an "adversarial distillation" campaign against its models between April and June 2026. The dispute marks a shift in AI's geopolitical contest from semiconductors and data centers toward the extraction of capabilities from the models themselves, with distillation used to train competing systems on a rival's outputs.

title: SpeechEQ: Benchmarking Emotional Intelligence Quotient in Socially Aware Voice Conversational Models
blurb: SpeechEQ is a benchmark for evaluating the emotional intelligence of Speech-Language Models, built on a validated dataset of 2,265 dialogues spanning 15 EQ subscales grounded in EQ-i 2.0 theory and scored with a multi-turn Spoken EQ metric. Experiments show current end-to-end models outperform cascaded systems but remain bottlenecked by a text-reliant modality shortcut, an alignment-induced safety trap, and contextual amnesia.

title: InvestPhilBench: A Multi-Layer Dynamic Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Model Procedural Reasoning in Expert Investment Philosophy
blurb: InvestPhilBench is a benchmark testing whether LLMs can reconstruct and apply expert investors' procedural decision frameworks across eight cognitive tiers.

title: Enhancing Brain MRI Anomaly Detection and Reasoning with ROI Rethink and Synthetic Data
blurb: BrReMark is a brain MRI diagnosis framework that grounds findings in explicit bounding box markings, generating abnormality hypotheses, marking supporting regions, then verifying conclusions by re-examining the marked evidence. Trained with supervised fine-tuning plus reinforcement learning over localization and reasoning rewards, it raises mAP50 from 0.74% to 37.54% over the base model and cuts false positives 45.7% on the NOVA out-of-distribution benchmark.

title: AutoRelAnnotator: Calibrated Model Cascades for Cost-Efficient Relevance Evaluation in Sponsored Search
blurb: This paper introduces AutoRelAnnotator, a calibrated model cascade that routes sponsored-search queries through progressively larger fine-tuned classifiers to produce relevance annotations without human labeling. The authors report that domain-specific fine-tuning adds 20 accuracy points, cascading halves compute cost while staying accuracy-neutral, and per-class isotonic calibration adds 0.6 points, with the system processing 150M+ annotations across six production use cases.

title: How Do Developers Maintain and Evolve Their Agents' Instructions? An Empirical Study
blurb: This paper plans an empirical study of how developers maintain Agent Context Files (ACFs), the structured instructions that guide autonomous coding agents. The authors propose a large-scale mining study that reconstructs ACF evolution at the commit level, classifies changes through a software-maintenance taxonomy, and tests their association with code quality metrics across the development lifecycle.

title: Staying In Character: Perspective-Bounded Memory For Book-Based Role-Playing Agents
blurb: REVERIEMEM is a three-layer memory architecture for novel-based role-playing agents that stores first-person episodic scenes, visibility-tagged facts, and situation-dependent personality patterns to keep characters from citing knowledge outside their perspective. On the authors' 4,386-question KBF-QA benchmark it improves Knowledge Boundary Fidelity by 34.6 percentage points over the strongest prior method and wins about 79% of pairwise narrative comparisons on BOOKWORLD.

title: Knowledge Cascade: Reverse Knowledge Distillation on Nonparametric Multivariate Functional Estimation
blurb: This paper introduces Knowledge Cascade (KCas), a reverse knowledge distillation framework that uses a small, cheap student model to guide construction of a more complex teacher, targeting cases where building the teacher is the bottleneck. Applied to nonparametric multivariate functional estimation, KCas transfers student-selected smoothing parameters to the full-sample regime via asymptotic scaling laws, cutting compute on large, high-dimensional datasets while retaining theoretical guarantees.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic spanning joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal across Micron's data center portfolio, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The two companies will analyze how HBM, DRAM, and SSD subsystems perform across AI workloads to improve energy efficiency and token economics as Anthropic scales compute to train and serve Claude.

title: Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness
blurb: This post defines the agent harness as the orchestration layer between a foundation model and its environments, covering system prompts, tool wrappers, planner-executor loops, retry policies, and context compaction, with an agent being a harness plus a model. The author argues most harness code is temporary scaffolding for the current level of model capability, and teams treating it as a permanent product surface will spend a year ripping it out as next-generation models absorb those functions.

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI released Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, co-designed with Broadcom and partly developed with OpenAI's own models. The play is margin: the chip targets the cost of running real-time models like Codex, where early tests show better performance-per-watt than current alternatives, while pre-training likely stays on Nvidia. It puts OpenAI on the same path as Google and Amazon, owning every layer from chip to product so inference economics stop depending on a single supplier. For startups built on OpenAI's API, cheaper inference upstream is the lever that eventually moves their own unit costs.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop Custom Chip for AI Inference
blurb: OpenAI is co-designing a custom inference chip with Broadcom, called Jalapeño, built to cut the cost and improve the efficiency of running its models. The move pushes OpenAI further into owning the full stack beneath its products, from models down to the silicon they run on. For a company whose biggest constraint is compute economics, controlling the inference hardware is how it stops paying Nvidia's margins and starts setting its own cost curve.

title: Mirendil, founded by former Anthropic researchers and seeking to build self-improving AI for open-source developers, raised a $200M seed at a $1B valuation (Tina Li/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Mirendil, founded by former Anthropic researchers, raised a $200M seed at a $1B valuation from Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins to build self-improving AI that can do the job of an AI engineer. The pitch is automation aimed at open-source developers, with the system improving itself rather than waiting on human tuning. A billion-dollar valuation on a seed round, before any product ships, signals how much capital is now chasing AI that writes and maintains the tools other developers depend on.

title: Adobe Just Deferred a Big Annual Price Increase. It’s the First Big Crack in B2B Pricing Power Since 2022.
blurb: Adobe just deferred roughly $500M ARR of Creative Cloud price increases it had already planned for the back half of FY2026, breaking a decade-long habit of raising prices like clockwork. For four years the B2B playbook was identical: hike every 12 to 18 months, bolt on an AI SKU, push net revenue retention above 110%, and customers paid because they had nowhere to go. When one of software's most pricing-powerful companies voluntarily walks away from a planned hike, the signal isn't generosity. It's the first real crack in B2B pricing power since 2022.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed to raise up to $29.43B through a Nasdaq ADR listing on July 10th, one of the largest such sales ever, with every dollar earmarked for AI memory fabs and EUV scanner orders. None of it eases the current shortage: the Yongin Y1 fab and Cheongju packaging plant don't reach volume output until 2027, and chairman Chey Tae-won expects AI demand to keep supply tight through 2030. SK hynix already holds about 57% of the HBM market and 32% of global DRAM, and it just placed a record $7.9B order with ASML for roughly 30 scanners.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics is taking its Digit humanoid public through a $2.5B SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI, becoming the first US-listed company built solely around humanoid robots. The filings show why the timing matters more than the numbers: Agility doesn't disclose revenue, remains unprofitable, burned roughly $100M in cash last year, and saw operating expenses climb to about $111M from $71M. Its headline $300M in "committed" Digit v5 orders comes with fine print that it isn't current revenue and hinges on a single undisclosed customer. The SPAC reload is funding humanoid bets long before the unit economics are proven.

title: Founding a company in Germany: €9600, 152 days and I still can't send an invoice
blurb: A founder spent more than €9,600 and 152 days incorporating his second company in Germany, and he still can't send a single invoice. The notary, two courts, a law firm, a tax firm, and the software vendors all billed him on time, while the one VAT ID he needs for reverse-charge invoicing to foreign clients is still coming by post. Everyone in the chain can invoice him; he is the only one who can't invoice anyone.

title: Alibaba Slides to 16-Month Low After Anthropic’s AI Accusations
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of "illicitly" accessing one of its AI models, and Alibaba's Hong Kong shares slid to a 16-month low on the news. The dispute pushes the US-China AI rivalry from chip export controls into the models themselves, where access claims are harder to prove and easier to weaponize. For founders, it signals that frontier labs will increasingly police how rivals touch their weights and APIs, and that a single accusation can now move a megacap's valuation before any of it is litigated.

title: B2B brands rank in Google but appear in just 3% of AI Overviews
blurb: B2B brands rank for thousands of keywords in Google but show up in just 3% of the AI Overviews their own queries trigger, according to Walker Sands' benchmark of 828 enterprise companies across 45 million March queries. AI Overviews now appear in half of the searches where these brands rank, so ranking and being cited have split into two separate problems. The gap usually traces back to thin topical authority, unstructured content, and pages that never directly answer the buyer's question, which means the SEO playbook that earned the ranking no longer earns the citation.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of running the largest distillation campaign yet against Claude
blurb: Anthropic told senators and White House officials that operators linked to Alibaba's Qwen lab ran nearly 29 million Claude exchanges across about 25,000 fake accounts between April and June, the largest distillation campaign yet against a US lab. The queries targeted software engineering and agentic reasoning, Claude's most commercially valuable skills, and the volume alone exceeded Anthropic's earlier February allegations against DeepSeek, MiniMax, and Moonshot combined. The frontier labs are treating distillation as a national security problem, and if your moat is model quality, your competitor may be training on your outputs faster than you can ship them.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire Modular
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup building a unified software stack for running models across different chips. The deal pushes Qualcomm beyond its mobile and Snapdragon roots and into the inference software layer, where the real fight against Nvidia's CUDA lock-in is being waged. For founders, it is another sign that the hardware giants now see the portability layer between models and silicon as the part of the stack worth owning outright.

title: Cerebras stock plunges after earnings as CEO says margin outlook was misunderstood
blurb: Cerebras shares fell almost 20% in its first earnings report as a public company, even after beating estimates with $193M in revenue, up 94% year-over-year. The drop came from guidance: full-year gross margin of 38% to 41%, down from 47% last quarter, because Cerebras will temporarily rent its own systems back from a large customer to free up capacity while it builds out data centers. The lesson for founders going public is that the market prices the forward number, not the beat, and a margin story you can't explain in one line gets punished.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to buy AI infrastructure startup Modular for a reported $3.92B to strengthen its software stack and push into the data center inference market. The buy is a bet on horizontal, developer-friendly platforms that run AI across different compute, positioning Qualcomm against Nvidia's grip on inference. The timing tracks a real operator pain point: enterprises are scrambling to cut AI spend as token costs eat into budgets, and the labs and chipmakers are racing to own the layer that makes inference cheaper to run.

title: Europe is pushing back on Washington’s chip war
blurb: The Netherlands sent its trade minister to Washington this week to lobby Congress against the MATCH Act, a bill that would block Chinese chipmakers from buying Western semiconductor equipment and hit ASML hardest. China accounts for 19% of ASML's net system sales, and the bill would extend curbs to its deep ultraviolet immersion machines on top of the existing ban on its most advanced EUV tools. The move signals that US chip controls now carry real costs for allied economies, and Europe's most valuable company is no longer willing to absorb them quietly.

title: Memory Chipmaker SK Hynix's $29 Billion US Listing Seizes on AI Demand
blurb: SK Hynix is seeking $29.4 billion in a US listing, tapping investor appetite for memory-chip stocks even after a selloff rattled the group this week. The offering follows an 850% run in the Seoul-traded stock over the past 12 months, pushing the company's market value above $1 trillion. Memory has become the leveraged play on AI buildout, but sentiment swings in both directions sharply, and a listing this size signals the company wants to lock in dollar capital while the demand premium holds.

title: South Korea’s biggest chipmaker SK Hynix plans to raise $29 billion via Nasdaq listing as soon as July 10
blurb: SK Hynix, now South Korea's most valuable company, plans to raise around $29 billion on the Nasdaq via American depositary receipts, with trading tentatively starting July 10. The chipmaker holds roughly 60% of the high-bandwidth memory market that AI systems are starving for, and its shares have soared over 280% this year to push its market cap past $1 trillion. The listing plants it in the US just as it builds a $4 billion packaging plant in Indiana and a massive new fab cluster at home, putting the HBM supply that every AI buildout depends on closer to American capital.

title: Amazon Pledges Additional $13 Billion in India AI Outlays
blurb: Amazon committed an additional $13B to India through 2030, pushing its total planned investment in the country higher and accelerating its AI and cloud buildout there. The money targets data centers and compute capacity in the world's most populous market, where Amazon already runs retail, Prime Video, and AWS. The bet is that India becomes a primary front in the infrastructure race, and the hyperscalers are now spending to lock in capacity before demand fully arrives.

title: Agility Robotics goes public at a $2.5B valuation, and its humanoid robots are already working in warehouses
blurb: Agility Robotics is going public via a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, raising more than $620M in gross proceeds. What separates it from the rest of the humanoid field is a commercial track record: its Digit robots already run on live production floors at Schaeffler, GXO, Toyota, and Mercado Libre, logging more than 65,000 operating hours across nine customer facilities. Foxconn, the iPhone manufacturer, is leading the PIPE, a signal that humanoid robotics is moving from research demos into a scale-manufacturing phase.

title: The VC that turned a $750M Anthropic deal into a $14B stake just raised $3B to do it again
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3 billion across two AI funds, its largest haul in 50 years, on the strength of an Anthropic stake now worth roughly $14 billion off about $1 billion invested. The firm backed Anthropic in 2023 when it was pre-product and pre-revenue, then led its $750M Series D through an unconventional special-purpose vehicle. One foundation-model bet now bankrolls a full-stack AI portfolio spanning Lovable, Suno, and Skild AI, which is the playbook every AI-era fund is now trying to copy.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun raised $1.03B in seed funding for AMI Labs, the Paris startup he founded after leaving Meta, with backing from Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, Cathay Innovation, HV Capital, and Hiro Capital. The bet is on world models, AI systems that learn how the physical world actually works rather than predicting the next token, and LeCun is staffing it across Paris, New York, Montreal, and Singapore. It is one of the few frontier labs positioned as neither Chinese nor American, with former Facebook researcher Alex LeBrun installed as CEO and a team that treats this as a long-horizon science project, not a product race.

title: Kalshi in talks to raise funds at $40 billion valuation: FT
blurb: Kalshi is in talks to raise at a $40B valuation, up from $5B last October, an eightfold jump in about eight months. The prediction-market platform has ridden a surge in event-contract trading on elections, sports, and macro outcomes that regulators spent years trying to keep off-limits. The pace tells you how fast capital is repricing prediction markets from legal gray area to mainstream financial infrastructure.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the salaries of the developers running them, as token-heavy autonomous workflows rack up usage faster than any per-seat license ever did. A single Codex-style agent left running can burn through compute continuously, and recent reports show clumsy logging alone quietly writing data and costing millions. The implication is that the old per-developer pricing math breaks down, and the constraint on engineering output shifts from headcount to how tightly you govern agent spend.

title: Micron stock jumps 15% as soaring prices from memory crunch lead to quadrupling of revenue
blurb: Micron's fiscal Q3 revenue more than quadrupled to $41.46B from $9.3B a year earlier, and it guided to roughly $50B next quarter as AI demand keeps memory in short supply. Gross margin hit 84.9%, up from 39% a year ago, and the stock is up about 700% over the past year, pushing the market cap past $1 trillion. The CEO expects shortages to persist into 2028, and Micron has locked in 16 long-term agreements worth $22 billion that put half or more of revenue under multi-year contracts.

title: Ending respiratory infections
blurb: Intercept is a $500M philanthropic initiative betting that respiratory infections like colds and flu can be eliminated, the way clean water and pharma made cholera and typhoid rare a century ago. The bet rests on new platform technologies, immunology advances, and protein design tools funding two product types: broad-spectrum preventatives and air cleaning. The wager is that the gap was never the science but the funding, since broad-spectrum work fits neither philanthropic nor commercial models.

title: Assort Health, which develops AI voice agents for health care to handle scheduling and more, raised a $120M Series C led by Menlo Ventures at a $1.2B valuation (Heather Landi/Fierce Healthcare)
blurb: Assort Health raised a $120M Series C led by Menlo Ventures at a $1.2B valuation, putting a new unicorn on AI voice agents that handle scheduling and other front-desk work for health systems. The bet is that the highest-volume, lowest-glamour calls — booking, rescheduling, intake — are where voice AI clears real cost before it touches anything clinical. With Menlo writing the check, expect more capital chasing the back-office layer of healthcare rather than diagnosis.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
blurb: PayPal pointed an Agentforce SDR agent at the 8,000 merchants a month its 200-rep org was never going to call, the ones who stalled partway through onboarding, and meeting conversion is now running about 50% higher than reps managed alone. The agent runs a 10-nudge cadence on every lead and books the meeting, then hands the rep a qualified prospect with full context loaded. The lesson for operators is to stop waiting on clean CRM data and aim agents at the leads you already drop, where anything beats zero coverage.

title: Former Infosys chief has a new startup that wants to challenge the IT services world
blurb: Vishal Sikka, the former Infosys CEO, raised a $32M seed led by Mayfield for Hang Ten Systems, a startup betting AI can replace much of the IT services work his old industry sells. The bet targets the outsourcing model where firms like Infosys made billions customizing, integrating, and maintaining enterprise software. Hang Ten already has paying customers including Siemens Gamesa and Fresenius, and its arrival sharpens the open question of whether AI grows IT services' addressable market or guts how enterprise software gets built and maintained.

title: SpaceX Starfall Demo Provides Validation for Reentry Startups
blurb: SpaceX launched a demo of its Starfall reentry capsule, signaling that an industry giant now sees money in returning cargo from orbit. Founders at the dozen-plus reentry startups already in the market read the move as validation, not a threat: when SpaceX enters a space, it confirms the demand signal is real. The specs back that reading, since Starfall carries about 1 metric ton and runs 3x the size of Varda's capsules but can't maneuver in orbit or guide itself to a precise landing, leaving room for rivals to own the niches it doesn't.

title: ChatGPT recommendations drive more brand website visits: Study
blurb: A Similarweb study found that users who saw a brand recommended by ChatGPT were 2.5 times more likely to visit that brand's site within a week than a direct competitor's, across finance, travel, and beauty. The traffic mostly hides in your analytics as search, not AI referrals, with 55.9% of AI-influenced visits arriving through search versus 40.4% for everyone else. Those visitors also convert harder once they land, viewing 12 pages and spending 11.8 minutes on site against 6.5 pages and 5.6 minutes for the rest, because they showed up already narrowed down.

title: Slate EV truck starts at $24,950
blurb: Slate's electric pickup starts at $24,950, undercutting every EV truck on the market with a stripped-down base model that ships as a two-seat truck and converts to a five-seat SUV. The whole pitch is owner-serviceable hardware: swappable panels, accessible parts, free manuals, and charging off a standard wall outlet. The bet is that a cheap, hackable, accessory-driven platform beats spec-loaded competitors, and the 200-plus add-on catalog turns one vehicle into a configurable product line.

title: Micron is tech's new margin king as memory crisis pushes company past Nvidia and Meta
blurb: Micron posted an 84.9% gross margin in fiscal Q3, the highest of any major US tech company, topping Meta at 81.9% and Nvidia at 75%. Revenue hit $41.46 billion and net income $28.24 billion, both company records, as data center buyers grab every chip they can to feed AI demand. The company is now locking in long-term strategic customer agreements with floor prices that hold margins above any past cycle, which means the memory cost squeeze hitting Nvidia, AMD, Google, and Apple is structural, not a temporary shortage.

title: Coval, which develops simulation and evaluation tech for testing, launching, and monitoring AI voice and chat agents, raised a $28M Series A led by Norwest (Duncan Riley/SiliconANGLE)
blurb: Coval raised a $28M Series A led by Norwest to scale its simulation and evaluation tooling for AI voice and chat agents. As founders ship more agents into production, the bottleneck shifts from building them to proving they work, and testing infrastructure is where that gap gets closed. Coval's bet is that agents need the same test, launch, and monitor loop software teams already expect, and the round signals investors see evaluation as its own layer in the AI stack rather than a feature inside the model.

title: How a $45M donation to a group supporting Trump brought Larry Ellison deeper into Trump's circle, helped Oracle's business, and his son's acquisition of WBD (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Larry Ellison's $45M donation to a pro-Trump group has translated into a personal friendship with the president that's paid off across his business empire. Oracle's federal positioning improved, and his son David's Skydance won regulatory clearance for its Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition. The episode shows how access at the top now moves through private relationships rather than formal lobbying, and how concentrated wealth converts political proximity into outcomes spanning cloud contracts and media consolidation.

title: SK Hynix surges 12% after Micron earnings; blockbuster Nasdaq listing
blurb: SK Hynix shares jumped more than 12% on plans to raise up to $29.4B through a Nasdaq ADR listing, with Micron's quarterly revenue more than quadrupling on AI memory demand reinforcing that high-bandwidth memory stays supply-constrained for years. The dual listing is mainly about narrowing the valuation gap with Micron and opening the Korea-only stock to global investors. With shares already up more than 300% year-to-date, the AI buildout is now propping up an entire memory-chip cohort, and Samsung and TSMC climbed alongside it.

title: New Zealand-based Partly, which develops an AI model for the auto parts industry, raised a $50M Series B led by DST at a $500M valuation, as it enters the US (Duncan Riley/SiliconANGLE)
blurb: Partly raised a $50M Series B led by DST at a $500M valuation, building an AI model for the fragmented auto parts industry as it expands into the US. Auto parts is a classic vertical-AI target: messy catalogs, inconsistent part numbers, and billions in mismatched orders that a model trained on the domain can clean up. The round signals that investors are paying frontier-style multiples for narrow AI applied to unglamorous, data-heavy industries most software never bothered to fix.

title: The Data-Center Boom Is Sparking a Third Wave of Inflation
blurb: Memory chip prices are climbing as the data-center buildout soaks up supply, and the WSJ frames it as a third wave of inflation working through the economy. The same AI capex that's supposed to lift productivity is, for now, bidding up the components everyone else needs, from PCs to phones to cloud bills. The open question is timing: whether AI's efficiency gains arrive fast enough to offset the price pressure the buildout is creating, or whether founders absorb higher hardware and compute costs first.

title: British Business Bank commits €104 million to support UK venture capital fund managers
blurb: The British Business Bank committed up to €104 million as a cornerstone investor in 10 new UK microfunds, its first deployment under the £400m Investor Pathways Capital initiative. The funds run £10 million to £20 million each and target pre-seed and seed first-cheque capital across AI, DeepTech, climate, and defence. The deliberate bet is on first-time managers who struggled to raise institutionally, with women making up 57% of GPs, on the theory that a more diverse pipeline of investors crowds in private capital and builds the early-stage track records the UK is short on.

title: Alan lève 480 millions d’euros et atteint 5,5 milliards d’euros de valorisation
blurb: Alan raised €480M at a €5.5B valuation, led by Prosus with Dara Holdings joining, just three months after a €100M round nearly five times smaller. The French health-insurance scale-up now posts €787M in ARR, up 54% year over year, and turned profitable in France in 2025 even as the group still lost €26M last year. The raise funds acquisitions and international expansion, with Alan already live in Belgium, Spain, and Canada and targeting group-wide profitability by 2027.

title: Exclusive: British Business Bank backs 10 VC funds with £90m pot
blurb: The British Business Bank committed up to £90m to 10 first-time UK VC funds, each sized between £10m and £20m and focused on pre-seed and seed bets in deeptech, defence, and climate. It is the first deployment from the £500m package announced last July to back managers from underrepresented backgrounds, and across the selected GPs 57% are women and 43% are from ethnic minorities. The state bank is funding roughly half of each vehicle to manufacture institutional track records and a capital multiplier at the earliest stage, where new fund types are scarcest.

title: Forget FAANG. Europe needs to bet on BRIOCHE
blurb: Europe has plenty of breakout tech companies but no unifying narrative, which is why one Sifted writer proposes BRIOCHE: Bolt, Revolut, Iceye, Oura, Celonis, Helsing, and ElevenLabs. Unlike FAANG's cluster of California neighbors, these seven span Estonia to Germany, Finland to the UK, across mobility, fintech, satellite intelligence, wearables, enterprise software, defense, and AI. The point isn't the pastry. It's that Europe now has so many credible contenders that Mistral, Synthesia, Spotify, and SAP all got left out, and the continent has stopped needing an American lens to decide which of its companies are worth celebrating.

title: European VC has beaten US funds for 15 years. So why are European LPs still sending billions to a16z?
blurb: European VC funds have beaten North American peers over both 10- and 15-year horizons on an average-fund basis, posting net IRRs of 16.59% and 17.06%, according to Invest Europe's 2025 benchmark. Yet European LPs keep wiring billions across the Atlantic to a16z and other US megafunds. The gap is partly home bias and partly brand: the headline US names feel safer to allocators even when the average European fund quietly delivers better returns.

title: DeepMind handpicked this startup for its robotics accelerator. Now it’s raised an $11.7m seed round
blurb: Greek robotics startup Acumino raised $11.7m in seed funding to scale its deployments with industrial customers, after DeepMind handpicked it for its robotics accelerator. The company builds robot models for dexterous tasks in industrial automation, the kind of fine-motor work that has stayed hard to automate even as warehouse and arm robotics matured. The signal for founders is where robotics money is now flowing: not general-purpose humanoids, but narrow models that make existing industrial hardware capable of the precise, high-variation tasks customers will actually pay to deploy.

title: ALAN dépasse le milliard d’euros levés avec l’arrivée de Prosus
blurb: Alan raised €480M from Prosus at a €5.5B valuation, pushing the French health insurance startup past €1B in total funding. The round lands just three months after a €100M raise and ranks among the largest in Europe this year outside of AI. The pace signals that investor appetite for proven European scale-ups with real revenue, not just AI narratives, is still very much intact.

title: AI was supposed to kill engineering jobs, but new data suggests they’re the most resilient
blurb: Engineering was the most resilient job function in tech in 2025, despite being the field most exposed to AI coding tools. SignalFire's analysis of millions of careers found that while total hiring at large tech companies fell 25% from 2019 levels, engineering roles dropped just 11%, and engineers made up 55% of new hires at the 12 Tech Majors, up from 46% in 2019. If AI were truly substituting for engineers, their hiring would be falling first. Instead it is growing faster than most other functions, and early-stage startups hired 7% more engineers than in 2019.

title: Beyond the $7.8B in deals: Why Wall Street is suddenly watching Argentum AI
blurb: Argentum AI, a startup less than a year old, signed roughly $7.8B in agreements to deploy about 47,000 Nvidia GB300 GPUs across a 300-megawatt datacenter buildout, with sources putting its real backlog above $10B. What has Wall Street watching isn't the GPU count but the model: Argentum locks in long-term customer commitments first, then finances and deploys the infrastructure to fulfill them. That inverts the past two years of neocloud strategy, where everyone raced to buy GPUs first and find customers later, and it looks more like a project-finance platform than an AI startup.

title: Hightouch : pourquoi cette startup a fait une offre à 1,2 milliard de dollars à Publicis
blurb: Hightouch, a composable customer data platform that plugs directly into Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks rather than copying data into a legacy stack, raised a $150M Series D at a $2.75B valuation and now claims $100M in ARR across more than 1,000 customers including Spotify and Warner Music. The pitch has shifted from data activation to agentic marketing, with AI agents that build audiences, generate content variants, and orchestrate across 300-plus channels. At Salomon, the number of people able to create marketing audiences went from a handful to about thirty, which is the real threat to incumbents like Adobe and Salesforce.

title: AI coding token costs are on track to rival human payroll
blurb: Gartner projects that within two years, enterprise spending on a developer's AI coding tokens will match or exceed that same engineer's salary, as consumption-based pricing replaces flat per-seat licensing. The shift is already producing scary outliers, with analysts citing single developers burning $20K in a month and business users hitting $32K. Most organizations have no real visibility into how tokens are billed, no built-in cost controls from vendors, and no framework to tie that spend to ROI, so budgets get wiped out earlier than anyone forecast.

title: Exclusive: XCures Lands $46M Series B To Clean Up Messy Medical Records With AI
blurb: XCures raised $46M in a Series B led by Innovius Capital, valuing the medical-records startup at $127M post-money, more than double its December 2023 Series A. The company connects directly to national healthcare interoperability networks and uses AI to structure the industry's "dirty data," the unusable patient records that hospitals have generated for decades. The round lands inside a fast-rising category: investors have put roughly $8.5B into AI-powered health tech this year through June 22, already nearly matching the $8.6B raised across all of 2024.

title: Slate Auto says each $24,950 electric pickup truck will be profitable as it aims to be cash-flow positive next year
blurb: Slate Auto says every one of its $24,950 electric pickups will ship gross-margin positive, with CEO Peter Faricy targeting positive free cash flow and EBITDA by 2027. The math leans on a stripped-down truck (optional speakers, crank windows, 205-mile range) and a break-even point of roughly 80,000 vehicles a year, just over half its planned 150,000-unit capacity in Warsaw, Indiana. That bet runs against the recent EV-startup record, where Lordstown and Fisker went bankrupt and Rivian and Lucid posted billions in losses before their latest layoffs.

title: Coval raises $28M as enterprises push voice agents into production
blurb: Voice agents fail in ways ordinary software doesn't, stumbling over accents, background noise, and customers who go off-script, and the manual QA most enterprises still rely on doesn't scale. Coval raised $28M to test and monitor those agents through simulation, the same approach its founder built for Waymo's self-driving stack, and says customers cut QA work up to 30 times and ship 10 times faster. With more than $7B poured into voice AI in Q1 alone, the bottleneck is shifting from building agents to proving they work in production.

title: Zombie unicorns are haunting Silicon Valley
blurb: A growing share of the world's unicorns are now stuck: too richly valued to sell, too unproven to go public, and too cash-hungry to keep raising at the prices they once commanded. These zombie companies sit on paper valuations from the last boom while exits stay frozen and down rounds threaten to wipe out earlier investors. For founders, the lesson is that a high valuation you can't grow into becomes a trap, narrowing every option until the only realistic moves are a fire sale, a recap, or a slow wind-down.

title: Visionaries general partner Judith Dada joins AI startup Langdock as co-CEO
blurb: Visionaries general partner Judith Dada is joining Berlin-based Langdock as co-CEO in September, while staying a senior partner at the firm ahead of its next fundraise. Langdock, which lets companies route work across Gemini, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Mistral from one platform, recently hit $40M in ARR, turned profitable, and serves more than 10k customers despite last raising just $3M in 2024. The move lands as Europe's sovereign-AI debate heats up, and Dada's mandate is largely external: making the public case to policymakers and customers that AI adoption can go well.

title: Chipmakers Rally With Traders Piling In After Micron Results
blurb: Semiconductor stocks jumped in early trading after Micron's strong results showed AI demand driving rapid profit growth, pulling in traders across the chip sector. Qualcomm and other chipmakers rallied alongside it, with the move reading as confirmation that AI capex is still flowing straight into memory and silicon. For founders building on AI infrastructure, the signal is that the hardware layer remains a seller's market, and compute costs aren't easing while demand keeps outrunning supply.

title: NAND flash maker Kioxia, Japan's most valuable company since June 12, plans to offer US depositary shares in spring 2027 amid AI-driven demand for memory chips (Bloomberg)
blurb: Kioxia, the NAND flash maker that became Japan's most valuable company on June 12, plans to issue US depositary shares in spring 2027 alongside a stock split to ride AI-driven demand for memory chips. The move signals that the memory layer of the AI stack has become a market story in its own right, not just a supplier footnote to GPUs. For founders building on AI infrastructure, it is another sign that memory supply and pricing are tightening as the buildout pulls in every component upstream of the model.

title: Agentic infrastructure startup Seltz raises $12.5M to help AI agents search the web for answers
blurb: Seltz raised $12.5M in seed funding led by Speedinvest and B Capital to build search infrastructure designed for AI agents rather than humans. The bet is that traditional engines surface ranked links for people, while agents need structured evidence pulled from tables, images, and page-level text that Google and Bing don't easily reach. Founder Antonio Mallia built the entire stack in-house, from crawlers to ranking, instead of reselling queries through an existing provider, which signals where retrieval value moves as machines, not people, become the primary consumer of search.

title: German startup FINN hits €1B valuation in seven years by building on the belief Europeans would stop buying cars
blurb: FINN, the Munich car-subscription platform, raised a €140M Series D and crossed a €1B valuation seven years after betting that Europeans would shift from owning cars to renting them by the month. The bet is paying off: more than 50,000 active subscriptions and over €300M in ARR, with revenue climbing from €3.2M to €444M between 2022 and 2024. The catch is concentration, as Germany accounts for roughly 34% of a European market projected to grow about 25% a year through 2035, so FINN's home strength is also its main dependency.

title: Micron’s Blockbuster Earnings Quiet the AI Doubters
blurb: Micron posted blockbuster earnings driven by surging demand for the high-bandwidth memory that AI data centers run on, and the stock rally pulled Nasdaq futures up with it. The print lands as a direct rebuttal to the growing camp arguing the AI buildout is overextended, because memory is the part of the stack that only sells when customers are actually deploying compute.
