For founders, it is a read on whether the infrastructure spending your roadmap assumes is still accelerating or starting to cool.

title: Micron Shares Jump as Forecast Beats Estimates
blurb: Micron guided fiscal fourth-quarter revenue to roughly $50 billion, well ahead of Wall Street estimates, and shares jumped in late trading. The beat signals that AI-driven demand for high-bandwidth memory is still accelerating, not cooling, well past the early ChatGPT surge. For founders, it is another read that the AI capex cycle running through chips and data centers shows no sign of topping out, which keeps compute pricing and availability a live variable in any product roadmap.

title: The Netherlands just sent its trade minister to lobby US Congress directly — and the reason exposes who actually pays when Washington tightens the chip war
blurb: The Netherlands sent its trade minister to lobby US Congress directly against the MATCH Act, a bill that would extend China export curbs to ASML's older DUV immersion machines. The point this exposes: Washington designs the chip-war perimeter, but the revenue hit lands on companies that don't vote in US elections. US firms like Nvidia and AMD design the chips, while ASML, TSMC, and Japanese suppliers build the machines and absorb the lost sales, and China has been one of ASML's largest single markets, so the alignment that held for years is now starting to crack.

title: GLM 5.2: why I’m replacing Opus in Claude Code with this new model
blurb: Claire Vo ran GLM 5.2, Z.AI's open-weight coding model, through four real tasks in her production Next.js app, including a 45-minute autonomous bug-hunt pulling Sentry and Vercel logs, for $3.36 across roughly 6M tokens. She argues open weights buy cost and vendor independence worth the trade-offs, and reports the model matched ChatPRD's design system on the first try.

title: Making Windows a developer platform, again
blurb: Microsoft introduced Coreutils for Windows at Build 2026, a maintained fork of the Rust-based uutils coreutils that installs as a single binary and brings standard Unix commands like `ls -al` to PowerShell and cmd. The preview matches Unix syntax and output formatting so scripts port across WSL and native Windows, though it remains incomplete and conflicts with existing cmd and PowerShell commands.

title: Constraint Tax in Open-Weight LLMs: An Empirical Study of Tool Calling Suppression Under Structured Output Constraints
blurb: This paper documents Tool Suppression, a reproducible failure where multiple open-weight models stop calling tools once JSON Schema constraints and tool calling are enabled together, even though each works fine in isolation. The authors trace it to grammar-based token masks that make tool-call tokens unreachable during decoding, and propose Transparent Two-Pass Execution to decouple tool calls from schema-constrained generation without retraining.

title: Cloudflare launched self-managed OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all Developer Platform customers, letting developers create and manage their own OAuth clients for scoped, delegated access to the Cloudflare API instead of relying on harder-to-manage API tokens. The release adds clearer consent screens, dashboard-based revocation, and visible app ownership to curb OAuth phishing, targeting SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and agentic tools that need delegated access.

title: Why Multi-Step Tool-Use Reinforcement Learning Collapses and How Supervisory Signals Fix It
blurb: This paper traces catastrophic collapse in multi-step tool-use RL to probability spikes in specific control tokens that break tool-invocation structure, while the underlying tool-use capability stays intact behind the broken format. The authors test supervisory signals like off-policy supervision and hint-based guidance, and find that interleaving SFT with RL stabilizes training at the cost of degraded performance on format and content out-of-distribution evaluation.

title: LuaJIT 3.0 proposed syntax extensions
blurb: LuaJIT maintainer Mike Pall opened an umbrella issue proposing a set of syntax extensions for LuaJIT 3.0, with consolidated documentation evolving in the thread. The proposals are scoped to additions that improve quality-of-life, are proven in other languages or Lua dialects, introduce no syntactic ambiguity, and preserve backwards compatibility, explicitly avoiding the complexity of Perl, Ruby, C++, or Rust.

title: LibEvoBench: Probing Temporal Knowledge Stratification in Code Generation Models
blurb: LibEvoBench tests whether code models reason about library versions; results show they're version-oblivious, and only supplying docs (not naming the version) helps.

title: Evaluating LLMs on Real-World Software Performance Optimization
blurb: SWE-Pro is a repository-level benchmark built from 102 expert-written optimizations across open-source projects, pairing each task with parameterized tests that measure runtime, peak memory, and Time-Weighted Memory Usage under noise-aware conditions. Current LLMs post negligible runtime gains and almost no memory wins, against expert implementations that hit an aggregate 15.5x speedup and 171.3x peak memory reduction.

title: How Netflix Simplified Batch Compute with Kueue
blurb: Netflix replaced the custom queuing and scheduling logic in its homegrown Compute Managed Batch system with Kueue, a cloud-native job queueing system for batch workloads, as part of moving its Titus container platform toward Kubernetes-native components. The post covers how the team migrated millions of batch jobs to Kueue while preserving CMB's tenant hierarchy, priority-ordered execution, and per-tenant capacity reservations federated across multiple Kubernetes cells.

title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
blurb: Curl shipped patches for 18 vulnerabilities, the most CVEs ever in a single release, including CVE-2026-8932, an mTLS connection-reuse bug introduced in version 7.7 in 2001 that can bypass authentication in libcurl applications. The flaws surfaced through a community audit after Anthropic's Mythos and Aisle's AI platform began probing the hard-to-reach paths in a library running on over 30 billion devices.

title: IntentTester: Intent-Driven Multi-agent Framework for Cross-Library Test Migration
blurb: IntentTester is a multi-agent framework that migrates unit tests across libraries and languages by abstracting them into a language-agnostic Test Description Language, aligning them to a repository graph, and synthesizing executable tests through LLM reasoning and iterative validation. Across nine Java and Python projects it produced 2,776 syntactically correct tests at 85% correctness versus 51% and 43% for structure-mapping baselines, and surfaced real defects including stack overflows and null dereferences that maintainers have patched.

title: Half-Life 2 in a Browser
blurb: A developer ported Half-Life 2 to run in the browser via WebAssembly, playable directly at the link.

title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
blurb: The author pointed go-git at a Tigris object storage bucket using billy, a Go filesystem abstraction, demonstrating that a git server can run directly on S3-style storage with no /usr/bin/git binary involved. The post walks through git's actual storage model, where a repository is just objects, trees, commits, and refs, and shows that go-git's filesystem-native design makes a bucket-backed repo Just Work.

title: Unlocking Model Potentials Through Adaptive Multi-Agent Scaffolding for Efficient Issue Resolution
blurb: icat-agent is a decentralized multi-agent scaffold that replaces shared context with synchronous event-based message passing, routing well-defined issues to parallel patching and validation while sending vague ones through preliminary exploration first. On SWE-bench Pro it improves 6.3-18.5% over SWE-agent, mini-SWE-agent, and Claude Code using the same backbone models, and cuts cost by $1.18 per instance versus the Claude Code baseline.

title: What you need to know about Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, which run user-supplied or AI-generated code in Firecracker-isolated sandboxes that boot in seconds from SnapStart snapshots and keep memory and disk state for up to 8 hours. Each session gets a persistent VM with a dedicated HTTPS endpoint over HTTP/2, gRPC, or WebSockets, auto-suspending when idle so you pay nothing between bursts of traffic.

title: The Dependency Black Hole
blurb: An exploratory case study of a product built from 267 microservices triangulated compile-time, run-time, and task dependencies and found a recurring "black hole" structure: a dense core of dependency magnets, a transitional region of increasingly entangled services, and a lightly connected outer ring. The authors map this to a singularity, event horizon, and accretion disk, proposing a theory for spotting services at risk of becoming magnets and motivating governance interventions before concentration compounds.

title: CodeChat-Eval: Evaluating Large Language Models in Multi-Turn Code Refinement Dialogues
blurb: CodeChat-Eval introduces a benchmark for multi-turn code refinement, testing whether LLMs preserve functional correctness when developers issue follow-up instructions like restructuring or style changes after the initial generation. Across open-weight and proprietary models, functional correctness dropped between 19.2% (GPT-5 Nano) and 69.2% (Llama 3.1 8B) over successive refinements, with logic-level and additive change requests causing the largest regressions.

title: PostgreSQL is enough (2024)
blurb: The author argues that PostgreSQL alone can replace most of the specialized infrastructure teams reach for by default, covering job queues, full-text and vector search, audit logging, change-data-capture, and REST/GraphQL APIs through extensions and built-in features. The post catalogs the tooling for each use case, from pg_cron and pgmq to pgvector and PostgREST, making the case that one well-understood database beats a sprawl of services you have to operate separately.

title: The Problem is Prompt Debt
blurb: The author argues that hand-tuned natural language prompts accrue "prompt debt," a slow-building liability that caps what AI systems can become. As teams pile edge-case instructions and all-caps threats onto a single prompt, iteration grinds to a halt, the prompt becomes illegible to colleagues, and the application locks to one aging model because hot fixes break on any newer one.

title: JetBrains Marketplace Ecosystem Security Update: Addressing Malicious Third-Party AI Plugins
blurb: JetBrains removed 15 third-party AI plugins from its Marketplace on June 16 after reports that they masqueraded as text-generation and unit-testing utilities to harvest developer-configured AI provider API keys. The company banned the 7 publisher accounts behind the campaign and triggered a backend kill-switch that disables the plugins on next IDE relaunch, but anyone who used them should revoke affected keys immediately.

title: Analyzing Claude Code usage with CloudWatch and OpenTelemetry
blurb: AWS's guide shows how to ship Claude Code telemetry to CloudWatch over the now-GA OTLP endpoint using bearer token auth, sending token consumption and per-developer cost metrics straight from developer laptops with no collector, sidecar, or IAM credential wiring. Once connected, the metrics are queryable in PromQL for team-level usage analytics and cost attribution, with the trade-off that bearer tokens are long-term credentials best avoided for in-AWS workloads where SigV4 fits.

title: GitHub shouldn't be a dependency for publishing Rust on crates.io
blurb: The author argues that crates.io's reliance on GitHub for authentication makes Microsoft a single point of failure for publishing Rust packages, leaving the ecosystem exposed to outages, account lockouts, and policy changes outside its control. The piece calls for independent authentication options so registry access doesn't hinge on one company's infrastructure or terms of service.

title: TabClean: Reusable LLM-Synthesized Programs for Tabular Data Cleaning
blurb: TabClean compiles LLM reasoning into reusable guarded cleaning programs, profiling a dirty table and a small annotated dev set to synthesize deterministic Python transformations that fire only when their dirty pattern, evidence support, and scope constraints all hold. The point is cost: instead of calling an LLM per row or per recurring batch, you pay once to compile a program and run it deterministically thereafter. Across six benchmarks it beats rule-based, learning-based, and LLM-based cleaners on F1 for five datasets while cutting recurring runtime and API spend.

title: Mistral OCR 4
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, an OCR model that returns bounding boxes, typed-block classification, and inline confidence scores alongside extracted text, with support for 170 languages and single-container self-hosted deployment. The structured output feeds RAG and enterprise search pipelines with citation-ready inputs, and Mistral reports a 72% average win rate against leading OCR systems plus a top 85.20 score on OlmOCRBench.

title: BitNet Text Embeddings
blurb: BITEMBED converts pretrained LLM backbones like Qwen3-0.6B and Gemma3-270M into BitNet-style embedding encoders with ternary weights and quantized activations, recovering near full-precision quality on MMTEB through contrastive pre-training and distillation from a full-precision teacher. The payoff is on the storage side: output embeddings are trained to support multiple precisions, so teams can dial the performance-versus-cost trade-off on large indexes instead of paying full-precision bandwidth on every vector lookup.

title: PostgreSQL is enough (2024)
blurb: The author argues that Postgres alone can replace most of the specialized tooling teams reach for by default, cataloging extensions and patterns that cover queues (pgmq), cron (pg_cron), vector search (pgvector), full-text search, analytics columnstores (ParadeDB, Hydra), CDC (Debezium, PeerDB), and REST/GraphQL APIs straight from the database. The trade-off is operational concentration, but the payoff is one system to run, back up, and reason about instead of a sprawling stack glued together with reverse ETL.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of image foundation models built for creative exploration rather than the single polished default that production diffusion tools tend to converge toward. The system pairs a custom diffusion transformer with a multi-stage pipeline of pretraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL, plus components like grouped-query attention and a Qwen3-VL text encoder to widen its range across styles, moods, and compositions.

title: Space4Architecture transforms Brooklyn warehouse into residence
blurb: Space4Architecture converted a 1931 warehouse in Brooklyn's Columbia Street Waterfront District into a 4,400-square-foot residence, retaining the industrial shell behind a black-painted facade and opening the interior with a double-height living space. The project sits on a tight 2,500-square-foot infill parcel, trading the original footprint's bulk for vertical volume while keeping the neighborhood's warehouse vernacular intact.

title: BMW Unveils Striking M Concept Neue Klasse
blurb: BMW revealed the M Concept Neue Klasse, its design study for an electric performance M-series, introducing yellow M Lights, three-dimensional Track Lights, and a trimaran-style front apron that doubles as structural support for the splitter. Industry reports peg it as a near-production design targeting 2027 or 2028, signaling how BMW M intends to carry its visual identity into an EV lineup built on the cleaner Neue Klasse language.

title: Why a stylist and her crew built a magazine instead of just moaning about AI
blurb: Fashion stylist Anna May and a small crew of photographers, makeup artists, and an art director responded to AI's spread through editorial imagery by self-funding MISC, a print magazine now on its second issue. The piece is a case for making something physical and human instead of just complaining, built on favors and unpaid time rather than a business plan.

title: Ilse Crawford designs pair of lamps for IKEA "that supports decor rather than dominating it"
blurb: Ilse Crawford designed Halgatt, a pair of ceramic-based, linen-shaded table lamps for IKEA, offered in a square form at £25 and a rounded form at £35. The collaboration continues a partnership that began with 2015's bamboo Sinnerlig lamp, and leans on classic proportions and earthy tones meant to support a room's decor rather than dominate it.

title: Does the 2026 World Cup font have a problem?
blurb: FWC26, the bespoke ultra-condensed typeface by Alistair McCready at Monolith, gives the 2026 World Cup a cohesive identity by cramming heavy geometric letters into tight spaces across jerseys, signage, and stadium displays. Typographers including Oliver Schöndorfer argue the same density breaks down on broadcast scoreboards and small stats labels, where the tight forms turn illegible and call for a dedicated low-resolution variant.

title: Ikea turns everyday objects into ingenious World Cup guessing game
blurb: Ikea Canada and Dentsu Creative launched Assemble the World, a social-first campaign that rebuilds 18 World Cup nations' flags from everyday Ikea products, like a VÅGSJÖN blanket as Canada's maple leaf and a PEPPRIG duster as Sweden's yellow stripes. Followers guess the products behind each flag, turning a brand tie-in into an interactive shopping prompt that also runs as OOH near stores through the summer.

title: Dramatic atriums define Bangkok shopping centre by Linehouse
blurb: Linehouse designed the interiors of Bangkok's 40,000-square-metre Central Park shopping centre around three atriums clad in silver, copper, and bronze, metals read as auspicious in Thai culture. Each void carries a distinct character, from the calm Silver Void at street level to the community-hub Copper Void, whose cladding shifts from dusty pink to reddish copper under overhead light against stepped, illuminated escalators.

title: When LLM Rationales Become User-Facing: Effects on Trust Perception, Decision-Making, and Gaze Behaviors
blurb: Two linked studies test whether LLM reasoning rationales help users calibrate trust or just persuade through fluent prose, finding that rationale correctness and certainty framing matter while presentation format does not, and that incorrect rationales drew more attention and cognitive effort while lowering trust in the system. The authors argue against the assumption that more reasoning is always better, favoring rationales that are selective, tied to evidence, and calibrated in how they express certainty.

title: Your design system runs on one person’s judgment-AI is about to prove it
blurb: The author argues that every mature design system develops a single human gatekeeper who reviews each contribution for tokens, naming, API sanity, and accessibility, making that person an unacknowledged single point of failure. As AI-generated contributions flood review queues, the post contends manual gatekeeping becomes untenable, and the only fix is dispersing ownership without lowering the quality bar.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is prepending JavaScript block comments containing fake nuclear and biological weapons policy text to spyware payloads, aiming to derail LLM-based triage that feeds file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data. The trick can trigger refusal, context pollution, or premature classification in naive LLM-first scanners, but it does not evade YARA rules, entropy checks, AST parsing, or behavioral detection.

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant disclosed active zero-day exploitation of CVE-2026-20245, a privilege-escalation flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that lets an attacker with a compromised admin account reach root via a malicious CSV upload that bypasses file-upload filtering. The actor, observed at a service provider in early 2026, established rogue SSH peering, altered default account passwords, and ran anti-forensic cleanup scripts to purge indicators, so defenders should patch, rotate appliance credentials, and audit peering configurations.

title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration in which untrusted pull requests trigger privileged GitHub Actions workflows, enabling command injection, credential theft, and supply-chain compromise exploitable by any unauthenticated user with a free account. A scan of roughly 30,000 high-impact repositories found 300+ fully exploitable, including projects at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare; audit workflows that run on `pull_request_target` and strip write permissions and secrets from untrusted PR triggers.

title: CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added four actively exploited flaws to its KEV catalog, including three max-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS bugs (CVE-2026-34908, -34909, -34910) that Bishop Fox chained for unauthenticated RCE with elevated privileges, plus a critical Lantronix EDS5000 command-injection (CVE-2025-67038). BOD 26-04 gives federal agencies three days to patch; apply Ubiquiti's May updates and Lantronix firmware 2.2.0.0R1, and run Bishop Fox's detection script to find vulnerable instances.

title: Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
blurb: Operation Endgame disrupted the Amadey botnet and StealC infostealer, with Microsoft, Europol, and partners taking down 326 servers and 142 domains tied to the malware-as-a-service operations, alongside the SocGholish loader. The action recovered roughly 27 million credentials stolen from over 385,000 systems; defenders should treat any credentials handled on affected hosts as compromised and force rotation.

title: Anthropic Claims Alibaba Ran ‘Brazen’ Campaign to Access Its Claude AI Model
blurb: Anthropic alleged in a letter obtained by the Wall Street Journal that Alibaba and its AI unit ran "the largest known distillation attack" against Claude to date. The accusation escalates IP-theft disputes around model distillation, where a rival systematically queries a frontier model to replicate its outputs and train a competing system.

title: macOS Weaknesses Chained to Silently Disable Endpoint Security Agents
blurb: XM Cyber demonstrated a macOS attack chain that lets a standard non-admin user silently unload EDR and MDM agents, abusing weakly validated XPC connections, NIB-file payload injection, and persistence of the kernel code-signing trust cache rather than any kernel exploit. Researchers fully unloaded CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor and permanently deactivated Kandji MDM, which assigned CVE-2026-39118 and patched; an open-source discovery tool, XPC Hunter, is due at Black Hat US in August.

title: Critical Ubiquiti Vulnerabilities in Attackers’ Crosshairs
blurb: CISA warns that three critical Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws (CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910, all CVSS 10) are under active exploitation, chaining an NGINX authentication-gateway bypass into unauthenticated command injection that lets attackers create rogue admin accounts. Ubiquiti patched the bugs in UniFi OS Server 5.0.8 on May 21, but reports indicate zero-day exploitation predating the fix, so operators should patch immediately and audit for unauthorized administrator accounts.

title: Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google released Chrome 149, patching 18 vulnerabilities including four critical, with more than half being use-after-free memory bugs (three critical, seven high) that can enable RCE and, when chained with OS or privileged-process flaws, sandbox escape. None are known to be exploited in the wild; update to 149.0.7827.196/197 on Windows and macOS and 149.0.7827.196 on Linux.

title: In a first, a court takedown goes after two cybercrime tools at once
blurb: Microsoft and law enforcement executed a single court-authorized takedown against both Amadey, a malware-delivery botnet, and StealC, a Russia-linked infostealer sold as malware-as-a-service, disrupting over 200 command-and-control servers under the RICO Act. The two tools were tied to more than 140,000 infected machines in the first week of May, so defenders should hunt for Amadey and StealC indicators and rotate any credentials exposed to infostealer activity.

title: Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching
blurb: Mandiant detailed exploitation of CVE-2026-20245, a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CLI flaw that an authenticated local attacker used to escalate to root, weaponized as a zero-day months before Cisco's early-June disclosure and patch. The actor reached an SD-WAN Manager via SSH using the default vmanage-admin account, escalated to root, then deleted artifacts and restored configurations to evade detection, making it the 7th SD-WAN bug found exploited in 2026.

title: 양자컴퓨터 시대 대비 나선 미국…PQC 의무화와 양자 기술 육성 병행
blurb: President Trump signed two executive orders accelerating federal post-quantum cryptography migration, setting deadlines of December 31, 2030 for key-establishment mechanisms and December 31, 2031 for digital signatures, while mandating per-agency crypto inventories and a Cryptographic Bill of Materials standard from NIST and CISA within 270 days. The orders cite "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" collection by adversaries and extend compliance pressure to federal contractors and critical-infrastructure operators that must prove PQC readiness.

title: Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke
blurb: Symantec disclosed Mistic, a stealthy new backdoor active since April in financially motivated intrusions against insurance, education, IT, and professional services organizations, and links it to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells footholds to Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta. Mistic side-loads a malicious version.dll via MpExtMs.exe, runs payloads in memory with no disk artifacts, and includes a self-delete kill switch built for long-term, low-visibility persistence.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230 (CVSS 8.6), an SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME that lets an unauthenticated remote attacker write files to the OS and escalate to root. Cisco patched it on June 3 with no workaround; defenders should patch immediately and, where possible, disable the WebDialer service, which is required for exploitation and off by default.

title: Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware
blurb: Microsoft, Europol, ESET, Proofpoint, and other partners disrupted the shared command-and-control infrastructure behind the Amadey loader and StealC infostealer under Operation Endgame, targeting hundreds of domains and servers and seizing over 25M stolen credentials from 385,000 systems. Investigators used AI-driven analysis to link the two families' C&C and exploited a flaw in the StealC panel to upload a web shell and collect takedown data.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers at XM Cyber disclosed a macOS privilege-escalation technique that lets a standard user disable enterprise security tools and invoke root-level functions without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or triggering alerts. The flaw stems from how macOS caches and reuses an application's CDHash, letting an attacker impersonate trusted components to abuse privileged XPC services, demonstrated against CrowdStrike Falcon EDR and Kandji MDM; Apple has not responded.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis models leak their training data membership, letting an attacker query a model to determine whether a specific patient's records were used to train it. The technique is a membership inference attack against the clinical AI itself, exposing protected health data without breaching the underlying dataset and undermining the de-identification assumptions hospitals rely on when sharing models.

title: New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns
blurb: Researchers linked a new in-memory backdoor named Mistic (aka MLTBackdoor) to initial-access broker KongTuke, deployed since April 2026 alongside the Python-based ModeloRAT against insurance, education, IT, and professional-services orgs via ClickFix lures. Per Symantec and Carbon Black, Mistic side-loads through trusted Microsoft tooling (MpExtMs.exe), runs payloads in memory with no disk artifacts, loads Beacon Object Files, and self-deletes via a kill switch for long-term, low-visibility access.

title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
blurb: Mandiant found an unknown threat actor exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN flaw CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS 7.8) as a zero-day for at least two months before disclosure, uploading a malicious CSV to gain root on a communications provider's device. The attack chained earlier authentication-bypass zero-days (CVE-2026-20127, CVE-2026-20182) and used anti-forensic config restoration to evade detection, so apply Cisco's patches and audit SD-WAN peering connections and admin credentials.

title: White House app auto-downloads to government phones, can't be uninstalled
blurb: The White House automatically pushed its mobile app onto the work phones of millions of federal employees, and workers across the USDA, State Department, and Department of Labor report being unable to uninstall it. The app, identical to the public version, lacks a dedicated privacy or data policy beyond a contact email, raising questions about what device permissions and telemetry it carries on government hardware.

title: How Reliable Is Your Jailbreak Judge? Calibration and Adversarial Robustness of Automated ASR Scoring
blurb: This paper audits the automated judges that assign attack-success rates in LLM jailbreak research, testing two families against 596 human-labeled HarmBench completions. Dedicated classifiers over-flag (recall 0.974) but resist surface attacks, while LLM-as-judges hold precision yet show erratic recall (0.06 to 0.65); benign framing wrappers flip every LLM-judge 57% to 100% of the time without altering the harmful content. The authors recommend reporting judge precision and recall on a human-labeled slice and adding an adversarial check.

title: Can Machine Learning Break Wi-Fi Privacy? A Study on MAC Address Randomization
blurb: This paper demonstrates that MAC address randomization, the 802.11 countermeasure against passive Wi-Fi tracking, can be defeated through ML-based device fingerprinting of unencrypted Probe Frame fields combined with inter-probe timing and signal-strength signals. Using DBSCAN clustering with a bitwise decomposition of the HT capabilities field and three SRSSI measurements, the authors re-identified devices across 22 units from six manufacturers at up to 89.6% accuracy.

title: LLM security advice looks solid until you check the hard cases
blurb: HelpBench, built by University College London and Google, tested 18 leading LLMs against 450 real privacy and security help questions sourced from Reddit and scored by expert rubrics. Advice averaged 82%, strongest on scams and account compromise, but roughly one in ten answers fell below 65%, with the worst failures in abuse and stalking scenarios. Models advised removing phone spyware without warning that abrupt removal can tip off an abuser, and praised an encrypted-vault deletion that left the original file recoverable. Watch what your users are being told.

title: Automated Detection of Configuration-Specific Security Vulnerabilities via Patch Analysis
blurb: PatchLens is a static analysis technique that recovers Vulnerability Impact Conditions, Boolean predicates over compile-time options identifying which configuration variants of C/C++ software contained a given flaw, without compiling any variant. Evaluated across 1,192 Linux kernel, 289 FFmpeg, and 100 PHP patches, it produces compact predicates and shows that CVE texts encode the required options only about 1% of the time, enabling variant-aware CI triage, targeted fuzzing, and feature risk scoring.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout extends LFR from temporary van-mounted deployments to fixed infrastructure, despite ongoing legal challenges and civil-liberties objections over consent, bias, and the absence of specific statutory authorization in UK law.

title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
blurb: Curl patched 18 vulnerabilities in a single release, including CVE-2026-8932, a 25-year-old mTLS connection-reuse flaw introduced in version 7.7 (March 2001) that lets libcurl reuse a connection after client certificate or private key changes, enabling authentication bypass. The bug affects libcurl applications rather than the command-line tool, with no public reports of in-the-wild exploitation; upgrade affected applications to the patched release.

title: A 13-word edit can steer what deep-research AI agents recommend
blurb: Cornell Tech researchers disclosed WARP (Web Agent Retrieval Poisoning), an attack that injects roughly 13 words into a Reddit thread, Wikipedia page, or forum post that deep-research agents already tend to retrieve, causing the agents to cite and recommend attacker-chosen fake entities. Fake entities surfaced in 38% to 51% of reports when one poisoned page was retrieved, rising to 62% across multiple pages, with no access to the model, prompts, or retrieval system required.

title: The Web4 Agent Economy: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of the Landscape, Challenges, and Opportunities
blurb: A large-scale empirical study of the emerging "Web4" agent economy analyzed 99,448 identity registrations, 317M transaction logs, and 341 MCP projects, finding autonomous agents already process millions of daily machine-to-machine payments on protocols like MCP, x402, and EIP-8004. The authors warn this growth rests on immature infrastructure, with weak identity and authorization practices and unresolved payment interoperability as the most persistent security gap.

title: Exploiting vulnerabilities in Johnson and Johnson web apps
blurb: A security researcher disclosed two vulnerabilities in Johnson & Johnson web apps, including a campus recruiting site that exposed data on nearly 1,000 students because its MSAL frontend auth was unenforced server-side, relying instead on a hardcoded API key the client could bypass. The second flaw enabled admin takeover of an internal audit system covering 20 J&J subsidiaries; both have since been remediated.

title: UK school’s network left wide open for invasion, student found
blurb: A student at a UK school discovered the institution's network was exposed with weak access controls, leaving internal systems reachable to anyone who probed them. The finding underscores how unmanaged edge devices and default configurations in education environments remain trivial to compromise, and defenders running similar setups should audit external attack surface and enforce segmentation before a less benign actor finds the same gap.

title: The hits keep on coming for Cisco vulnerabilities
blurb: The Register catalogs a continuing run of disclosures across Cisco's product line, from edge appliances to management software, with several flaws drawing active exploitation interest. Teams running Cisco gear on the perimeter should track the vendor's advisory feed closely, prioritize patches for internet-facing devices, and treat any unpatched exposed appliance as a likely target rather than a deferred maintenance item.

title: A Tattered Cloak of Invisibility: Measuring Anonymity Loss in Railgun on Ethereum
blurb: A new paper measuring anonymity loss in the Railgun mixer on Ethereum links 17.65% of withdrawals to specific deposits using five behavioral heuristics: timing patterns, address reuse, transaction-graph proximity, amount fingerprints, and knapsack-style amount matches. The authors report a 3.42-bit median anonymity loss from the knapsack solver alone, showing that cryptographically strong privacy pools still leak heavily through user behavior rather than protocol weakness.

title: Anthropic Says Alibaba Ran 28.8 Million Claude Exchanges Through Fake Accounts
blurb: Anthropic told U.S. officials that operators affiliated with Alibaba's Qwen lab ran 28.8 million Claude exchanges through nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to harvest outputs for adversarial distillation. Per a June 10 letter, the April 22 to June 5 campaign targeted Claude's software engineering and agentic reasoning skills and exceeds Anthropic's February claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax combined.

title: Decoupling Reconnaissance and Exploitation: Measuring the Capability Boundaries of LLM-Based Web Penetration Testing
blurb: This paper proposes a two-stage evaluation framework that separates exploitation from reconnaissance to measure where LLM-based penetration-testing agents actually fail, using ground-truth injection across 70 web vulnerability testbeds. Given accurate vulnerability context, five open-source agents hit a 90% functional success rate on exploitation, but autonomous reconnaissance plateaus near 50% recall, mostly from failing to parse unstructured telemetry. Architecture matters: multi-agent designs handle long-chain deserialization better, while monolithic and graph-driven agents win on short-chain injections and access-control flaws.

title: NIST Opens Updated IoT Security Guidance to Public Review
blurb: NIST opened the initial public draft of SP 800-213 Revision 1, "IoT Product Cybersecurity Guidelines for the Federal Government," for comment through August 24. The revision reframes guidance around IoT products rather than devices, treats them as system elements in risk management, and ties into SP 800-30, SP 800-53 Rev. 5, and the SP 800-213A capability catalog.

title: Helpful or Harmful? Evaluating LLM-Assisted Vulnerability Patching via a Human Study
blurb: This paper proposes a controlled human study, using a balanced crossover design, to test whether LLM-assisted vulnerability patching actually outperforms manual debugging or just produces superficial fixes. The key methodological detail is hidden "Ghost Tests" that validate patch security beyond the visible functional checks, catching repairs that pass functionality but fail security validation. Only a pilot has run so far, so treat the full results as forthcoming rather than established.

title: Exclusive: Meet AIVEX, a New Triage Model Built to Reduce Supply Chain Threat and Risk
blurb: AIVEX, a CycloneDX VEX extension from researcher Devashri Datta, adds machine-readable exploitation context to vulnerability triage, pairing with a safety relevance interpretation layer (SRIL) that scores how a flaw's impact shifts across AI lifecycle stages. The pair aims to fix SBOM, VEX, and CVSS-based prioritization, which Datta argues misses context and lets supply chain attacks slip through; her SRIL writeup publishes via ISACA on July 1.

title: Scoring AI hackers when there is no answer key
blurb: FrontierCyber, a new benchmark from AI security lab Irregular, drops models onto real systems with live defenses and no planted bugs or hints, then scores how far they get toward a security goal. The targets are everyday phones, hosted services, databases, and networks, each keeping its real sandboxing, authentication, and network boundaries intact. Because difficulty is unknown up front, Irregular predicts each challenge's band before runs and wires systems with canary strings and planted markers to credit partial progress.

title: Tracing Target Answers in Poisoned Retrieval Corpora via Token Influence Attribution
blurb: This paper introduces TRACE, a lightweight framework that detects corpus-poisoning attacks against RAG systems by tracing answer-related tokens through token influence attribution, avoiding the auxiliary classifiers or secondary LLM verification that existing detectors require. Across three QA benchmarks and six LLMs, TRACE flagged poisoned retrieved documents and recovered the attacker-specified target answers, giving defenders a low-overhead way to audit retrieval corpora for injection.

title: NSA Loses Anthropic Mythos Access After June Export-Control Order
blurb: The NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 cybersecurity model after the Trump administration's June export-control directive forced the company to suspend its most advanced models for all foreign nationals, including noncitizen employees on US soil. The cutoff hit analysts mid-evaluation, where Mythos had surfaced software vulnerabilities in a controlled red-team test that Sen. Mark Warner initially characterized as breaking into classified systems "in hours.

title: Representation Matters: An Empirical Study of Program Representations for LLM Vulnerability Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces RepBench, a benchmark comparing how program representations affect LLM vulnerability detection across 107 Joern-derived C/C++ testcases spanning five CWE categories. The AST+PDG variant hit 83.2% accuracy versus 53.5% for raw source code, and graph-only prompts beat source-plus-graph while using far less context. The takeaway for AppSec teams building LLM detection tooling: feed models static-analysis graphs, not raw source, since dumping in full source dilutes the vulnerability-relevant evidence and degrades reasoning.

title: SoK: AI Secure Code Generation: Progress, Pitfalls, and Paths Forward
blurb: This SoK evaluates AI secure code generation with a three-level framework measuring models' natural-language understanding of secure-coding principles, their code-level actuation during generation, and the gap between the two. Principle understanding strongly predicts security, functional correctness, and joint outcomes, but a large knowledge-actuation gap persists: models recognize the right security principles yet fail to implement them in working code. Teams relying on coding agents should keep security review in the loop rather than trusting that the model knows better.

title: Security and Privacy in Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Architectures, Threats, Defenses, and Future Directions for Building Trustworthy Systems
blurb: This survey maps the security and privacy attack surface of Retrieval-Augmented Generation across centralized, on-device (Micro-RAG), federated, and hybrid deployments. The authors build a taxonomy spanning the retrieval, context-construction, and generation stages, cataloging attack classes including membership inference, index inference, knowledge-base poisoning, gradient leakage, and collusion, then review architectural, algorithmic, and cryptographic defenses against each. Useful for teams threat-modeling RAG pipelines beyond the usual prompt-injection focus.

title: Nation-state actors cracked critical Australian infrastructure to ‘cripple it at a time of their choosing’
blurb: Australia's intelligence agencies disclosed that nation-state actors have compromised critical infrastructure networks and established persistent access positioned to disrupt operations at a time of their choosing. The intrusions reflect pre-positioning rather than immediate sabotage, so operators of energy, water, and telecom systems should hunt for living-off-the-land activity, audit dormant accounts, and segment OT networks to limit lateral movement.

title: BlowLive: Blow-Based Multi-Factor Biometrics with Liveness Detection and Revocability
blurb: This paper introduces BlowLive, a multi-factor biometric framework that fuses blow-acoustic signals with facial recognition and derives stable cryptographic keys from noisy inputs via a fuzzy extractor, hitting 99.56% accuracy on blow-acoustics and 100% on fusion across 50 participants. A Doppler shift-based liveness check defends against playback, synthetic, and deepfake spoofing at 99.42% accuracy, while the key-derivation scheme adds template protection and revocability that raw-template biometrics lack.

title: Leaking Circuit Secrets: Gradient Leakage Attacks on Graph Neural Networks
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of gradient leakage attacks against graph neural networks used in circuit design and hardware-security tasks. Testing GraphSAGE, GCN, GIN, and GAT on ISCAS'85, EPFL, and TrustHub netlists, the authors show GLAs can recover gate types and hardware-Trojan properties, aiding adversaries in defeating logic locking or evading Trojan detection. Attention-based GAT leaks the most while injective GIN resists best, and standard defenses like differential privacy and gradient clipping only help in narrow settings and degrade accuracy.

title: DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison
blurb: Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old Minnesota man who operated the "Snoopy" shop selling access to hacked DraftKings accounts, was sentenced to 18 months in prison after pleading guilty to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion. The 2022 credential-stuffing attack compromised roughly 68,000 accounts and stole $600,000; Austad was ordered to pay $463,684 in forfeiture and $1.3M in restitution.

title: WhatsApp will warn users before they message a potential scammer
blurb: WhatsApp is rolling out an Android and iOS warning screen that flags chats with unknown numbers before users open them.

title: Privacy Vulnerabilities of Attention Layers in Tabular Foundation Models and Protection of High-Risk Queries
blurb: This paper shows that tabular foundation models leak membership data through their attention layers, introducing AMIA, a shadow-model-free membership inference attack that exploits concentrated attention patterns to beat confidence-based attacks by an average of 7.7% in low false-positive regimes. The authors propose a k-anonymity-inspired inference-time defense targeting high-risk queries, cutting AMIA leakage by 50% with 3.9% utility loss.

title: General Techniques for Reducing Key-Switching Overhead in Privacy-Preserving Two-Party Transformer Inference
blurb: This paper introduces packing-independent techniques to cut key-switching overhead, the dominant cost in secure two-party Transformer inference over FHE. The authors decompose attention into precomputable and online phases to shift key switches offline, add storage-communication trade-offs to bound the resulting ciphertext storage, and fuse relinearization with rotation to collapse the multiply-then-rotate pattern common in RNS-CKKS matmuls. The methods drop online key-switch cost without changing existing packing strategies.

title: Shoot the Honey, Cloak the Player: Towards Zero-Runtime-Overhead Proactive Defense and Detection for Visual Game Cheating
blurb: AimTrap defends FPS games against visual aimbots with adversarial camouflage and honeypot textures, hitting 96.9% lure success at negligible overhead.

title: Why patch directives only go so far
blurb:
CISA's June 21 emergency directive for CVE-2026-50751, a CVSS 9.3 authentication bypass in Check Point Remote Access VPN, landed six weeks after a Qilin affiliate began exploiting it in early May to compromise dozens of organizations. The IKEv1 certificate-validation flaw turns the VPN gateway itself into the attack vector, so patching does nothing for networks already breached, where the attacker now operates as a trusted user. Hunt the May-June window, don't just patch.

(78 words)

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant disclosed active zero-day exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, a privilege-escalation flaw in the file-upload feature that let an attacker turn a compromised admin account into root access via a malicious CSV upload. Enterprises running Catalyst SD-WAN, common across banks, retail, and healthcare branch networks, should prioritize patching and audit for the anti-forensic cleanup Mandiant observed, since attackers reverted configs to erase indicators.

title: CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added three max-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws and a critical Lantronix EDS5000 command-injection bug to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, giving federal agencies three days under directive BOD 26-04 to patch or mitigate. Bishop Fox showed the UniFi flaws can be chained for unauthenticated remote code execution with elevated privileges, so IT teams running these devices should apply the May updates and run the vendor's detection script immediately.

title: Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
blurb: Microsoft, Europol, and international partners disrupted the Amadey and StealC malware-as-a-service operations under Operation Endgame, seizing 326 servers and 142 domains and recovering roughly 27 million stolen credentials from over 385k compromised systems. The action degrades the initial-access and credential-theft infrastructure that feeds ransomware and fraud campaigns, with Microsoft, ESET, Proofpoint, and IBM X-Force among the private-sector partners supplying intelligence.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom inference chip co-designed to run large language models more efficiently than general-purpose GPUs. The partnership gives OpenAI a path to reduce its dependence on Nvidia for serving capacity, and signals to enterprise buyers that the largest model providers are moving inference onto bespoke silicon to control cost and supply at scale.

title: macOS Weaknesses Chained to Silently Disable Endpoint Security Agents
blurb: XM Cyber demonstrated a macOS attack chain that lets a standard, non-admin user silently unload enterprise endpoint agents, fully disabling CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor and permanently deactivating Kandji MDM without kernel exploits or alerts. The technique abuses legitimate XPC and code-signing trust-cache behavior rather than a software bug, so IT teams relying on macOS EDR and MDM should confirm their vendors have shipped the detections and patches, including Kandji's fix for CVE-2026-39118.

title: Critical Ubiquiti Vulnerabilities in Attackers’ Crosshairs
blurb: CISA warned that threat actors are actively exploiting three critical UniFi OS vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-34908, -34909, and -34910, all rated CVSS 10/10), which chain an NGINX authentication-gateway bypass into unauthenticated command injection on Ubiquiti devices. Ubiquiti patched the flaws in UniFi OS Server 5.0.8 on May 21 without disclosing in-the-wild abuse, but users reported zero-day exploitation that created rogue 'John Sim' administrator accounts on centrally networked infrastructure.

title: In a first, a court takedown goes after two cybercrime tools at once
blurb: Microsoft, working with Europol and partners including ESET, BitSight, IBM X-Force, and Proofpoint, executed a court-authorized takedown that simultaneously disrupted the Amadey botnet and the StealC infostealer, knocking out more than 200 command-and-control servers tied to over 140,000 infected machines. It was the first operation to target two cybercrime tools at once, with the legal team using insights from Copilot to treat both malware families as a single RICO conspiracy.

title: Boffin claims Microsoft's supposed quantum leap does not compute due to 'basic Python errors'
blurb: Nature published a peer-reviewed critique by University of St Andrews physicist Henry Legg arguing that Microsoft's 2025 Majorana quantum-computing results rest on flawed tune-up software and coding errors that produced incorrect statements to peer reviewers. Legg contends the raw data points to disordered devices incompatible with a topological gap, casting doubt on the Majorana roadmap Microsoft has staked years of research and its Majorana 2 chip announcement on.

title: Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching
blurb: Mandiant disclosed that attackers exploited a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw, CVE-2026-20245, as a zero-day for months before Cisco's early-June patch, gaining root on a service provider's orchestration infrastructure via an authenticated CLI command-injection bug. It is the seventh Cisco SD-WAN exploitation case surfaced in 2026, reinforcing that the controllers managing software-defined networks are now a primary target for attackers bypassing the perimeter.

title: Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke
blurb: Symantec linked a newly discovered, file-less backdoor called Mistic to KongTuke/Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells corporate network access to ransomware groups including Qilin, Akira, and Black Basta. Active since April against insurance, education, IT, and professional-services firms, Mistic side-loads through legitimate executables, runs payloads in memory, and includes a self-delete kill switch built for long-term, low-visibility persistence.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
blurb: Attackers are now actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME, three weeks after Cisco patched it on June 3 and warned it could grant unauthenticated remote attackers root access. The bug carries a CVSS score of 8.6, requires the non-default WebDialer service to be enabled, and has no workaround, leaving patching the only fix for enterprise voice and conferencing deployments.

title: Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware
blurb: Microsoft, Europol, ESET, IBM X-Force, and Proofpoint disrupted the shared command-and-control infrastructure behind the Amadey loader and StealC infostealer under Operation Endgame, seizing 25M stolen credentials from 385,000 systems and flagging $47M in crypto. AI-driven analysis exposed the families' common C&C, letting the coalition target the full cybercrime supply chain rather than individual threats across hundreds of domains and servers.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers at XM Cyber disclosed a macOS privilege-escalation technique that lets a standard user disable enterprise security tools like CrowdStrike Falcon EDR and Kandji MDM without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or triggering alerts, by abusing how macOS caches and reuses an application's CDHash to validate trusted XPC services. Any macOS app that exposes privileged XPC services running as root is potentially exposed, and Apple has not yet responded.

title: Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google released Chrome 149, patching 18 vulnerabilities, including four critical and 14 high-severity flaws, more than half of them use-after-free memory bugs that can enable remote code execution and sandbox escape. Google found 17 of the 18 itself, a streak likely driven by AI-assisted discovery, and reports no evidence that any of the flaws have been exploited in the wild.

title: White House app auto-downloads to government phones, can't be uninstalled
blurb: The White House automatically pushed its official app onto the work phones of millions of federal employees, and IT-managed device controls prevent workers from uninstalling it. Staff at the USDA, State Department, and Department of Labor report the app reappears after deletion and surfaces partisan news feeds alongside a "text President Trump" button, while its App Store listing provides no specific privacy or data policy.

title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
blurb: Mandiant found an unknown threat actor exploited a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day, CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS 7.8), for at least two months before disclosure, chaining a malicious CSV upload to escalate a compromised admin account to root on a communications service provider's device. The intrusion paired the flaw with earlier authentication-bypass zero-days and anti-forensic file restoration, so enterprises running Catalyst SD-WAN controllers should patch and audit peering connections and admin credential changes immediately.

title: HPE’s Slingshot tops supercomputer interconnects, but China steals the show
blurb: HPE's Slingshot fabric overtook 100G Ethernet as the most common interconnect on the TOP500 for the first time, appearing in 57 systems and four of the top five, including Frontier and Aurora. The headline shift is at the top, where a mysterious Chinese system running an unknown fabric displaced HPE's El Capitan, signaling that domestic interconnect development is advancing despite US export controls on high-end compute.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout expands biometric surveillance into one of the city's busiest commercial districts, raising compliance and data-governance questions for enterprises operating in the area amid an unsettled UK regulatory framework.

title: 양자컴퓨터 시대 대비 나선 미국…PQC 의무화와 양자 기술 육성 병행
blurb: The White House signed two executive orders accelerating federal post-quantum cryptography adoption, setting deadlines of December 2030 for key-establishment migration and December 2031 for digital signatures, plus mandatory cryptographic asset inventories and new procurement requirements for contractors. Government suppliers and critical-infrastructure operators will need to prove PQC readiness to keep federal business, citing the risk that adversaries are already harvesting encrypted data to decrypt once quantum hardware matures.

title: Making Windows a developer platform, again
blurb: Microsoft used Build 2026 to reposition Windows as a first-class developer platform, headlined by Coreutils for Windows, a Microsoft-maintained fork of the Rust-based uutils package that ships standard Unix commands as a single installable binary. The tooling narrows the context-switching gap between WSL, Linux VMs, and native PowerShell or cmd, letting Unix scripts port to Windows, though it remains a preview with incomplete coverage and conflicts against existing shell commands.

title: Qualcomm shares jump 14% on Modular acquisition, guidance upgrade
blurb: Qualcomm shares jumped 14% in after-hours trading after it agreed to acquire inference-software startup Modular for $3.92 billion in stock, previewed two data center chips, and raised its fiscal 2029 non-handset revenue target to $40 billion. Modular's platform automates porting AI models across chips, which Qualcomm can use to lower the switching cost off Nvidia accelerators and drive adoption of its new Dragonfly C1000 CPU and AI300 accelerator.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC reportedly told customers to brace for 5–10% price increases across all advanced nodes, extending beyond 3nm and 2nm to 5nm and 7nm and covering 74% of its wafer revenue. Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek now face higher silicon costs that will flow downstream into device and datacenter hardware pricing for enterprise buyers.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across a channel, monitoring activity, posting alerts and comments under preset rules, and fixing code issues on command. The move pushes Claude from a sidebar assistant toward a standing presence in enterprise messaging, positioning Anthropic against Slack's own AI and Microsoft Copilot for the workplace-agent layer IT teams will have to govern.

title: Retirement of Azure DevOps issuer in Workload identity federation service connections
blurb: Microsoft is deprecating the Azure DevOps issuer in workload identity federation service connections, with retirement set for July 1, 2027, as it standardizes on the Microsoft Entra issuer across Azure. Teams running secretless Azure Pipelines on single-tenant Entra apps or managed identities in the public cloud must migrate their federated credentials off the vstoken.dev.azure.com prefix, though multi-tenant apps and non-public clouds are excluded for now.

title: UK school’s network left wide open for invasion, student found
blurb: A student discovered that a UK school's network was left wide open to attack, with security gaps that would let an intruder move freely across its systems. The case highlights how thin IT staffing and unsegmented networks leave the education sector exposed, and why basic access controls and network segmentation remain unfinished business for under-resourced public-sector buyers.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS added high-resolution 20-second metrics for service auto scaling, cutting scale-out trigger time from 363 seconds to 86 seconds and total provisioning time from 386 seconds to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Available across Fargate, ECS Managed Instances, and EC2, the faster scaling lets teams cut baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding they ran to absorb spikes, lowering compute costs.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: The Register argues that AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the developers who run them, as token-metered pricing and agent-orchestrated workflows push consumption far beyond flat per-seat SaaS norms. The shift turns coding tools into a variable, usage-billed line item that IT leaders must budget and cap like cloud compute rather than a predictable per-developer license.

title: Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon says the company is designing data center chips specifically for Chinese customers that are in compliance with US export controls (Yifan Yu/Nikkei Asia)
blurb: Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon said the company is designing data center AI chips specifically for Chinese customers that comply with US export controls, part of its broader push into the AI processor market it detailed this week. The move positions Qualcomm to capture China demand that Nvidia and AMD have ceded under tightening restrictions, giving enterprise buyers in the region a sanctioned alternative for AI infrastructure.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
blurb: PayPal deployed a Salesforce Agentforce AI SDR agent into its 200-rep org 14 weeks ago, putting it on the roughly 8,000 monthly merchant leads no human had capacity to chase, and meeting conversion is running about 50% higher than reps working alone. The agent runs a 10-nudge cadence to book qualified meetings and hand reps fully contextualized leads, positioning Agentforce as a funnel-feeder rather than a headcount cut.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: Soaring memory chip demand helps Micron quadruple its revenue and crush expectations again
blurb: Micron reported Q3 revenue of $41.46B, up from $9.3B a year ago, and gross margin of 84.9%, now the highest of any major US tech company ahead of Meta and Nvidia. AI data center demand has turned memory from a commodity into a pricing-power business, and the resulting supply shortages are pushing up costs for PCs, smartphones, and enterprise hardware that CEO Sanjay Mehrotra expects to persist into 2028.

title: Nation-state actors cracked critical Australian infrastructure to ‘cripple it at a time of their choosing’
blurb: Australian authorities warned that nation-state actors have pre-positioned inside critical infrastructure networks, holding access intended to cripple essential services "at a time of their choosing" rather than for immediate disruption. The disclosure raises the bar for enterprise operators in energy, water, and telecom, who now face board-level pressure to fund threat-hunting for dormant intrusions and segment OT systems that legacy procurement left exposed.

title: Microsoft rivals line up to tell UK watchdog where the software behemoth hurt them
blurb: Microsoft's rivals are submitting evidence to the UK Competition and Markets Authority detailing how the company's licensing and bundling practices have disadvantaged them, escalating the regulator's ongoing probe into Microsoft's cloud and software dominance. For enterprise buyers, any remedies forced on Microsoft could reshape pricing on Azure, Windows Server, and Office licensing terms that currently lock procurement decisions into a single vendor.

title: The hits keep on coming for Cisco vulnerabilities
blurb: Cisco is fielding active exploitation of two more flaws, including CVE-2026-20230, a Unified Communications Manager SSRF bug now used to gain root, and CVE-2026-20245, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day exploited against a communications provider. It marks Cisco's sixth SD-WAN vulnerability under attack this year and the second zero-day in two months, pushing IT teams toward emergency patch cycles across core network infrastructure.

title: Nvidia and Samsung to power 6G-ready digital twin for Japan telecom giant
blurb: KDDI launched a joint initiative with Nvidia, Samsung, and Keysight to build a high-fidelity RAN digital twin for training and validating AI-driven network optimization ahead of 6G. Nvidia supplies its Aerial Omniverse Digital Twin and accelerated compute, Samsung contributes vRAN technology, and Keysight provides equipment emulation, with a prototype targeted by March 2028 and platform expansion by 2030.

title: Companies are not looking before they're leaping into the AI playpen
blurb: 93% of organizations report infrastructure incidents tied to AI deployments, according to a survey arguing that enterprises are pushing models into production faster than their ops and governance can absorb. The implication for IT leaders is that the bottleneck has shifted from model access to operational readiness, and the cost of skipping capacity planning, observability, and rollback discipline now shows up as outages rather than line items.

title: NIST Opens Updated IoT Security Guidance to Public Review
blurb: NIST released the first public draft of SP 800-213 Revision 1, updating its IoT cybersecurity guidance for federal systems and reframing the focus from individual devices to whole IoT products, with comments open through August 24. The shift requires IT teams to treat IoT products as system elements in risk assessments, tightening procurement and compliance expectations for vendors selling connected hardware into government environments.

title: Qualcomm claims it's not too late for Dragonfly to land in datacenters
blurb: Qualcomm argued it is not too late to enter the datacenter market with Dragonfly, its Arm-based server chip line, despite Nvidia, AMD, and hyperscaler in-house silicon already dominating AI infrastructure buildouts. The pitch targets enterprise buyers weighing power and cost alternatives for inference workloads, though Qualcomm faces an entrenched software ecosystem and supply commitments that competitors locked in years ahead of its arrival.

title: Elastic lays off 7% of employees
blurb: Elastic cut roughly 7% of its workforce as CEO Ash Kulkarni reorganized engineering into three core areas with fewer management layers, citing AI and automation that let some teams run leaner. Kulkarni framed the cuts as a structural simplification rather than a retreat, expanding customer-facing sales and projecting total headcount to still grow year-over-year this fiscal year.

title: Building a state-of-the-art development platform with Backstage
blurb: The author argues that Backstage solved the developer-portal problem but not the platform problem, since it organizes catalogs, docs, and templates while assuming an execution layer for deployments, environments, and policies already exists beneath it. Wiring Backstage directly to CI/CD, GitOps, Kubernetes, and observability creates a fragile "messy middle." The fix is a control plane between portal and runtime that compiles developer and platform abstractions into infrastructure, enforces policies, and reconciles drift.

title: UK Finance and banks collaborate on digital verification service
blurb: UK Finance is backing Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, Nationwide, NatWest and Santander to build a bank-led digital verification service that lets customers confirm details like name, age or address through their banking app, sharing only consented data with third parties. The project has finished synthetic-data proof-of-concept work with technical partner Select ID and moves to a live pilot in the coming months.

title: Micron locks in historically high memory prices for five years
blurb: Micron signed multiyear supply agreements that lock in today's historically high memory prices through 2031, guaranteeing the chipmaker outsized margins and profits as buyers move to secure DRAM and NAND capacity. The deals shift pricing risk onto enterprise hardware purchasers, raising the floor on server, storage, and PC component costs for IT budgets across the next five procurement cycles.

title: AI coding token costs are on track to rival human payroll
blurb: Gartner projects that enterprise AI coding token costs will match or exceed a software engineer's monthly salary within two years, driven by agentic tool adoption and a shift from flat per-seat SaaS to consumption-based licensing. The bigger problem is governance: vendors lack mature cost-optimization controls, billing is opaque, and IT leaders have little visibility into per-developer spend, with anecdotes of single developers burning $20K in a month. Without FinOps-style oversight, budgets get wiped out before ROI can be justified.

title: Commvault deepens Microsoft tie-up with native Azure resilience service
blurb: Commvault signed a multiyear partnership with Microsoft to offer its data security and cyber recovery tools as a native Azure service, provisionable directly from the platform with no separate infrastructure or manual integration. Azure customers can buy Commvault Cloud through the Microsoft marketplace and count the spend toward their Azure Consumption Commitment, which removes a procurement step and folds recovery tooling into existing prepaid commitments.

title: NetApp federates performance-boosted StorageGRIDs into single namespace for greater scale
blurb: NetApp's StorageGRID 12.1 federates object stores into a single namespace scaling to 10EB, with up to 400% higher throughput for AI workloads.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cybersecurity breach in which an attacker claimed to have stolen trade secrets tied to its work for Apple and Tesla, with the threat actor publishing samples to substantiate the claim. The incident raises supply-chain exposure questions for OEMs that route component manufacturing through Tata, and pressures enterprise buyers to scrutinize the security posture of contract manufacturers holding sensitive design data.

title: JetBrains Marketplace Ecosystem Security Update: Addressing Malicious Third-Party AI Plugins
blurb: JetBrains removed 15 malicious third-party plugins from its Marketplace, banned 7 publisher accounts, and triggered a backend kill-switch that disables the extensions in users' IDEs on next relaunch. The plugins posed as AI text-generation and unit-testing utilities while harvesting developer-configured AI provider API keys, which JetBrains now says any affected user should treat as exposed and revoke immediately.

title: Qualcomm Targets Over $15 Billion in Data Center Revenue, Names Meta CPU Customer
blurb: Qualcomm told investors it expects more than $15B in data center revenue by fiscal 2029 and named Meta as the first public customer for its Dragonfly C1000 server CPUs, with systems entering production in the second half of 2028. The target nearly doubles Qualcomm's prior non-handset chip goal to $40B and sent shares up more than 12% after hours, signaling a credible challenge to incumbent server-CPU suppliers in hyperscaler procurement.

title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
blurb: Google is splitting its Web & App Activity setting into separate Search Services History and Personalized Recommendations controls across Search, Maps, Shopping, Flights, Hotels, Translate, News, and Google Play, rolling out to accounts over the next few days. The new Search Services History defaults on for users with Web & App Activity enabled and now saves media like images, audio, and video from Lens and other interactive searches, which IT teams managing Google Workspace and consumer accounts should review.

title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration where untrusted pull requests trigger privileged workflows, and its scan of 30,000 high-impact repos found 300-plus fully exploitable by any unauthenticated user with a free account. Affected projects include Microsoft's Azure Sentinel, Google's adk-samples, Apache Doris, and Cloudflare's Workers SDK, where forged approvals or crafted branch names let attackers run code on CI runners and steal long-lived credentials.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS service auto scaling now supports high-resolution 20-second metrics, cutting the time to trigger scale-out from 363 to 86 seconds and total scale-and-provision time from 386 to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Faster reaction lets teams trim baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding that hedges against traffic spikes, lowering compute cost while target tracking replaces the custom step-scaling policies that used to deliver aggressive scaling.

title: Minimus container images are now free
blurb: Minimus opened its catalog of hardened, minimal container images to a free tier, with curated Helm charts for deployment. The free images ship without support, SLA, or guaranteed patching timelines, and paid subscriptions may receive security updates first, so teams should weigh the smaller attack surface against slower CVE response before pulling them into production.

title: We’re making Bunny DNS free
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated DNS query fees entirely, making Bunny DNS free with no query limits or per-request billing and free hosting for up to 500 domains per account. The change removes the unpredictable-bill risk on a service that already handles nearly 200 billion queries a month across 300,000 domains, using latency data, health checks, and JavaScript for dynamic routing.

title: Cloudflare launched self-managed OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all developers on its platform, letting them create and manage their own OAuth clients for scoped, delegated access to the Cloudflare API instead of relying on API tokens. The change targets SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and agentic tools, and ships with clearer consent screens, dashboard-based revocation, and app-ownership visibility to blunt OAuth phishing.

title: HCP Vault Dedicated introduces cluster disaster recovery (public preview)
blurb: HashiCorp added cluster disaster recovery to HCP Vault Dedicated in public preview, letting teams run DR drills at the cluster level to simulate full cluster failures and verify failover readiness. The feature targets a gap in production Vault operations, where secrets-management outages have broad blast radius and recovery procedures often go untested until a real incident forces the issue.

title: Companies are not looking before they're leaping into the AI playpen
blurb: A survey found that 93% of organizations have logged infrastructure incidents attributable to AI workloads, as teams push models into production faster than they can harden the platforms underneath them. The gap shows up as unplanned capacity spikes, misconfigured access, and reliability regressions that land on the SRE and platform teams left to absorb the blast radius.

title: Building a state-of-the-art development platform with Backstage
blurb: Backstage solved the developer-portal problem but not the platform problem, the author argues: a portal organizes catalogs and templates, while a platform owns deployments, environments, policies, and runtime operations. Wiring Backstage point-to-point into CI/CD, GitOps, Kubernetes, and observability produces a fragile "messy middle." The post's fix is a control plane between portal and runtime that compiles developer and platform-engineer abstractions into Kubernetes resources, enforces policies, and reconciles drift.

title: The Dependency Black Hole
blurb: This paper introduces the "dependency black hole" theory from an industrial case study of 267 microservices, where compile-time, run-time, and task dependencies all concentrate around a dense core of "dependency magnet" services. The authors map the network to a black hole's anatomy: a singularity core, an event horizon of services being pulled in, and an outer accretion disk of lightly coupled services. The practical payoff is a lens for spotting services at risk of becoming magnets and justifying governance interventions before the core's blast radius grows.

title: What you need to know about Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, giving teams Firecracker-grade isolation with no shared kernel, sub-second snapshot boots, and stateful sessions that persist memory and disk for up to eight hours. The author argues it finally fits the AI-code-sandbox problem that EC2, ECS, and Lambda functions each solved only partially. The model is per-session VMs with dedicated HTTPS endpoints, bearer-token auth, and automatic suspend on idle so you stop paying when traffic stops.

title: How Cloudflare responded to the “Copy Fail” Linux vulnerability
blurb: Cloudflare detailed its response to "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation in the AF_ALG crypto path disclosed on April 29, 2026. Because the company runs a custom LTS-based kernel that ships weekly internal builds and reboots its edge on a four-week ERR cycle, the fix was already deployed before public disclosure, and behavioral detections flagged the exploit pattern within minutes. The post argues that disciplined patch cadence, not incident heroics, is what neutralizes kernel CVEs at scale.

title: Paris-based Tsuga, whose observability software runs in customers' own clouds to help them skip per-byte ingestion costs, raised a $35M Series A led by Singular (Ana Maria Constantin/The Next Web)
