blurb: Tsuga, a Paris-based observability startup, raised a $35M Series A led by Singular to expand software that runs inside customers' own clouds and avoids per-byte ingestion costs. The in-cloud model targets the spend problem behind Datadog-style bills, where high-cardinality telemetry from AI agents pushes monitoring costs up faster than the infrastructure it watches.

title: Rethinking cloud operations with agentic observability
blurb: Microsoft announced general availability of the Azure Copilot Observability Agent, built on Azure Monitor to correlate logs, metrics, traces, and topology across agents, applications, and infrastructure for faster root-cause analysis. The launch targets a complexity problem Microsoft's own survey quantifies: 84% of organizations report rising cloud complexity, and 69% say it is outpacing their current operating model.

title: The uptime questions every engineering leader should ask this week
blurb: In an interview with Help Net Security, Oh Dear CTO Mattias Geniar argues that most outages start quietly as creeping latency or a slowly climbing error rate, and that teams page on the wrong signals: absolute numbers and isolated endpoints instead of deltas from baseline and real user outcomes. He recommends alerting on change rather than thresholds, and running end-to-end scenario checks like a simulated login or checkout. The piece closes with questions leaders can use to pressure-test their uptime story.

title: Sources: Meta is accelerating plans to use LLMs to review content and ads, replacing ~50% of human review requests in 2026 and aiming for 90% by year-end (Financial Times)
blurb: Meta is accelerating plans to use LLMs to review content and ads, with sources saying automated systems will handle roughly 50% of human review requests in 2026 and a targeted 90% by year-end. The shift moves a large share of policy enforcement and ad approvals to automated judgment, raising the stakes for advertisers on appeals, false flags, and creative that gets caught in machine review.

title: B2B brands rank in Google but appear in just 3% of AI Overviews
blurb: Most enterprise B2B brands rank organically for roughly 9,700 keywords, but the median company appears in just 3% of relevant AI Overviews, according to Walker Sands' benchmark of 828 companies and 45M queries. AI Overviews now surface in 50% of searches where these brands rank, so strong keyword coverage no longer guarantees presence in the answers buyers actually read.

title: Google Ads API to stop supporting new Smart Campaign creation
blurb: Google will stop supporting new Smart Campaign creation through its Ads API on August 3rd, pushing advertisers toward Performance Max and other automated campaign types while existing Smart Campaigns keep serving. Platform partners and agencies that build campaigns programmatically need to update their workflows before the deadline, since any API request setting the channel type to SMART_CAMPAIGN will fail afterward.

title: A 13-word edit can steer what deep-research AI agents recommend
blurb: Cornell Tech researchers showed that deep-research AI agents can be steered by edits as short as 13 words to public pages like Reddit threads and Wikipedia, with injected fake entities appearing in 38% to 51% of generated reports and up to 62% when multiple pages are poisoned. Reddit was the biggest exposure, supplying 54% to 71% of the user-generated URLs these agents retrieved, which puts brand mentions in AI answers within reach of cheap manipulation.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
blurb: PayPal deployed an Agentforce AI SDR agent against the roughly 8,000 monthly leads its 200-rep org was never going to work by hand, and meeting conversion is running about 50% higher than reps achieved alone. The agent runs a 10-nudge cadence on every stalled merchant, then hands a qualified, fully contexted lead to a human to close. Salesforce's advice for teams stuck on messy CRM data: don't fix everything first. Stand up a web agent on your site, FAQs, and product data while you consolidate the rest.

title: OpenAI says ChatGPT ad dismissals have dropped 50% as relevance improves
blurb: OpenAI says ChatGPT users are dismissing ads 50% less often since it launched its advertising business in February, a decline CRO Denise Dresser frames as proof its conversational ad format is getting more relevant. The drop signals a new high-intent channel where ads ride alongside user tasks rather than interrupting them, giving brands a reason to test placements before CPMs harden.

title: ChatGPT recommendations drive more brand website visits: Study
blurb: Users who got a brand recommended by ChatGPT were 2.5 times more likely to visit that brand's site within a week than a direct competitor's, according to Similarweb's study of U.S. desktop behavior across finance, travel, and beauty. AI-influenced visitors also engaged harder, viewing 12 pages and spending 11.8 minutes on site versus 6.5 pages and 5.6 minutes for everyone else.

title: How to measure paid social’s impact on paid search performance
blurb: Branded search volume is the clearest signal that paid social is driving demand that paid search ultimately captures and gets last-click credit for. Track brand-name, brand-plus-category, and brand-plus-reviews queries in Google Ads and Microsoft Advertising before and after a Meta or TikTok launch, and watch for lifts that coincide with social spend. Treating the two channels as separate ROAS lines understates social's contribution and overstates search, which can push budget toward the channel that only harvested the demand.

title: Actress Cate Blanchett launches a free registry tool to keep AI from using your likeness
blurb: Cate Blanchett launched the Human Consent Registry at the European Parliament, a free RSL Media tool that lets anyone permit, restrict, or prohibit AI use of their name, face, voice, and likeness. For marketers building AI-generated campaigns, the registry signals a coming consent layer around identity, raising the prospect that synthetic-likeness and voice assets will require documented opt-in before they can run.

title: Avenue Z’s Jonathan Snow: TikTok Shop’s Best Customers Never Show Up in Its P&L
blurb: TikTok Shop's recorded GMV undercounts its real value, argues Avenue Z co-founder Jonathan Snow, because most demand a creator generates converts later on Amazon, a brand's DTC site, or in-store rather than in the app. Snow frames TikTok's roughly 2M commission-only affiliates as an "upper funnel that never existed before," seeding products for free and driving halo sales Sephora has reported in triple digits. The tactical takeaway: judge TikTok Shop on blended channel lift and incrementality, not its standalone P&L.

title: Google tests “Strongest match” labels on Search ads
blurb: Google began testing "Strongest match" and "Strong match" labels on a small share of US Search ads, surfacing its existing ad-quality and relevance scores directly in the results rather than adding a new ranking factor. If the test expands, advertisers with strong relevance signals could win higher CTR while weaker ads get easier to skip, making Quality Score work visible to users for the first time.

title: Tailor Your Feed: The Google Discover fan-out that surfaces niche sites
blurb: Google's "Tailor Your Feed," now testing in US Search Labs, lets users type natural-language prompts that reshape their Discover feed into SEE_MORE/SEE_LESS actions, with a query-intent fan-out that pulls in niche sites and small creators that had barely circulated before. The feature opens a third visibility path beyond implicit affinity and the Follow button, shifting selection power to users and giving small publishers a way into Discover.

title: Ikea turns everyday objects into ingenious World Cup guessing game
blurb: Ikea Canada and Dentsu Creative launched "Assemble the World," a social-first campaign that rebuilds the flags of all 18 nations in the 2026 World Cup using everyday Ikea products and invites followers to guess the items. The play extends to OOH placements near stores through the summer, turning a trend-jacking moment into an interactive shopping prompt without abandoning the brand's flat-pack aesthetic.

title: Meta to Unify Creator Marketplace and Partnership Ads Hub, Adds Facebook Creators to Roster
blurb: Meta will merge Creator Marketplace and its Partnership Ads tools into a single Meta Creator Marketing Hub later this year, and is adding Facebook creators alongside the 5M-plus Instagram creators already discoverable in the marketplace. The hub lets brands surface product-tagged and user-generated posts, run pre-permitted content as Partnership Ads, and pull paid performance insights, with Meta citing 19% lower CPA and 13% higher CTR for Partnership Ads over standard campaigns.

title: Hightouch : pourquoi cette startup a fait une offre à 1,2 milliard de dollars à Publicis
blurb: Hightouch closed a $150M Series D from Goldman Sachs and Bain Capital Ventures at a $2.75B valuation, building a composable CDP that activates customer data sitting in Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks without the legacy baggage of Adobe or Salesforce. The company now claims $100M in ARR and over 1,000 customers including Spotify and Warner Music, and is pushing agentic AI that builds audiences and generates content variants across 300-plus channels.

title: How Large Language Models Source Brand Reputation Across Languages and Markets
blurb: A study of 167,551 URL-grounded citations across 128 brands, 12 markets, and 13 languages found that LLMs ground 85.7% of brand answers in third-party sources the brand doesn't own, with just 14.3% from owned domains. Sources are concentrated: 80% of citations come from about 18% of domains, and Wikipedia is the most-cited domain in 11 of 12 languages. The mix shifts by market, with YouTube leading for Polish national brands, so AI brand visibility depends on earning third-party and Wikipedia presence, not publishing more owned content.

title: Less Than a Quarter of Americans Use AI to Create or Edit Images
blurb: Fewer than one in four Americans (24%) use AI to create or edit images or videos, even as half of US adults now use AI chatbots and 44% specifically use ChatGPT, per Pew's February survey of 5,119 adults. The gap signals that AI image generation remains a niche behavior, so marketers leaning on AI visuals are still ahead of mainstream consumer adoption rather than chasing it.

title: Meta outlines best practices for AI-generated multimedia ads
blurb: Meta published best-practice guidance for its AI-powered ad creation tools, which generate multiple image, video, and text variations so advertisers can test combinations and surface the highest-performing creative. The pitch is volume-led optimization: let the system produce variants across formats, then let delivery data pick winners rather than hand-building each asset. For performance marketers, the practical move is feeding the tools strong source creative and clear brand constraints, since AI variation amplifies whatever input it starts from.

title: LinkedIn tests suggested feeds
blurb: LinkedIn is testing suggested feeds that surface posts from outside a user's network, echoing a similar alternative-feed experiment it ran last year. The change broadens exposure beyond first-degree connections, giving B2B marketers a potential path to organic reach without paying for it, while raising the risk that follower counts matter less as the algorithm decides who sees what.

title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
blurb: Google is splitting Web & App Activity into two new controls, Search Services History and Personalized Recommendations, giving users separate toggles for saved activity and personalized results across Search, Maps, Shopping, Flights, Hotels, Translate, and News. The change rolls out to Google Accounts over the next few days, but users with Web & App Activity already on will have the new media-saving option enabled by default.

title: LinkedIn automates job application process for premium users
blurb: LinkedIn is rolling out a Premium Apply Assistant that uses AI to pre-fill job applications and auto-generate tailored cover letters for suitable roles, available to its premium subscribers. The shift lowers friction on applications and will likely spike volume per posting, pushing recruiters and employer-brand marketers toward sharper screening signals and differentiated job content to cut through the AI-generated noise.

title: When LLM Rationales Become User-Facing: Effects on Trust Perception, Decision-Making, and Gaze Behaviors
blurb: Two studies tested whether LLM reasoning rationales actually help users calibrate trust or just persuade through fluent prose. Rationale correctness and certainty framing moved trust and decision confidence, while presentation format (instant, delayed, on-demand) did not, and incorrect rationales drew more attention and cognitive effort while lowering trust in the system. For PMs shipping reasoning-visible features, the takeaway is to make rationales selective, tied to evidence, and honest about certainty rather than defaulting to "show more reasoning.

title: Less Than a Quarter of Americans Use AI to Create or Edit Images
blurb: Pew's February survey of 5,119 U.S. adults found that fewer than a quarter use AI to create or edit images and videos, even as half now use chatbots at all and 38% of employed adults use them at work. The gap is a signal for PMs shipping generative-media features: text-based assistance has crossed into mainstream habit, while image and video generation remains a niche behavior most users have not yet adopted.

title: LinkedIn automates job application process for premium users
blurb: LinkedIn launched Premium Apply Assistant, an AI feature that pre-fills job applications and drafts cover letters for roles it deems a good fit for premium subscribers. The move pushes LinkedIn deeper into agentic workflows on both sides of its marketplace, raising the prospect of AI-written applications meeting AI-driven recruiter screening with little human input between them.

title: LLM-Based Discovery of Latent Requirements from Stakeholder Conversations: Preliminary Results from Industry
blurb: This paper introduces LENS, an LLM approach that reads stakeholder interview transcripts to surface not just explicitly stated requirements but the latent ones buried in how people describe their workflows and pain points. Across twelve industrial cybersecurity interviews, it hit an 84.4% F1 on explicit requirements, and domain experts judged 75% of the inferred latent requirements as offering real automation or time-saving value. For PMs running discovery, the signal is that the gap between what stakeholders say and what they actually need is becoming machine-tractable, with transcript excerpts linked back to each user story for traceability.

title: CoinEx processed $3.8 billion in Iran-linked funds, acting as crypto gateway: TRM Labs
blurb: TRM Labs reported that exchange CoinEx processed $3.8B in Iran-linked funds, operating as a primary crypto gateway for the sanctioned economy. The firm also flagged CoinEx interactions with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and sanctioned Russian entities, deepening compliance scrutiny of the exchange.

title: Ripple launches RLUSD stablecoin in Japan after regulatory approval
blurb: Ripple launched its RLUSD stablecoin in Japan after securing regulatory approval, making the dollar-pegged token available to both institutional and retail users through SBI VC Trade. The listing gives RLUSD one of the first compliant stablecoin entries into Japan's tightly regulated market, where local rules require yen-backed reserves and licensed intermediaries for fiat-pegged tokens.

title: The banking lobby is wrong about stablecoins and community banks
blurb: Eco CEO Ryne Saxe argues that the banking lobby's claim that stablecoins will drain deposits from community banks is unproven, and that Congress should not weaken the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act to protect them. He notes community banks hold roughly one-tenth of US banking assets but originate over a third of small business loans and nearly two-thirds of agricultural loans, surviving on relationships rather than settlement speed.

title: The Web4 Agent Economy: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of the Landscape, Challenges, and Opportunities
blurb: A large-scale empirical study of the emerging Web4 agent economy analyzed 99,448 multi-chain identity registrations and over 317M transaction logs across protocols like MCP, x402, and EIP-8004. The authors found autonomous agents already process millions of daily machine-to-machine payments on immature infrastructure, with payment interoperability the most persistent unresolved bottleneck.

title: Gold, silver and bitcoin tumble as debasement trade unwinds
blurb: Gold, silver, and bitcoin have all sold off sharply as the 2025 "debasement trade" unwinds, with gold down 28% from its $5,600 January peak, silver off more than 50%, and bitcoin below $62,000 after a 50% correction from its October high. The reversal tracks markets pricing in two 25 basis point Fed hikes by March 2027 under new Chair Kevin Warsh, though bitcoin has gained roughly 30% against gold and 55% against silver since February.

title: YZi Labs ends proxy war with BNB treasury company CEA Industries
blurb: YZi Labs, the Binance-linked firm formerly known as Binance Labs, ended its activist campaign at BNB treasury company CEA Industries through a cooperation agreement that installs YZi partner Alex Odagiu as interim president and adds Ella Zhang and Matthew Roszak to the board. CEA shares closed 8.35% higher at $2.27 on the news and rose nearly 20% more in Wednesday pre-market trading.

title: A Tattered Cloak of Invisibility: Measuring Anonymity Loss in Railgun on Ethereum
blurb: This paper measures real-world anonymity loss in Railgun, the Ethereum mixer, using five behavioral heuristics: timing patterns, address reuse, transaction-graph proximity, amount fingerprints, and knapsack-style amount matches. The authors uniquely link 17.65% of Railgun withdrawals to their deposits, and a knapsack solver produces a 3.42-bit median anonymity loss per withdrawal. The takeaway is that cryptographically sound privacy pools still leak heavily through user behavior, not protocol flaws.

title: Kalshi in talks to raise funds at $40 billion valuation: FT
blurb: Kalshi is in talks to raise capital at a $40B valuation, according to the FT, up from the $5B valuation the prediction market reported last October. The 8x markup in eight months tracks surging volumes on event-contract trading, which has drawn regulatory scrutiny and competition from Polymarket as the category moves toward mainstream financial markets.

title: Bitcoin’s $10 Billion Options Expiry Risks Deepening Selloff
blurb: Roughly $10B in Bitcoin options expire this week, threatening to deepen a selloff already driven by fading institutional demand and macroeconomic headwinds. The concentration of open interest near current strikes raises the risk of dealer hedging flows amplifying downside moves rather than cushioning them.

title: Can Trustless Agents Be Trusted? An Empirical Study of the ERC-8004 Decentralized AI Agent Ecosystem
blurb: An empirical study of ERC-8004, the permissionless trust layer for AI agent economies, audited Identity, Reputation, and Validation registries across Ethereum, BSC, and Base through May 2026. Most registrations are placeholders, with only 3% to 15% exposing a valid registration file with a live endpoint, and 59% to 91% of reviewers exhibit coordinated Sybil behavior. The authors conclude the Reputation Registry cannot function as a trust signal as deployed and propose concrete revisions.

title: Memory maker SK hynix files for $29B US IPO amid AI demand
blurb: SK hynix filed to list on the Nasdaq for up to $29.4 billion, the second-largest US IPO on record behind SpaceX, on the strength of its dominant HBM memory business. The world's largest HBM supplier posted 198% year-over-year revenue growth to $38 billion in Q1 at a 77% net margin, making the listing a direct bet on sustained AI-chip demand.

title: CoinEx processed $3.8 billion in Iran-linked funds, acting as crypto gateway: TRM Labs
blurb: Blockchain-intelligence firm TRM Labs reported that crypto exchange CoinEx processed $3.8B in Iran-linked funds, functioning as a gateway for transactions tied to sanctioned Russian entities and the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. The findings sharpen compliance and sanctions-screening pressure on exchanges, as OFAC and FinCEN continue to scrutinize venues that route flows for designated terrorist organizations and embargoed jurisdictions.

title: Ripple launches RLUSD stablecoin in Japan after regulatory approval
blurb: Ripple launched its RLUSD stablecoin in Japan after securing regulatory approval, distributing it through SBI VC Trade to both institutional and retail users. The move gives Ripple a foothold in one of the first major markets to formalize a stablecoin regulatory framework, positioning RLUSD against incumbent dollar-pegged tokens in the region.

title: Kalshi in talks to raise funds at $40 billion valuation: FT
blurb: Kalshi is in talks to raise funds at a $40B valuation, according to the FT, up from the $5B valuation it reported last October. The eightfold jump in eight months reflects surging volume on regulated prediction markets, positioning Kalshi as a fast-growing CFTC-regulated venue as event contracts move further into mainstream trading.

title: The Inference-Compute Frontier and a Latency-Efficient Architecture for Limit Order Book Prediction
blurb: This paper tests whether the scaling-law relationship between compute and predictive loss holds for limit order book prediction, and finds it does. Using the FI-2010 dataset, a power law fit to lower-compute models extrapolates across orders of magnitude to predict the high-compute frontier with R²=0.941, but the same relationship breaks down in latency space. The authors use that gap to motivate FastBiNLOB, a hardware-friendly mixer that beats published macro-F1 targets at lower latency than existing state-of-the-art architectures.

title: The banking lobby is wrong about stablecoins and community banks
blurb: Eco CEO Ryne Saxe argues the banking lobby's claim that stablecoins will drain community-bank deposits is unproven, and that Congress should not weaken the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, which cleared the Senate Banking Committee 15-9, to protect against it. He notes community banks hold roughly a tenth of US banking assets but originate over a third of small-business loans and nearly two-thirds of agricultural loans, surviving on relationships stablecoins do not replace.

title: SoFi Launches SOFIUSD Stablecoin on Solana
blurb: SoFi launched SOFIUSD, a dollar-backed stablecoin issued on Solana, extending its national bank charter into onchain settlement. The move lets SoFi route payments and stored value across a public blockchain rather than relying solely on ACH and card rails, and positions a chartered bank to compete directly with issuers like Circle on cross-border and programmable-money use cases.

title: PBOC Plans Overnight Reverse Repo in Next Stage of Policy Shift
blurb: The PBOC plans to add an overnight tenor to its open-market operations, a step toward making short-term rates the primary lever for steering borrowing costs. The move pushes China's central bank closer to a corridor-style framework like those at the Fed and ECB, where a benchmark overnight rate, rather than quantity-based liquidity injections, anchors money-market pricing.

title: UK Finance and banks collaborate on digital verification service
blurb: UK Finance is backing Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, Nationwide, NatWest and Santander to build a bank-led digital verification service that lets customers confirm details like name, age or address through their banking app, with verified data shared to third parties only on explicit consent. The project has cleared proof-of-concept work using synthetic data and moves to a live pilot in the coming months, with Select ID leading technical design and delivery.

title: Exclusive: British Business Bank backs 10 VC funds with £90m pot
blurb: The British Business Bank committed up to £90m to 10 first-time UK VC funds, its first deployment from the £500m package announced in July 2025 to back fund managers from underrepresented backgrounds. Each fund runs £10m to £20m and targets pre-seed and seed deeptech, defence, and climate startups, with the BBB anchoring roughly 50% of each and GPs that are 57% women and 43% from ethnic minority backgrounds.

title: Alan lève 480 millions d’euros et atteint 5,5 milliards d’euros de valorisation
blurb: Alan raised €480M at a €5.5B valuation, led by Prosus with Dara Holdings joining and existing backers Teachers' Venture Growth and Index Ventures participating, just three months after a €100M round. The French health-insurance scale-up reports €787M in ARR, up 54% year-over-year, and reached profitability in France in 2025 while still posting a €26M group loss as it funds acquisitions and international expansion.

title: Forget FAANG. Europe needs to bet on BRIOCHE
blurb: The author proposes BRIOCHE, a European answer to FAANG, grouping Bolt, Revolut, Iceye, Oura, Celonis, Helsing, and ElevenLabs into one continental tech narrative spanning mobility, defence, enterprise software, AI, and wearables. The argument is that Europe needs its own coherent story to stop measuring success through an American lens, with enough credible contenders now that even Mistral, Synthesia, and Spotify get left out.

title: Chinese Banks Rein in Retail Gold Trading on Volatility
blurb: Several major Chinese banks are suspending or restricting retail precious-metals trading services after gold and silver reversed a multiyear rally with sharp declines in recent months. The move limits retail exposure as volatility spikes, signaling that banks would rather curb speculative inflows than absorb the customer losses and operational risk that a swing in the metals market can bring.
